points.title: India is the seventh largest country in the world, with an area of approximately 3.3 million square kilometres.
points.text: 1. India is the seventh largest country in the world, with an area of approximately 3.3 million square kilometres. It is the largest democracy in the world and is reported in this report as the fourth largest economy, with a nominal GDP of around USD 4 trillion. India has emerged as the world’s most populous country, with an estimated population of 1.46 billion persons and an annual growth rate of 0.88%. The report records an urban-rural distribution of 37.8% urban and 62.92% rural population, with an average density of 433 persons per square kilometre. It also notes that India’s predicted median age in 2026 is 30.4 years, reflecting a predominantly young population.
points.subsection: A. Demographic and Economic Profile of the Country

points.title: India is culturally, religiously and linguistically diverse.
points.text: 2. India is culturally, religiously and linguistically diverse. According to Census 2011, the religious composition is 79.8% Hindu, 14.2% Muslim, 2.3% Christian, 1.7% Sikh, 0.7% Buddhist and 0.4% Jain. Zoroastrianism, Sanamahism and Judaism also have several thousand adherents. India also has significant linguistic diversity, with 122 languages and 234 mother tongues. Twenty-two languages are included in the Eighth Schedule to the Constitution of India, and Hindi is the mother tongue of approximately 44% of the population.
points.subsection: A. Demographic and Economic Profile of the Country

points.title: According to Census 2011, India recorded 26.8 million persons with disabilities, representing 2.21% of the total population.
points.text: 3. According to Census 2011, India recorded 26.8 million persons with disabilities, representing 2.21% of the total population. The National Family Health Survey-5 (2019-21) reported disability prevalence at 0.93%, and further indicated that 5.11% of households had one or more persons with disabilities. The difference between these figures points to possible under-reporting and methodological variation between data sources.
points.subsection: A. Demographic and Economic Profile of the Country

points.title: India attained independence on 15 August 1947.
points.text: 4. India attained independence on 15 August 1947. The Constitution of India was adopted on 26 November 1949 and came into force on 26 January 1950. It was framed by a Constituent Assembly representing different sections of Indian society. Democratic inclusion is secured through universal adult franchise, under which every citizen above the age of 18 years has the right to vote.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: The Constitution provides a broad framework of fundamental rights and constitutional duties.
points.text: 5. The Constitution provides a broad framework of fundamental rights and constitutional duties. Part III contains Fundamental Rights, which are enforceable before courts. Part IV contains the Directive Principles of State Policy, which guide governance and law-making. The laws and schemes referred to in this report seek to advance equality of opportunity and prohibit discrimination against citizens, including persons with disabilities.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: India follows a bicameral parliamentary system within a federal structure consisting of 28 States and 8 Union Territories.
points.text: 6. India follows a bicameral parliamentary system within a federal structure consisting of 28 States and 8 Union Territories.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: The Supreme Court of India has consistently held that Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles are complementary.
points.text: 7. The Supreme Court of India has consistently held that Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles are complementary. It has also interpreted the constitutional right to life as including the right to live with human dignity and the basic conditions necessary for such dignity, including food, clothing, shelter, a clean environment and basic education.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 came into force on 19 April 2017.
points.text: 8. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 came into force on 19 April 2017. It expanded the scope of disability rights and entitlements to cover non-discrimination, protection from abuse, violence and exploitation, community life, legal capacity and access to justice. The Act provides for a minimum of 4% reservation in government jobs and 5% reservation in seats in government and government-aided higher educational institutions for persons with benchmark disabilities. The report further notes the extension of reservation in promotion in Central Government establishments. The Act also places emphasis on accessibility in public buildings, transportation systems and information and communication technology ecosystems, while promoting education, health, social security, sports and cultural participation.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: The Supreme Court has played an important role in interpreting and enforcing the RPwD Act, 2016.
points.text: 9. The Supreme Court has played an important role in interpreting and enforcing the RPwD Act, 2016. In Vikash Kumar v. Union Public Service Commission, the Court held that persons with non-benchmark disabilities may also be entitled to reasonable accommodation, including the use of a scribe in examinations. In Rajive Raturi v. Union of India, the Court held that accessibility is integral to the exercise of fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19 and 21, covering physical environments, transport, information, communication and digital platforms.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: In State of Kerala v.
points.text: 10. In State of Kerala v. Leesamma Joseph, the Supreme Court held that persons with disabilities are entitled to reservation in promotion and directed the Union Government to frame executive instructions for reservation in promotion under Section 34 of the RPwD Act, 2016. In Rajneesh Kumar Pandey v. Union of India, the Court directed pupil-teacher ratios for children with disabilities and emphasised meaningful and effective education. Earlier, Rajive Raturi v. Union of India resulted in accessibility directions for public spaces, airports, railways and public transport. In Jeeja Ghosh v. Union of India, the Court awarded compensation for discriminatory treatment in air travel and affirmed the importance of dignity under Article 21.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: The right to education has been strengthened through the 86th Constitutional Amendment, which provides free and compulsory educ…
points.text: 11. The right to education has been strengthened through the 86th Constitutional Amendment, which provides free and compulsory education for children between 6 and 14 years as a fundamental right. This right is implemented through the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009. The Supreme Court’s decision in Rajneesh Kumar Pandey v. Union of India also addressed pupil-teacher ratios for children with disabilities, including 8:1 for cerebral palsy, 5:1 for intellectual disability and autism spectrum disorder, and 2:1 for deaf-blindness, together with a roadmap for integrating special educators into schools.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: The Right to Information Act, 2005 enables citizens to access information held by public authorities and promotes transparency…
points.text: 12. The Right to Information Act, 2005 enables citizens to access information held by public authorities and promotes transparency and accountability in governance. The Act allows citizens to seek information on all matters except those expressly exempted by law and establishes designated information officers to respond to such requests.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: The Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA), implemented from February 2006, provides livelihood secur…
points.text: 13. The Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA), implemented from February 2006, provides livelihood security in rural areas. It guarantees at least 100 days of wage employment in a financial year to every rural household whose adult members volunteer for unskilled manual work. Employment must be provided within 15 days of application; otherwise, an unemployment allowance is payable. The programme operates as a social safety net and advances growth with equity.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: The National Rural Health Mission was launched in 2005 to provide accessible, affordable and accountable health services, parti…
points.text: 14. The National Rural Health Mission was launched in 2005 to provide accessible, affordable and accountable health services, particularly to poorer households in remote rural regions. Its focus is on decentralised, community-owned health delivery and inter-sectoral convergence. Immunisation is a key intervention. The mission has been strengthened under the National Health Mission, which covers rural and urban health needs through primary, secondary and tertiary interventions. The earlier Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojana extended smart card-based cashless health insurance coverage to workers in the unorganised sector and several other vulnerable occupational groups.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: The National Health Mission continues to provide accessible and affordable health services.
points.text: 15. The National Health Mission continues to provide accessible and affordable health services. The Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana, launched on 23 September 2018, is described in the draft as the world’s largest health assurance scheme. It provides health coverage of Rs. 5 lakh per family per year for secondary and tertiary hospitalisation to over 12 crore poor and vulnerable families. Since September 2024, all senior citizens aged 70 years and above receive coverage under PM-JAY irrespective of income, benefiting approximately 4.5 crore families. The scheme includes persons with disabilities among beneficiary categories and covers pre-existing conditions from the first day.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: Remedies are supported by an independent judiciary and the separation of judicial and executive functions.
points.text: 16. Remedies are supported by an independent judiciary and the separation of judicial and executive functions. Indian legislation is subject to constitutional review, and executive action is also reviewable by courts. A person whose Fundamental Rights are violated may directly approach the Supreme Court, as this remedy is itself a Fundamental Right.
points.subsection: B. Constitutional, Political and Legal Framework

points.title: India is a party to major international human rights conventions and remains committed to the rights proclaimed in the Universa…
points.text: 17. India is a party to major international human rights conventions and remains committed to the rights proclaimed in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. India has signed and ratified several core human rights conventions, including the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights, the International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights, the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Racial Discrimination, the Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination against Women and the Convention on the Rights of the Child. In 2005, India ratified the two Optional Protocols to the Convention on the Rights of the Child, and in 2007 it ratified the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities.
points.subsection: General Framework for the Protection and Promotion of Human Rights

points.title: India implemented its CRPD commitments domestically through the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, which became ope…
points.text: 18. India implemented its CRPD commitments domestically through the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, which became operational on 15 June 2017 and replaced the Persons with Disabilities (Equal Opportunities, Protection of Rights and Full Participation) Act, 1995. The 2016 Act expanded the recognised categories of disability from 7 to 21 and increased reservation in government employment from 3% to 4%. India has not ratified the Optional Protocol to the CRPD.
points.subsection: General Framework for the Protection and Promotion of Human Rights

points.title: The Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993 was enacted to strengthen human rights protection through national institutions.
points.text: 19. The Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993 was enacted to strengthen human rights protection through national institutions. It enabled the establishment of the National Human Rights Commission and State Human Rights Commissions. Their jurisdiction reflects India’s federal structure and their functions support the protection and promotion of human rights.
points.subsection: A. Legal Framework for the Protection of Human Rights at the National Level

points.title: Human Rights Commissions may inquire into matters, follow up on findings, make recommendations to the concerned Government, app…
points.text: 20. Human Rights Commissions may inquire into matters, follow up on findings, make recommendations to the concerned Government, approach the High Court or Supreme Court for appropriate directions, orders or writs, and recommend interim relief to victims or their family members where necessary.
points.subsection: A. Legal Framework for the Protection of Human Rights at the National Level

points.title: In addition to the National and State Human Rights Commissions, dedicated commissions have been established to address the conc…
points.text: 21. In addition to the National and State Human Rights Commissions, dedicated commissions have been established to address the concerns of other vulnerable groups.
points.subsection: A. Legal Framework for the Protection of Human Rights at the National Level

points.title: The National Commission for Women was established by an Act of Parliament in 1990 to safeguard the rights and entitlements of w…
points.text: 22. The National Commission for Women was established by an Act of Parliament in 1990 to safeguard the rights and entitlements of women. It studies and monitors constitutional and legal protections relating to women, reviews legislation, investigates complaints and takes suo motu notice of matters involving deprivation of women’s rights.
points.subsection: A. Legal Framework for the Protection of Human Rights at the National Level

points.title: Under Sections 75 and 80 of the RPwD Act, 2016, the Chief Commissioner and State Commissioners for Persons with Disabilities ar…
points.text: 23. Under Sections 75 and 80 of the RPwD Act, 2016, the Chief Commissioner and State Commissioners for Persons with Disabilities are empowered to look into complaints relating to deprivation of disability rights and non-implementation of laws, rules, regulations, executive orders, guidelines and instructions issued for the welfare and protection of persons with disabilities.
points.subsection: A. Legal Framework for the Protection of Human Rights at the National Level

points.title: The Commissions for Protection of Child Rights Act, 2005 established the National Commission for Protection of Child Rights.
points.text: 24. The Commissions for Protection of Child Rights Act, 2005 established the National Commission for Protection of Child Rights. This mechanism reviews the implementation of child-rights policies and recommends remedial action in cases of violation. The RPwD Act, 2016 strengthens protections for children with disabilities through provisions on inclusive education and reasonable accommodation. The Government has also operationalised early screening and rehabilitation services for children aged 0-6 through 14 Cross Disability Early Intervention Centres at National Institutes and Composite Regional Centres.
points.subsection: A. Legal Framework for the Protection of Human Rights at the National Level

points.title: The National Council for Older Persons advises and supports the Government on policies and programmes for older persons and pro…
points.text: 25. The National Council for Older Persons advises and supports the Government on policies and programmes for older persons and provides feedback on implementation. The Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act, 2007 provides for tribunals and other measures for the maintenance and welfare of parents and senior citizens. The draft recognises the intersection of ageing and disability through equality, employment reservation, education reservation and accessibility provisions in the RPwD Act, 2016.
points.subsection: A. Legal Framework for the Protection of Human Rights at the National Level

points.title: The Accessible India Campaign, or Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan, was launched on 3 December 2015 as a national flagship initiative for…
points.text: 26. The Accessible India Campaign, or Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan, was launched on 3 December 2015 as a national flagship initiative for universal accessibility. The report records progress including 1,630 government buildings made accessible, all 709 A1, A and B category railway stations equipped with accessibility features, 35 international and 55 domestic airports made accessible, 627 government websites made accessible, and 71% of government and government-aided schools made barrier-free, covering 8,33,703 out of 11,68,292 schools.
points.subsection: A. Legal Framework for the Protection of Human Rights at the National Level

points.title: The Unique Disability Identity Project is being implemented to create a national database of persons with disabilities and issu…
points.text: 27. The Unique Disability Identity Project is being implemented to create a national database of persons with disabilities and issue UDID cards. The report states that more than 10.6 million UDID cards have been generated across 749 of 784 districts. It also notes the establishment of nine National Institutes, Composite Regional Centres, the Indian Sign Language Research and Training Centre, an Indian Sign Language dictionary of 10,000 words and expressions, and 14 Cross Disability Early Intervention Centres providing screening and rehabilitation services for children aged 0-6 years.
points.subsection: A. Legal Framework for the Protection of Human Rights at the National Level

points.title: The Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment is the administrative Ministry responsible for empowerment of disadvantaged and…
points.text: 28. The Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment is the administrative Ministry responsible for empowerment of disadvantaged and marginalised sections of society, including persons with disabilities. To provide focused attention to disability-related issues, a separate Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities was created in 2012 within the Ministry.
points.subsection: B. Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities

points.title: This report is submitted to fulfil India’s obligation under Article 35(1) of the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disab…
points.text: 29. This report is submitted to fulfil India’s obligation under Article 35(1) of the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities. In line with the treaty-specific reporting guidelines, the report first includes a common core document containing general country information and the broader framework for protection and promotion of human rights. It then provides treaty-specific analysis, describing legal developments, institutional measures and article-wise initiatives undertaken in relation to the CRPD.

points.title: The report was prepared with inputs from concerned Ministries and Departments of the Government of India, State Governments and…
points.text: 30. The report was prepared with inputs from concerned Ministries and Departments of the Government of India, State Governments and Union Territories, national and state human rights institutions, national institutes, the National Trust, the Rehabilitation Council of India and the Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities. Inputs were invited from Ministries dealing with home affairs, minority affairs, consumer affairs, food and public distribution, health and family welfare, housing, education, labour and employment, law and justice, panchayati raj, rural development, statistics and programme implementation, tribal affairs and women and child development.

points.title: The data-collection process used two questionnaires: one with 60 questions for State and Union Territory Governments and anothe…
points.text: 31. The data-collection process used two questionnaires: one with 60 questions for State and Union Territory Governments and another with 77 questions for Central Government Ministries and Departments. The questionnaires covered the period 2017-2022 and gathered information across eight thematic areas: education, healthcare, financial assistance and schemes, infrastructure and accessibility, employment, sports, disaster management, and legal and governance mechanisms. They sought both quantitative and qualitative inputs, including year-wise data, financial allocations and expenditure, beneficiary numbers and implementation status under the RPwD Act, 2016.

points.title: The State and Union Territory questionnaire focused on jurisdiction-specific implementation, including literacy rates, enrolmen…
points.text: 32. The State and Union Territory questionnaire focused on jurisdiction-specific implementation, including literacy rates, enrolment, scholarships, healthcare programmes, early intervention and rehabilitation centres, infrastructure accessibility, employment reservation and statutory bodies. The Central Government questionnaire addressed national policy, programme and oversight mechanisms, including UDID implementation, national health missions, skill development, Accessible India Campaign, disability certification and national institutions. Both questionnaires assessed compliance with statutory obligations under the RPwD Act, 2016, including appointment of Commissioners, advisory boards, committees and grievance redressal mechanisms.

points.title: Based on the inputs received and independently collected material, a draft report was prepared and discussed at a National Cons…
points.text: 33. Based on the inputs received and independently collected material, a draft report was prepared and discussed at a National Consultation involving concerned Ministries, State Governments, disability rights advocates, organisations of persons with disabilities and disability rights experts. The report was also made available virtually for stakeholder feedback. The subsequent draft was presented to the Central Coordination Committee, the apex disability policy-making body with representation from concerned Ministries, State Governments and non-governmental organisations working in the field of disability.

points.title: This report uses the terminology of the Convention and primarily refers to the subjects of the report as persons with disabilit…
points.text: 34. This report uses the terminology of the Convention and primarily refers to the subjects of the report as persons with disabilities. The RPwD Act, 2016 also adopts the term “persons with disabilities” as the preferred terminology. Where older terms such as “handicapped”, “mentally retarded”, “differently-abled” or “children with special needs” appear, they are retained only when referring to the wording of the relevant law, policy or rule. Such terms should be placed in quotation marks to indicate that they are not the preferred terminology of this report.

points.title: Before 2016, the primary disability-rights legislation in India was the Persons with Disabilities Act, 1995.
points.text: 35. Before 2016, the primary disability-rights legislation in India was the Persons with Disabilities Act, 1995. The 1995 Act was enacted in furtherance of India’s obligations as a signatory to the Proclamation on the Full Participation and Equality of People with Disabilities in the Asian and Pacific Region. Following India’s ratification of the CRPD in 2007, the 1995 Act was repealed and replaced by the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016.
points.subsection: A. Definition of Persons with Disabilities

points.title: The 2016 Act marks a significant shift in the definition of disability.
points.text: 36. The 2016 Act marks a significant shift in the definition of disability. Unlike the 1995 Act, which followed a primarily medical approach by listing conditions, the 2016 Act recognises the social dimension of disability and the role of barriers in restricting participation. It also expands the recognised categories of disability from 7 to 21. The present definition under the 2016 Act conforms to the approach reflected in Article 1 of the CRPD.
points.subsection: A. Definition of Persons with Disabilities

points.title: The 2016 Act also adopts definitions of “communication”, “discrimination in relation to disability” and “universal design” that…
points.text: 37. The 2016 Act also adopts definitions of “communication”, “discrimination in relation to disability” and “universal design” that are aligned with the Convention.
points.subsection: A. Definition of Persons with Disabilities

points.title: Unlike the 1995 Act, the 2016 Act expressly incorporates the requirement of a “long term” impairment as part of the definition…
points.text: 38. Unlike the 1995 Act, the 2016 Act expressly incorporates the requirement of a “long term” impairment as part of the definition of disability.

points.title: The 2016 Act gave statutory expression to reasonable accommodation for the first time in India.
points.text: 39. The 2016 Act gave statutory expression to reasonable accommodation for the first time in India. The definition in Section 2(y) is drawn from Article 2 of the CRPD. Section 3(5) also requires the appropriate Government to take necessary steps to ensure reasonable accommodation for persons with disabilities.

points.title: Even before the statutory incorporation of reasonable accommodation, Indian courts had recognised the principle as part of equa…
points.text: 40. Even before the statutory incorporation of reasonable accommodation, Indian courts had recognised the principle as part of equality jurisprudence. Equality under the Constitution requires not only formal equal treatment, but substantive equality. In the disability-rights context, this places a positive obligation on the State to ensure that persons with disabilities are able to enjoy rights equally and effectively.

points.title: Indian courts have interpreted reasonable accommodation broadly.
points.text: 41. Indian courts have interpreted reasonable accommodation broadly. The right is not confined only to persons with benchmark disabilities. The Supreme Court has held that employees with conditions such as colour blindness, even when not falling within the defined statutory categories, must be accommodated wherever their functional capacity permits. A contrary approach would undermine equal opportunity in public employment.

points.title: According to the most recent country-wide Census referred to in the draft, there are 2,68,14,994 persons with disabilities in I…
points.text: 42. According to the most recent country-wide Census referred to in the draft, there are 2,68,14,994 persons with disabilities in India, constituting 2.21% of the total population. Census 2011 classified disability under the heads of seeing, hearing, speech, movement, mental retardation, mental illness, any other disability and multiple disability.
points.subsection: D. Counting of Persons with Disabilities

points.title: Before the RPwD Act, the general principles set out in Article 3 of the CRPD were reflected primarily in judicial decisions rat…
points.text: 43. Before the RPwD Act, the general principles set out in Article 3 of the CRPD were reflected primarily in judicial decisions rather than in disability-specific legislation. Courts have held that these principles are consistent with the Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles in the Constitution of India.

points.title: The RPwD Act gives express legislative recognition to these general principles through its Preamble and rights framework.
points.text: 44. The RPwD Act gives express legislative recognition to these general principles through its Preamble and rights framework. This has more concretely integrated the CRPD’s guiding principles into India’s disability-rights framework.

points.title: India’s ratification of the CRPD is both an international commitment and an advancement of its constitutional principles.
points.text: 45. India’s ratification of the CRPD is both an international commitment and an advancement of its constitutional principles. To give effect to Article 4 of the Convention, India has developed laws, policies, plans, initiatives and programmes for the rights and empowerment of persons with disabilities.

points.title: The implementation and development of disability rights falls primarily under the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Dis…
points.text: 46. The implementation and development of disability rights falls primarily under the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities within the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment. The Department oversees implementation of the RPwD Act, supervises statutory functionaries, creates and administers welfare schemes, and issues guidelines and regulations. It also oversees the National Trust Act and the Rehabilitation Council of India Act. The Department further coordinates with nine National Institutes, the National Divyangjan Finance and Development Corporation and the Artificial Limbs Manufacturing Corporation of India, which support rehabilitation services, research, concessional finance and assistive devices.

points.title: India’s constitutional jurisprudence on equality and non-discrimination is consistent with CRPD principles.
points.text: 47. India’s constitutional jurisprudence on equality and non-discrimination is consistent with CRPD principles. Articles 14, 15 and 16 provide for equality before the law, prohibition of discrimination on specified grounds and equality of opportunity in public employment.

points.title: Courts have held that discrimination on the basis of disability is contrary to the constitutional scheme.
points.text: 48. Courts have held that discrimination on the basis of disability is contrary to the constitutional scheme. To achieve substantive equality under Article 14, courts have placed a positive obligation on the State to take appropriate special measures so that persons with disabilities can enjoy Fundamental Rights, especially the right to life, on an equal basis with others.

points.title: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 is the central and most comprehensive disability-rights legislation in India.
points.text: 49. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 is the central and most comprehensive disability-rights legislation in India. It replaced the Persons with Disabilities (Equal Opportunities, Protection of Rights and Full Participation) Act, 1995 in order to give effect to the CRPD. The 2016 Act goes beyond the earlier framework by recognising the social dimensions of disability and by creating a broader rights framework. It also strengthens affirmative action by increasing reservation in government and government-aided higher education institutions from 3% to 5% for persons with benchmark disabilities, and increasing reservation in government employment from 3% to 4%.
points.subsection: H. Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016

points.title: The RPwD Act was passed by the Parliament of India on 27 December 2016 and came into force on 19 April 2017.
points.text: 50. The RPwD Act was passed by the Parliament of India on 27 December 2016 and came into force on 19 April 2017. India follows a federal structure in which legislative powers are distributed between Parliament and State Legislatures under Article 246 and the Seventh Schedule to the Constitution. Parliament also has power under Article 253 to make laws for implementing international agreements. The 2016 Act applies across India and establishes enforcement and supervisory mechanisms at both national and state levels.
points.subsection: H. Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016

points.title: The broad structure of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 is set out chapter-wise below.
points.text: 51. The broad structure of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 is set out chapter-wise below. The Act begins with preliminary definitions and moves through rights and entitlements, education, skill development and employment, social security, health, rehabilitation, accessibility, certification, advisory bodies, Commissioners, Special Courts, funds, offences and miscellaneous provisions.
points.subsection: H. Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016

points.title: Chapter XVI provides penalties for contravention of the Act, Rules or Regulations.
points.text: 52. Chapter XVI provides penalties for contravention of the Act, Rules or Regulations. The Act also criminalises specified atrocities against persons with disabilities and makes offences triable by a Special Court. Where an offence is committed by a company, liability may extend to persons responsible for the conduct of the company where the statutory conditions are met.
points.subsection: H. Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016

points.title: The Act creates important implementation and oversight bodies.
points.text: 53. The Act creates important implementation and oversight bodies. The Central Advisory Board on Disability and the State Advisory Boards on Disability function as consultative and advisory bodies for evolving disability policy. The Chief Commissioner and State Commissioners inquire into deprivation of rights, initiate corrective action, monitor implementation, promote awareness, encourage research and monitor the use of funds for the benefit of persons with disabilities.
points.subsection: H. Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016

points.title: A group of schemes has been developed for implementation of the Act, including the Creation of Barrier-Free Environment for per…
points.text: 54. A group of schemes has been developed for implementation of the Act, including the Creation of Barrier-Free Environment for persons with disabilities, Accessible India Campaign, National Action Plan for Skill Development of persons with disabilities, Unique Disability Identification Project, Awareness Generation and Publicity with in-service training, assistance to spinal injury centres, disability research and Central Project Monitoring-cum Data Strategy Unit.
points.subsection: H. Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016

points.title: The Rehabilitation Council of India Act, 1992 establishes the Rehabilitation Council of India.
points.text: 55. The Rehabilitation Council of India Act, 1992 establishes the Rehabilitation Council of India. The Council regulates and monitors the training of rehabilitation professionals and personnel, and promotes research in rehabilitation and special education. Its functions include determining minimum education standards, recommending recognition of qualifications in India and abroad, conducting inspections, registering rehabilitation professionals and determining professional privileges and conduct.
points.subsection: I. Rehabilitation Council of India Act, 1992

points.title: The Rehabilitation Council of India is actively involved in developing and strengthening education and training for rehabilitat…
points.text: 56. The Rehabilitation Council of India is actively involved in developing and strengthening education and training for rehabilitation professionals in India. The report records that recent work has focused on updating course curricula in disability rehabilitation.
points.subsection: I. Rehabilitation Council of India Act, 1992

points.title: The National Trust Act is a specialised law for persons with autism, cerebral palsy, mental retardation and multiple disabilities.
points.text: 57. The National Trust Act is a specialised law for persons with autism, cerebral palsy, mental retardation and multiple disabilities. Its objects include enabling persons with disabilities to live as independently and fully as possible within or close to their community; strengthening family-based support; supporting registered organisations during family crises; addressing the needs of persons without family support; providing care and protection after the death of parents or guardians; evolving procedures for guardianship and trusteeship; and facilitating equal opportunity, rights protection and full participation.
points.subsection: J. The National Trust for Welfare of Persons with Autism, Cerebral Palsy, Mental Retardation and Multiple Disabilities Act, 1999

points.title: The National Trust performs two major functions.
points.text: 58. The National Trust performs two major functions. First, Local Level Committees constituted in every district appoint guardians for persons covered by the Act. Appointment or removal of guardians follows an application by a parent, relative or welfare organisation with appropriate permission. The report records that 68,500 guardians have been appointed by Local Level Committees across India since the inception of the Act.
points.subsection: J. The National Trust for Welfare of Persons with Autism, Cerebral Palsy, Mental Retardation and Multiple Disabilities Act, 1999

points.title: Second, the National Trust funds and oversees welfare schemes implemented through registered organisations.
points.text: 59. Second, the National Trust funds and oversees welfare schemes implemented through registered organisations. The report records 602 organisations registered with the National Trust. These organisations implement one or more of the following schemes: Disha for early intervention and school readiness; Vikaas for day care; Samarth for respite care and residential support; Gharaunda for adult group housing; and Nirmaya for health insurance.
points.subsection: J. The National Trust for Welfare of Persons with Autism, Cerebral Palsy, Mental Retardation and Multiple Disabilities Act, 1999

points.title: Article 14 of the Constitution of India guarantees equality before the law to all persons within India, including persons with…
points.text: 60. Article 14 of the Constitution of India guarantees equality before the law to all persons within India, including persons with disabilities. This constitutional guarantee aligns with Article 5(1) of the Convention.
points.subsection: A. Right to Equality and Non-discrimination under the Constitution

points.title: Articles 15(1) and 16(1) prohibit discrimination on specified grounds, including religion, race, caste, sex, descent, place of…
points.text: 61. Articles 15(1) and 16(1) prohibit discrimination on specified grounds, including religion, race, caste, sex, descent, place of birth or residence. These provisions also empower the State to provide reservation for backward classes of citizens where constitutionally permissible.
points.subsection: A. Right to Equality and Non-discrimination under the Constitution

points.title: The RPwD Act strengthens equality and non-discrimination through Section 3.
points.text: 62. The RPwD Act strengthens equality and non-discrimination through Section 3. The provision requires the appropriate Government to ensure equality, dignity and respect for integrity; to use the capacity of persons with disabilities by providing an appropriate environment; to prevent discrimination on the ground of disability unless justified by a legitimate and proportionate aim; to ensure that no person is deprived of personal liberty only on the ground of disability; and to take steps for reasonable accommodation.
points.subsection: B. Right to Equality and Non-discrimination under the RPwD Act

points.title: Section 3 aligns with Article 5 of the Convention.
points.text: 63. Section 3 aligns with Article 5 of the Convention. The rights to equality and non-discrimination are reflected in Article 5(1) of the Convention and Sections 3(1) and 3(4) of the Act. Measures for de facto equality are reflected in Article 5(3) and Sections 3(2) and 3(3). Reasonable accommodation is reflected in Article 5(3) and Section 3(5).
points.subsection: B. Right to Equality and Non-discrimination under the RPwD Act

points.title: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Rules, 2017 provide the implementation framework.
points.text: 64. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Rules, 2017 provide the implementation framework. They ensure that persons with disabilities are not made to bear the cost of reasonable accommodation; create a complaint mechanism; require public institutions to frame equal opportunity policies and make them publicly available; and provide rules for computing and implementing reservation in vacancies.
points.subsection: B. Right to Equality and Non-discrimination under the RPwD Act

points.title: A person with disability who faces disability-based discrimination may invoke the writ jurisdiction of the Supreme Court or the…
points.text: 65. A person with disability who faces disability-based discrimination may invoke the writ jurisdiction of the Supreme Court or the High Court. Article 32 provides the right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights, and Article 226 empowers High Courts to issue writs within their jurisdiction.

points.title: A person may also approach the Chief Commissioner or the State Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities.
points.text: 66. A person may also approach the Chief Commissioner or the State Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities. These authorities may inquire into deprivation of rights and safeguards available to persons with disabilities and take up the matter with appropriate authorities for corrective action.

points.title: The 2016 Act creates offences intended to prevent discrimination and atrocities against persons with disabilities.
points.text: 67. The 2016 Act creates offences intended to prevent discrimination and atrocities against persons with disabilities. Section 90 prevents misuse of the corporate form by recognising liability of companies and responsible individuals, where applicable. Government officials may also be tried under the Act, subject to sanction by the appropriate Government before cognisance is taken.
points.subsection: D. Penalisation of Breach of the Act and Atrocities against Persons with Disabilities

points.title: Breach of any provision of the Act may attract a fine up to ten thousand rupees for the first contravention and up to five lakh…
points.text: 68. Breach of any provision of the Act may attract a fine up to ten thousand rupees for the first contravention and up to five lakh rupees for subsequent contraventions.
points.subsection: D. Penalisation of Breach of the Act and Atrocities against Persons with Disabilities

points.title: Section 92 criminalises specified atrocities against persons with disabilities.
points.text: 69. Section 92 criminalises specified atrocities against persons with disabilities. These include assault or use of force with intent to dishonour a person with disability or outrage the modesty of a woman with disability, and knowingly denying food or fluids to a person with disability who is under one’s charge or control. The punishment is imprisonment for not less than six months and up to five years, together with a fine.
points.subsection: D. Penalisation of Breach of the Act and Atrocities against Persons with Disabilities

points.title: To ensure speedy trial of offences under the Act, States are required to designate a Special Court in each district, attached t…
points.text: 70. To ensure speedy trial of offences under the Act, States are required to designate a Special Court in each district, attached to an existing Court of Session, for trial of such offences.
points.subsection: D. Penalisation of Breach of the Act and Atrocities against Persons with Disabilities

points.title: Reasonable accommodation is incorporated into Section 3(5) of the RPwD Act.
points.text: 71. Reasonable accommodation is incorporated into Section 3(5) of the RPwD Act. Even before statutory recognition, the Supreme Court had read this principle into Article 14 of the Constitution as part of substantive equality.

points.title: In Deaf Employees Welfare Association v.
points.text: 72. In Deaf Employees Welfare Association v. Union of India, the Supreme Court recognised reasonable accommodation by referring to the CRPD and affirming its application in India, keeping in view India’s status as a signatory to the Convention.

points.title: In Vikash Kumar v.
points.text: 73. In Vikash Kumar v. Union Public Service Commission, the Supreme Court held that reasonable accommodation is a positive obligation of the State. It enables persons with disabilities to lead lives of equal dignity. In that case, the Court held that providing a scribe for a public examination may fall within reasonable accommodation, even where the examination authority had denied it.

points.title: In Avni Prakash v.
points.text: 74. In Avni Prakash v. National Testing Agency, the Supreme Court held that extra writing time may form part of reasonable accommodation. The Court directed examination authorities to rectify denial of such accommodation and remain aware of their future obligation to provide it.

points.title: India has an affirmative action framework for socially, economically and educationally disadvantaged communities, including per…
points.text: 75. India has an affirmative action framework for socially, economically and educationally disadvantaged communities, including persons with disabilities. This is consistent with Article 5(4) of the Convention.

points.title: The RPwD Act mandates reservation of at least 5% of the total vacant seats in Government institutions of higher education and G…
points.text: 76. The RPwD Act mandates reservation of at least 5% of the total vacant seats in Government institutions of higher education and Government-aided higher education institutions for persons with benchmark disabilities.

points.title: The Act also mandates reservation of at least 4% of job vacancies in each cadre of each Government establishment.
points.text: 77. The Act also mandates reservation of at least 4% of job vacancies in each cadre of each Government establishment. Within this reservation, 1% is earmarked for blindness and low vision, 1% for deaf and hard of hearing, 1% for locomotor disability, and 1% for autism, intellectual disability, specific learning disability, mental illness and multiple disabilities.

points.title: Data received from Central Government ministries and from States and Union Territories such as Chandigarh, Meghalaya, Goa, Mizo…
points.text: 78. Data received from Central Government ministries and from States and Union Territories such as Chandigarh, Meghalaya, Goa, Mizoram and Uttarakhand indicates adherence to the minimum reservation requirements of 5% in educational institutions and 4% in public employment.

points.title: The report further records that Andhra Pradesh, Assam, Bihar, Chhattisgarh, Gujarat, Himachal Pradesh, Karnataka, Kerala, Madhy…
points.text: 79. The report further records that Andhra Pradesh, Assam, Bihar, Chhattisgarh, Gujarat, Himachal Pradesh, Karnataka, Kerala, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Meghalaya, Nagaland, Odisha, Punjab, Rajasthan, Sikkim, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Tripura and Uttar Pradesh have updated reservation policies to align with the 2016 Act.

points.title: The Constitution of India provides a strong framework for formal and substantive equality.
points.text: 80. The Constitution of India provides a strong framework for formal and substantive equality. Article 14 guarantees equality, Article 15 prohibits discrimination on the basis of sex, and Article 16 affirms equality in public employment. Article 15(3) empowers the State to make special provisions for women, including women with disabilities. The Directive Principles also place a positive obligation on the State to improve the status of women. This constitutional framework aligns with Article 6 of the Convention.

points.title: The RPwD Act incorporates Article 6 of the Convention through Section 4, which requires the appropriate Government and local au…
points.text: 81. The RPwD Act incorporates Article 6 of the Convention through Section 4, which requires the appropriate Government and local authorities to take measures to ensure that women and children with disabilities enjoy their rights equally with others.

points.title: Census 2011 recorded 26.8 million persons with disabilities in India.
points.text: 82. Census 2011 recorded 26.8 million persons with disabilities in India. Of these, 11.8 million are women. Women therefore constitute approximately 44% of the population of persons with disabilities recorded in the Census.

points.title: The National Policy for Persons with Disabilities, 2006 predates the CRPD but recognises the need to give special attention to…
points.text: 83. The National Policy for Persons with Disabilities, 2006 predates the CRPD but recognises the need to give special attention to doubly disadvantaged groups. It identifies women with disabilities as a separate category and envisages special programmes for their empowerment. It also aims to ensure that at least 25% of beneficiaries of rehabilitation programmes are women, and provides for short-stay homes, hostels for working women with disabilities, homes for aged women with disabilities and financial assistance for mothers with disabilities who need childcare support.
points.subsection: D. National Policy for Persons with Disabilities, 2006

points.title: The Ministry of Women and Child Development implements Mission Shakti as the main umbrella scheme for women.
points.text: 84. The Ministry of Women and Child Development implements Mission Shakti as the main umbrella scheme for women. It has two sub-components: Sambal, focused on security and protection, and Samarthya, focused on empowerment.
points.subsection: E. Policies and Schemes for the Empowerment of Women with Disabilities

points.title: The Sambal component provides integrated service delivery infrastructure, especially through One Stop Centres.
points.text: 85. The Sambal component provides integrated service delivery infrastructure, especially through One Stop Centres. These centres provide immediate assistance to women in distress, including temporary shelter, medical and police assistance, counselling and legal support. The Women’s Helpline 181 is a toll-free 24-hour service for emergency and non-emergency responses to women affected by violence. One Stop Centres receive budgetary support and directives for accessibility, including wheelchairs, stretchers and ramps.
points.subsection: E. Policies and Schemes for the Empowerment of Women with Disabilities

points.title: The Women’s Helpline may also be accessed through text or other message-based formats by persons who are unable to speak or com…
points.text: 86. The Women’s Helpline may also be accessed through text or other message-based formats by persons who are unable to speak or communicate due to situational or physical challenges. It is intended to be sensitive to the needs of persons who are hearing or speech impaired and persons with disabilities. If a call is interrupted or the caller cannot explain the problem, the system may trace the call and facilitate emergency response through the nearest One Stop Centre or Emergency Response Support System.
points.subsection: E. Policies and Schemes for the Empowerment of Women with Disabilities

points.title: Beti Bachao Beti Padhao is a national awareness and mindset-change programme that promotes the value of the girl child through…
points.text: 87. Beti Bachao Beti Padhao is a national awareness and mindset-change programme that promotes the value of the girl child through multi-sectoral interventions. Nari Adalat is an experimental grassroots platform that provides women an alternative grievance redressal mechanism at the Gram Panchayat level through negotiation, mediation and reconciliation.
points.subsection: E. Policies and Schemes for the Empowerment of Women with Disabilities

points.title: The Samarthya component focuses on empowerment.
points.text: 88. The Samarthya component focuses on empowerment. SANKALP: Hubs for Empowerment of Women bridge information gaps regarding schemes and facilities for women. The report records that these hubs have grown to 35, including 742 at the district level, and that 2,793,400 beneficiaries have sought assistance through them.
points.subsection: E. Policies and Schemes for the Empowerment of Women with Disabilities

points.title: The Palna Programme addresses time poverty by providing day-care crèche services to women, including women with disabilities, w…
points.text: 89. The Palna Programme addresses time poverty by providing day-care crèche services to women, including women with disabilities, who disproportionately carry childcare responsibilities. The report records 1,249 centres across the country and 164,730 beneficiaries assisted.
points.subsection: E. Policies and Schemes for the Empowerment of Women with Disabilities

points.title: The Pradhan Mantri Matru Vandana Yojana provides financial assistance during maternity.
points.text: 90. The Pradhan Mantri Matru Vandana Yojana provides financial assistance during maternity. The report records that this scheme has benefited 41,900,000 persons.
points.subsection: E. Policies and Schemes for the Empowerment of Women with Disabilities

points.title: Sakhi Niwas centres provide affordable and safe housing for working women.
points.text: 91. Sakhi Niwas centres provide affordable and safe housing for working women. The report records 523 such centres, which have assisted 529,483 women.
points.subsection: E. Policies and Schemes for the Empowerment of Women with Disabilities

points.title: Shakti Sadan centres provide relief and rehabilitation homes for women in difficult circumstances, including violence, traffick…
points.text: 92. Shakti Sadan centres provide relief and rehabilitation homes for women in difficult circumstances, including violence, trafficking and destitution. The report records 404 centres and 292,681 beneficiaries.
points.subsection: E. Policies and Schemes for the Empowerment of Women with Disabilities

points.title: Mission Shakti Guidelines recognise the layered discrimination faced by women with disabilities and require disability-sensitiv…
points.text: 93. Mission Shakti Guidelines recognise the layered discrimination faced by women with disabilities and require disability-sensitive implementation in relevant places. For example, if accommodation is limited in Sakhi Niwas, the Guidelines require preference to women with disabilities. One Stop Centres are also required to be made accessible through ramps and other facilities.
points.subsection: E. Policies and Schemes for the Empowerment of Women with Disabilities

points.title: The Government of India has adopted gender budgeting as a gender-sensitive approach to allocation of public resources.
points.text: 94. The Government of India has adopted gender budgeting as a gender-sensitive approach to allocation of public resources. Introduced in 2005-2006, the approach has increased budgetary attention to women-focused schemes. Schemes are classified into Part A, Part B and Part C depending on the proportion of the scheme directed towards women. The report records that in FY 2025-26, 8.86% of the total budget has been designated as the Gender Budget, comprising 23.5% Part A schemes, 72.75% Part B schemes and 3.75% Part C schemes. It further records that this share has increased by 4.5% since FY 2014-15.

points.title: The Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005 protects women, including women with disabilities, from domestic viole…
points.text: 95. The Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005 protects women, including women with disabilities, from domestic violence. The Act defines domestic violence broadly to include acts, omissions or conduct that harm, injure or endanger the health, safety, life, limb or well-being of the aggrieved person, whether mental or physical. It provides immediate remedies such as protection orders, temporary custody orders, monetary relief, compensation and access to shelter homes.
points.subsection: G. Protection for Women against Domestic Violence

points.title: The National Commission for Women and State Commissions for Women advance women’s rights and facilitate redressal of rights vio…
points.text: 96. The National Commission for Women and State Commissions for Women advance women’s rights and facilitate redressal of rights violations before appropriate authorities. The report records that these Commissions have, in the past, redressed grievances of women with disabilities.

points.title: The Constitution recognises the special position of children.
points.text: 97. The Constitution recognises the special position of children. Article 39(f) directs the State to ensure that children are given opportunities and facilities to develop in a healthy manner and in conditions of freedom and dignity, and that childhood and youth are protected against exploitation and moral and material abandonment. Article 15(3) permits the State to make special provisions for children.

points.title: Children are defined as persons between 0 and 18 years of age under the Convention on the Rights of the Child and the Indian Ma…
points.text: 98. Children are defined as persons between 0 and 18 years of age under the Convention on the Rights of the Child and the Indian Majority Act, 1875. The report notes that Census 2011 approximates the number of children with disabilities by age group. The age-wise table should be inserted or verified from the Census table before final consolidation, because the present point introduces the table but does not reproduce the numerical age-group data in the available text.
points.subsection: B. Percentage of Children with Disabilities

points.title: Section 4 of the RPwD Act requires the Government to ensure that children with disabilities enjoy their rights equally with oth…
points.text: 99. Section 4 of the RPwD Act requires the Government to ensure that children with disabilities enjoy their rights equally with others. It also requires appropriate authorities to ensure that children with disabilities can express their views on matters affecting them and receive appropriate support to do so.

points.title: The National Charter for Children, 2003 is an early articulation of rights for children with disabilities.
points.text: 100. The National Charter for Children, 2003 is an early articulation of rights for children with disabilities. It requires the State and the community to ensure that children with disabilities are able to live with dignity and respect, receive education, healthcare and appropriate training, and benefit from early intervention and prevention programmes supported by scientific study.

points.title: The Commissions for Protection of Child Rights Act, 2005 establishes the National Commission and State Commissions for Protecti…
points.text: 101. The Commissions for Protection of Child Rights Act, 2005 establishes the National Commission and State Commissions for Protection of Child Rights, along with Children's Courts for speedy trial of matters concerning children. These bodies are empowered to monitor compliance with child-rights laws and judicial decisions, and their welfare mandate extends to children with disabilities.

points.title: Article 21A of the Constitution guarantees free and compulsory education to all children between six and fourteen years of age.
points.text: 102. Article 21A of the Constitution guarantees free and compulsory education to all children between six and fourteen years of age. This guarantee includes children with disabilities and operates with the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009.

points.title: Section 16 of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 requires appropriate governments and local authorities to ensur…
points.text: 103. Section 16 of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 requires appropriate governments and local authorities to ensure that educational institutions funded or recognised by them provide inclusive education. The core measures are: non-discriminatory admission; equal opportunities in sports and recreation; accessible buildings and campuses; reasonable accommodation; individualised support; appropriate languages, modes and means of communication for blind, deaf and deafblind learners; early detection of specific learning disabilities; monitoring of participation and attainment; and transportation support for children with disabilities and attendants of children with high support needs.

points.title: Section 17 operationalises inclusive education by requiring periodic surveys to identify children with disabilities, assess the…
points.text: 104. Section 17 operationalises inclusive education by requiring periodic surveys to identify children with disabilities, assess their learning needs, and identify institutional capacity, professional-development needs and resource gaps. This converts the legal duty of inclusion into a planning and implementation exercise.

points.title: The Department of School Education and Literacy, Ministry of Education, organised an Inclusive Education Summit in New Delhi fr…
points.text: 105. The Department of School Education and Literacy, Ministry of Education, organised an Inclusive Education Summit in New Delhi from 21-23 January 2026. The Summit focused on using technology and assistive devices to improve access and learning outcomes, capacity-building, teacher training, inclusive sports, vocational education, curriculum adaptation and education-to-employment pathways.

points.title: At the Summit, the PRASHAST 2.0 App was presented as a digital school-level disability-screening tool to support teachers and e…
points.text: 106. At the Summit, the PRASHAST 2.0 App was presented as a digital school-level disability-screening tool to support teachers and educators in early identification of the 21 disabilities recognised under the 2016 Act.

points.title: Under Samagra Shiksha, inclusive education for children with disabilities is supported through an allocation of more than Rs.
points.text: 107. Under Samagra Shiksha, inclusive education for children with disabilities is supported through an allocation of more than Rs. 1,000 crore towards resources, teacher training, early identification and related support. Major interventions include identification, functional and formal assessment, appropriate educational placement, Individualised Education Plans, free aids and appliances, transport or escort support, teacher training, resource teachers, therapeutic support and barrier-free access. The scheme also supports stipends, travel allowance and home-based education with special educators where required.

points.title: The scheme follows a Zero Rejection Policy: no educational institution should refuse admission on the ground of disability.
points.text: 108. The scheme follows a Zero Rejection Policy: no educational institution should refuse admission on the ground of disability. Instead, the institution must provide reasonable accommodation and respond to the child's specific learning needs.

points.title: In Rajneesh Kumar Pandey & Others v.
points.text: 109. In Rajneesh Kumar Pandey & Others v. Union of India & Others, the Supreme Court recognised that the teacher-pupil ratio for learners with disabilities must be lower than the general ratio. Pending final specification, it recorded interim ratios of 8:1 for children with cerebral palsy, 5:1 for children with intellectual disability, autism spectrum disorder and specific learning disabilities, and 2:1 for deafblind children and children with a combination of two or more of the seven disabilities.

points.title: The All India Council for Technical Education has introduced the Saksham Scholarship Scheme to support students with disabiliti…
points.text: 110. The All India Council for Technical Education has introduced the Saksham Scholarship Scheme to support students with disabilities pursuing technical degree and diploma programmes. The the report records 5,230 beneficiaries during 2019-2024.

points.title: Early identification and prevention of disabilities is a crucial part of child-rights implementation.
points.text: 111. Early identification and prevention of disabilities is a crucial part of child-rights implementation. The Central Government's Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK) provides child health screening and early intervention services. It screens children for the “4 Ds”: defects at birth, diseases, deficiencies and developmental delays, and links children to appropriate treatment and support.

points.title: Management under RBSK is delivered through District Early Intervention Centres (DEICs) and, where required, tertiary-care refer…
points.text: 112. Management under RBSK is delivered through District Early Intervention Centres (DEICs) and, where required, tertiary-care referral points. DEICs bring together multidisciplinary professionals to support children, especially in the 0-6 age group, through medical, dental, occupational therapy, physical therapy, psychological, cognition, audiology, speech pathology, vision, nutrition, laboratory and social-support services.

points.title: State inputs from Meghalaya, Chandigarh, Mizoram, Uttarakhand, Goa and Uttar Pradesh indicate active implementation of RBSK, wh…
points.text: 113. State inputs from Meghalaya, Chandigarh, Mizoram, Uttarakhand, Goa and Uttar Pradesh indicate active implementation of RBSK, while implementation in some jurisdictions, including the Andaman and Nicobar Islands, is still developing.

points.title: The Disha (Early Intervention and School Readiness) Scheme focuses on children covered by the National Trust Act in the 0-10 ag…
points.text: 114. The Disha (Early Intervention and School Readiness) Scheme focuses on children covered by the National Trust Act in the 0-10 age group. It provides day-care and professional support through Registered Organisations. The the report records 590 ROs across India, with the distribution shown below.

points.title: The National Disaster Management Authority Guidelines recognise the heightened vulnerability of children, women and older perso…
points.text: 115. The National Disaster Management Authority Guidelines recognise the heightened vulnerability of children, women and older persons with disabilities in disaster situations, and require this factor to be incorporated into disaster management planning.

points.title: Section 24 of the 2016 Act requires appropriate governments to formulate social security schemes for the protection and welfare…
points.text: 116. Section 24 of the 2016 Act requires appropriate governments to formulate social security schemes for the protection and welfare of persons with disabilities, including facilities for children with disabilities who have no family, have been abandoned, or are without shelter or livelihood.

points.title: The best interest of the child remains the guiding consideration in decisions involving children, including under family law an…
points.text: 117. The best interest of the child remains the guiding consideration in decisions involving children, including under family law and the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015.

points.title: The Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 applies to all children, including children with disabilities.
points.text: 118. The Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 applies to all children, including children with disabilities. The Act permits the use of special educators and support persons where a child has a mental or physical disability.

points.title: The Central Adoption Resource Authority guidelines contain special provisions for children with special needs, with the categor…
points.text: 119. The Central Adoption Resource Authority guidelines contain special provisions for children with special needs, with the category understood in relation to the 2016 Act.

points.title: Section 92(d) of the 2016 Act makes it an offence for a person in a position to dominate the will of a child or woman with disa…
points.text: 120. Section 92(d) of the 2016 Act makes it an offence for a person in a position to dominate the will of a child or woman with disability to use that position for sexual exploitation. This operates alongside other offences against persons with disabilities and is punishable with imprisonment and fine.

points.title: The judiciary has repeatedly emphasised the State's responsibility to ensure the safety and security of children with intellect…
points.text: 121. The judiciary has repeatedly emphasised the State's responsibility to ensure the safety and security of children with intellectual disabilities in government and private institutions, especially against sexual abuse. In Reena Banerjee v. State (NCT of Delhi), the Supreme Court has directed National Law Universities to conduct surveys of such institutions, on the basis of which further guidelines may be issued.

points.title: In May 2024, the POCSO Committee and Juvenile Justice Committee resolved to prepare a handbook on protecting the rights of chil…
points.text: 122. In May 2024, the POCSO Committee and Juvenile Justice Committee resolved to prepare a handbook on protecting the rights of children living with disability, including children in conflict with law and children in need of care and protection, with attention to intersectionality across gender, LGBTQIA+ identity, caste and community. The handbook is recorded as under development and not yet published.

points.title: Article 8 of the Convention is implemented through statutory duties under the RPwD Act and the National Trust Act, which requir…
points.text: 123. Article 8 of the Convention is implemented through statutory duties under the RPwD Act and the National Trust Act, which require awareness-raising and sensitisation around disability rights.
points.subsection: A. Statutory Obligations under the RPwDA and NTA

points.title: Section 39 of the RPwD Act places the responsibility for awareness-raising on the appropriate government, acting with the relev…
points.text: 124. Section 39 of the RPwD Act places the responsibility for awareness-raising on the appropriate government, acting with the relevant commissioner. It requires awareness campaigns and sensitisation programmes to support the rights recognised in the Act.
points.subsection: A. Statutory Obligations under the RPwDA and NTA

points.title: Section 10(g) of the National Trust Act requires the Trust to facilitate equal opportunities, protection of rights and full par…
points.text: 125. Section 10(g) of the National Trust Act requires the Trust to facilitate equal opportunities, protection of rights and full participation of persons with disabilities. Section 11(2)(a) empowers the Board to approve programmes promoting independent living in the community through a conducive environment.
points.subsection: A. Statutory Obligations under the RPwDA and NTA

points.title: The 2021-22 Annual Report of the Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities records that Andaman and Nicobar, Assam, Biha…
points.text: 126. The 2021-22 Annual Report of the Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities records that Andaman and Nicobar, Assam, Bihar, Chandigarh and Chhattisgarh undertook regular awareness and sensitisation programmes.
points.subsection: A. Statutory Obligations under the RPwDA and NTA

points.title: The Awareness Generation and Publicity Scheme is administered by the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities, Mi…
points.text: 127. The Awareness Generation and Publicity Scheme is administered by the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities, Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment. Operational since 2014, it aims to disseminate information and sensitise stakeholders so that persons with disabilities can enjoy equal opportunity, equity and social justice, and participate meaningfully in national development.

points.title: The scheme is implemented through national and international awareness events, outreach with commercial establishments, communi…
points.text: 128. The scheme is implemented through national and international awareness events, outreach with commercial establishments, community radio and television participation, appointment of ambassadors, helplines/helpdesks and content development.

points.title: Divya Kala Mela is regularly organised under this scheme to provide artisans with disabilities a platform to showcase and sell…
points.text: 129. Divya Kala Mela is regularly organised under this scheme to provide artisans with disabilities a platform to showcase and sell their products.

points.title: International Day of Persons with Disabilities is commemorated on 3 December each year.
points.text: 130. International Day of Persons with Disabilities is commemorated on 3 December each year. Events organised across India on this day raise public awareness about disability rights, prejudice, harmful practices and health-related concerns associated with prevention of disability.
points.subsection: C. International Day of Persons with Disabilities

points.title: Since 1969, the Government of India has conferred National Awards on International Day of Persons with Disabilities to recognis…
points.text: 131. Since 1969, the Government of India has conferred National Awards on International Day of Persons with Disabilities to recognise individuals and institutions working for the empowerment of persons with disabilities. These awards aim to encourage leadership, service and public recognition in the disability sector.
points.subsection: D. National Awards for the Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities

points.title: The In-Service Training Scheme, introduced in 2021, aims to sensitise Central and State government functionaries on disability-…
points.text: 132. The In-Service Training Scheme, introduced in 2021, aims to sensitise Central and State government functionaries on disability-related matters so that official conduct better supports enforcement of disability rights.

points.title: The RPwD Act, 2016 and the RPwD Rules, 2017 form the central legal framework for accessibility of public spaces, transport, inf…
points.text: 133. The RPwD Act, 2016 and the RPwD Rules, 2017 form the central legal framework for accessibility of public spaces, transport, information, communication and public services for persons with disabilities.

points.title: Section 40 of the Act requires the Central Government, in consultation with the Chief Commissioner, to formulate rules laying d…
points.text: 134. Section 40 of the Act requires the Central Government, in consultation with the Chief Commissioner, to formulate rules laying down accessibility standards for the physical environment, transportation, information and communications, technologies and systems, and other public facilities and services.

points.title: Section 41 requires appropriate governments to provide access to transport by ensuring accessible facilities at bus stops, rail…
points.text: 135. Section 41 requires appropriate governments to provide access to transport by ensuring accessible facilities at bus stops, railway stations, airports and similar places, including parking, toilets, ticketing counters and machines. It also covers personal mobility assistance and retrofitting of vehicles.

points.title: Section 42 requires measures to make audio, print and electronic content accessible through formats such as audio description,…
points.text: 136. Section 42 requires measures to make audio, print and electronic content accessible through formats such as audio description, sign language interpretation and closed captioning. It also promotes universally designed electronic goods and consumer products.

points.title: Section 44 requires buildings open to the public, whether public or private, to comply with accessibility standards.
points.text: 137. Section 44 requires buildings open to the public, whether public or private, to comply with accessibility standards. Existing buildings were required to be made compliant within the statutory time frame after the rules were notified.

points.title: Section 46 requires service providers, including government and private providers, to comply with accessibility rules within th…
points.text: 138. Section 46 requires service providers, including government and private providers, to comply with accessibility rules within the prescribed time frame.

points.title: Rule 15 of the RPwD Rules, 2017 requires establishments to follow standards for physical environment, transport and information…
points.text: 139. Rule 15 of the RPwD Rules, 2017 requires establishments to follow standards for physical environment, transport and information and communication technology, including the Harmonised Guidelines, the Bus Body Code and the Guidelines for Indian Government Websites.

points.title: Rajive Raturi v.
points.text: 140. Rajive Raturi v. Union of India is a leading accessibility case concerning persons with visual impairments and wider accessibility of roads, transport and public spaces. The proceedings, initiated in 2005, led to directions for accessibility implementation and periodic oversight. The case has become a foundational authority for the right to accessibility in India.

points.title: In Disabled Rights Group and Others v.
points.text: 141. In Disabled Rights Group and Others v. Union of India, the Supreme Court directed the University Grants Commission to consider accessibility guidelines for students with disabilities in higher education institutions. This led to the adoption of Accessibility Guidelines and Standards for Higher Education Institutions and Universities.

points.title: In Pragya Prasun and Others v.
points.text: 142. In Pragya Prasun and Others v. Union of India and Others, the petitioners challenged digital KYC processes that excluded persons with disabilities, including persons with facial disfigurement and low vision. The Supreme Court directed compliance with accessibility obligations and revision of digital KYC processes in line with Web Content Accessibility Guidelines.

points.title: In Gulshan Kumar v.
