points.text: 143. In Gulshan Kumar v. Institute of Banking Personnel Selection and Others, the Supreme Court directed that examination centres must be made accessible to persons with disabilities.

points.title: The Accessible India Campaign, or Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan, was launched in December 2015 as a nationwide initiative for universa…
points.text: 144. The Accessible India Campaign, or Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan, was launched in December 2015 as a nationwide initiative for universal accessibility. It operates under SIPDA and focuses on three domains: built environment, transportation systems and information and communication technologies.

points.title: The 2024-25 Annual Report of the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities records that 1,723 government buildings…
points.text: 145. The 2024-25 Annual Report of the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities records that 1,723 government buildings have been retrofitted. The report further records 648 State and 1,100 Central buildings; this sub-breakdown should be verified because the arithmetic does not match the stated total.

points.title: Transport accessibility progress is recorded across airports, railways, bus stations and public buses.
points.text: 146. Transport accessibility progress is recorded across airports, railways, bus stations and public buses. All 35 international airports and 55 of 69 domestic airports have been made fully accessible. In railways, 709 stations are fully accessible and 4,068 are partially accessible. In bus infrastructure, 3,120 of 3,533 bus stations are accessible. Of 145,747 public buses, 29.05% are partially accessible and 5.96% are fully accessible.

points.title: The Guidelines for Indian Government Websites provide a framework for accessible government websites.
points.text: 147. The Guidelines for Indian Government Websites provide a framework for accessible government websites. Under the Common Minimum Framework, 95 of 100 Central Ministry/Department websites have been made GIGW compliant, and 500 additional Central Government websites are in the process of conversion. At the State level, 676 websites have been changed, of which 476 have gone live.

points.title: The Sugamya Bharat App, launched in 2021, enables users to report accessibility barriers in public infrastructure and provide f…
points.text: 148. The Sugamya Bharat App, launched in 2021, enables users to report accessibility barriers in public infrastructure and provide feedback on accessibility-related challenges.

points.title: Seventeen of twenty Central ministries have notified sector-specific accessibility guidelines or standards under Rule 15 of the…
points.text: 149. Seventeen of twenty Central ministries have notified sector-specific accessibility guidelines or standards under Rule 15 of the 2017 Rules. The Department of Disability Affairs is following up with the remaining ministries for notification and implementation.

points.title: The Government has also supported the development of specialised accessibility courses in B.Tech, B.Plan and B.Arch programmes…
points.text: 150. The Government has also supported the development of specialised accessibility courses in B.Tech, B.Plan and B.Arch programmes in collaboration with IIT Kharagpur.

points.title: This initiative, as with the Accessible India Campaign, falls under the SIPDA and has been in operation since 1999.
points.text: 151. This initiative, as with the Accessible India Campaign, falls under the SIPDA and has been in operation since 1999. It is particularly focused on the accessibility of physical buildings. To that end, the scheme offers financial assistance to make government buildings disability-friendly. State/UT Governments, including autonomous organizations, national institutions, and central or state universities can apply for grants-in-aid in order to fund the retrofitting of their buildings under this scheme.
points.subsection: D. Creation of Barrier-Free Environment for Persons with Disabilities

points.title: Certain measures under the APID scheme may be viewed from the perspective of accessibility as they promote personal mobility.
points.text: 152. Certain measures under the APID scheme may be viewed from the perspective of accessibility as they promote personal mobility. In particular, this scheme provides subsidisation of motorised tricycles and motorised wheelchairs, as well as prosthetics required by persons with locomotor disabilities. At present, over 87,000 motorised tricycles have been distributed under this Scheme.
points.subsection: E. Assistance to Persons with Disabilities for Purchasing/ Fitting of Aids/ Appliances (APID) Scheme

points.title: Meghalya a.
points.text: 153. Meghalya a. Under the Accessible India Campaign, the identified Government buildings have been made accessible as per the Harmonised Guidelines and Space Standards for Barrier Free Built Environment for Persons with Disabilities and Elderly Persons, February 2016, issued by the Ministry of Urban Development Government of India. b. Further, Section E of the Meghalaya Building Bye Laws 2011 categorically provides for particular requirements such as guiding/ warning floor materials, visual signage, site planning, parking, building requirements, etc. c. Out of 24 State government buildings, 23 have been retrofitted to make them more accessible over the period of 2017-2022. Further, 34 State websites have been changed according to the accessibility requirements. d. In order to make transportation more accessible, a concession of 50% off the standard rate is given to persons with disabilities, and two seats are reserved for such persons on all buses. e. All government meetings of the state have included sign language interpretation since 2017. f. The State Government of Meghalaya has also developed the ‘Meghalaya SignBank App’ in order to impart education about what is referred to as Meghalaya Sign Language.

points.title: Chandigarh a.
points.text: 154. Chandigarh a. Accessible parking reserved for PwDs close to building entrances b. Creation of barrier-free entry into buildings, including at least one accessible entrance, ramps, doors large enough to accommodate wheelchairs, guiding patterns on floors for visually impaired persons, and the inclusion of at least one elevator in each building. c. Under the Accessible India Campaign, 41 government buildings thus far have been made fully accessible for persons with disabilities, and 181 government buildings have been equipped with disability-friendly bathrooms. d. The state government has instructed all functionaries to have a sign language interpreter in government meetings.

points.title: Mizoram a.
points.text: 155. Mizoram a. Chapter VI of the Aizwal Municipal Corporation Building Regulation Act, 2012 specifies certain building requirements to make it accessible for PwDs. b. Throughout 2017-2022, 43 buildings of the state government have been retrofitted to make them more accessible for PwDs.

points.title: Uttarakhand a.
points.text: 156. Uttarakhand a. Throughout 2017-2022, 64 government buildings have been retrofitted under the Accessible India Campaign. b. All government meetings require the presence of a sign language interpreter, and there have been schools set up by NGOs to impart the knowledge of Indian Sign Language.

points.title: Uttar Pradesh a.
points.text: 157. Uttar Pradesh a. Out of the 139 buildings identified under the Accessible India Campaign, 98 have been retrofitted using funds from the Central government. Further, 34 out of the 68 buildings identified in the Lucknow district of the State alone have been retrofitted using State funds. At present, the state is actively working on making the rest of the government buildings accessible. b. All UPMRC metro stations in Lucknow, Kanpur, and Agra have been made fully accessible for PwDs and all metro stations in construction are being made with all accessibility features.

points.title: The first Harmonised Guidelines were compiled and issued by the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs in 2016.
points.text: 158. The first Harmonised Guidelines were compiled and issued by the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs in 2016. The latest version of the Harmonised Guidelines was passed in 2021.
points.subsection: G. Harmonised Guidelines and Standards for Universal Accessibility [https://niua.in/intranet/sites/default/files/2262.pdf]

points.title: The Harmonised Guidelines provide an extensive framework for designing physical spaces with a view to achieving universal acces…
points.text: 159. The Harmonised Guidelines provide an extensive framework for designing physical spaces with a view to achieving universal accessibility. The guidelines span across external elements, internal elements, information and wayfinding systems, building typologies, building operations and machines. They also include guidelines on evaluating accessibility.
points.subsection: G. Harmonised Guidelines and Standards for Universal Accessibility [https://niua.in/intranet/sites/default/files/2262.pdf]

points.title: The stated objectives of the Guidelines include sensitisation of diverse stakeholders about the various accessibility needs for…
points.text: 160. The stated objectives of the Guidelines include sensitisation of diverse stakeholders about the various accessibility needs for diverse population groups in the built environment, the introduction of universal design principles to stakeholders for creating inclusive built environments, the recommendation of specific built environment elements with their accessibility attributes and specifications, the development of a holistic approach to accessibility, and guiding the assessment of of accessibility of built environments.
points.subsection: G. Harmonised Guidelines and Standards for Universal Accessibility [https://niua.in/intranet/sites/default/files/2262.pdf]

points.title: The Accessibility Guidelines were published by the University Grants Commission (UGC) in 2022.
points.text: 161. The Accessibility Guidelines were published by the University Grants Commission (UGC) in 2022.
points.subsection: G. Harmonised Guidelines and Standards for Universal Accessibility [https://niua.in/intranet/sites/default/files/2262.pdf]

points.title: These Guidelines pertain specifically to making higher education institutions accessible for persons with disabilities.
points.text: 162. These Guidelines pertain specifically to making higher education institutions accessible for persons with disabilities. To that end, the guidelines encompass various areas of accessibility, such as accessible information, communication, and learning resources, inclusive mobility infrastructure, universal accessibility in built infrastructure, accessible curriculum, teaching, and learning systems, accessible assessment and examination systems, accessibility of resources and services, inclusive campus life, and for the governance and monitoring of accessibility and inclusive practices.
points.subsection: G. Harmonised Guidelines and Standards for Universal Accessibility [https://niua.in/intranet/sites/default/files/2262.pdf]

points.title: The Right to Life is a fundamental right guaranteed to every person under Article 21 of the Constitution, which states that ‘no…
points.text: 163. The Right to Life is a fundamental right guaranteed to every person under Article 21 of the Constitution, which states that ‘no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.’ The Supreme Court has given an expansive and liberal interpretation to this right, consistently holding that the right to life does not entail merely the right to an ‘animal existence,’ but a right to live with dignity and all the concomitant rights. Therefore, while the 2016 Act does not explicitly guarantee the ‘right to life,’ it supplements the right to life under the Constitution by giving substance to it through specialised rights, such as the right to education, employment, etc.

points.title: Article 21 prohibits the arbitrary deprivation of life of all persons, including persons with disabilities.
points.text: 164. Article 21 prohibits the arbitrary deprivation of life of all persons, including persons with disabilities.

points.title: The 2016 Act provides that PwDs shall have ‘equal protection and safety in situations of risk, armed conflict, humanitarian eme…
points.text: 165. The 2016 Act provides that PwDs shall have ‘equal protection and safety in situations of risk, armed conflict, humanitarian emergencies and natural disaster.’ In order to make this right meaningful, the National Disaster Management Authority and State Disaster Management Authorities have been entrusted with the responsibility of ensuring the inclusion of PwDs in their disaster management activities. Furthermore, the District Disaster Management Authorities are obligated to maintain records of the details of all PwDs in their respective districts and take suitable measures to inform such persons of any situations of risk so as to enhance disaster preparedness.
points.article: Article 11: Situations of Risk and Humanitarian Emergencies
points.subsection: A. Incorporation of Disaster Management Act, 2005

points.title: These authorities, namely the National, State and District Disaster Management Authorities, are instituted and governed by the…
points.text: 166. These authorities, namely the National, State and District Disaster Management Authorities, are instituted and governed by the Disaster Management Act, 2005. Every authority is statutorily obligated to create ‘Disaster Management Plans’ for its own jurisdiction.
points.article: Article 11: Situations of Risk and Humanitarian Emergencies
points.subsection: A. Incorporation of Disaster Management Act, 2005

points.title: ‘Disaster management’ has been described as a continuous process which encompasses different stages of disaster management, fro…
points.text: 167. ‘Disaster management’ has been described as a continuous process which encompasses different stages of disaster management, from prevention to prompt response to rehabilitation. The definition of disaster is similarly inclusive, and extends to all situations whether natural or caused by humans, which cause suffering, damage and/ or destruction.
points.article: Article 11: Situations of Risk and Humanitarian Emergencies
points.subsection: A. Incorporation of Disaster Management Act, 2005

points.title: In cases of reconstruction done subsequently to a situation or risk or disaster, the concerned authorities are required to unde…
points.text: 168. In cases of reconstruction done subsequently to a situation or risk or disaster, the concerned authorities are required to undertake such activity in accordance with the accessibility requirements of PwDs.
points.article: Article 11: Situations of Risk and Humanitarian Emergencies
points.subsection: A. Incorporation of Disaster Management Act, 2005

points.title: In order to address the disproportionate impact of the disasters on PwDs, the Government of India has adopted a disabilitiy-inc…
points.text: 169. In order to address the disproportionate impact of the disasters on PwDs, the Government of India has adopted a disabilitiy-inclusive policy in its disaster management practices which is made operational primarily through the National Disaster Management Guidelines on Disability Inclusive Disaster Risk Reduction, 2019 (the ‘2019 Guidelines’). These guidelines have been made with participation from PwDs and their respective organisations and are addressed to stakeholders who are connected to the implementation of disaster risk reduction (DRR) across different jurisdictions.
points.article: Article 11: Situations of Risk and Humanitarian Emergencies
points.subsection: B. Disability Inclusive Disaster Risk Reduction and 2019 Guidelines

points.title: The Guidelines undertake a comprehensive consideration of the risks and barriers which make inclusive management a challenge.
points.text: 170. The Guidelines undertake a comprehensive consideration of the risks and barriers which make inclusive management a challenge. This includes deficient data and resource-mapping, which might effectively exclude a portion of people from receiving aid, the historical lack of input from PwDs in the DRR framework, making DRR technology universal, an adverse social attitude which leads to socio-economic exclusion. In order to overcome these barriers, the report identifies targeted solutions such as preparedness and mitigation strategies, an Early Warning System (EWS), a strong Search, Rescue and Evacuation (SRE) service, ready access to relief, and an inclusive rehabilitation and reconstruction programme. The report further highlights the compounding disadvantages faced by PwDs who are women, children, elderly, or belong to groups such as Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, and the LGBTQ+ community.
points.article: Article 11: Situations of Risk and Humanitarian Emergencies
points.subsection: B. Disability Inclusive Disaster Risk Reduction and 2019 Guidelines

points.title: Furthermore, the Guidelines make instructions as to how this system should be implemented along with the specific functionaries…
points.text: 171. Furthermore, the Guidelines make instructions as to how this system should be implemented along with the specific functionaries which should be responsible for each task. These range from taking steps to better understand disaster risk, strengthening disaster risk management through policy changes and an oversight mechanism, promoting inter-agency coordination, investing in DRR through both non-structural and structural means, working on the capacity development of PwDs, and formulating a strong preparedness and response plan. Throughout this list, specific departments/ functionaries from central, state and local governments have been identified and assigned tasks.
points.article: Article 11: Situations of Risk and Humanitarian Emergencies
points.subsection: B. Disability Inclusive Disaster Risk Reduction and 2019 Guidelines

points.title: The most recent National Disaster Management Plan, released in 2019, dedicates a chapter to social inclusion and draws from the…
points.text: 172. The most recent National Disaster Management Plan, released in 2019, dedicates a chapter to social inclusion and draws from the 2019 Guidelines. The Plan requires Disaster Management Authorities at the state and local level to draw up lists of all PwDs situated in their respective jurisdictions and to update them periodically, to build local support networks for the aid of PwDs in times of emergency, in particular requiring the designation of at least three persons for each PwD, to make special arrangements at disaster-response institutions for the accommodation of PwDs, and create robust systems for early warning and for inclusive rehabilitation. The responsibility of mobilising support of national and international bodies and for the development of universal technology in DRR rests with the National Disaster Management Authority.
points.article: Article 11: Situations of Risk and Humanitarian Emergencies

points.title: States are at different stages of adopting and implementing DMPs which are inclusive of PwDs.
points.text: 173. States are at different stages of adopting and implementing DMPs which are inclusive of PwDs.
points.article: Article 11: Situations of Risk and Humanitarian Emergencies

points.title: Article 14 of the Constitution enshrines the right to equality of all persons before the law and equal protection under the law…
points.text: 174. Article 14 of the Constitution enshrines the right to equality of all persons before the law and equal protection under the law in India, including persons with disabilities. Indian law recognises persons with disabilities as persons before the law and like all other citizens, they have the right to equality under Article 14.
points.article: Article 12: Equal Recognition before the Law
points.subsection: A. Equal Recognition as Persons before the Law

points.title: Section 3 of the 2016 Act states that: ‘the appropriate Government shall ensure that the persons with disabilities enjoy the ri…
points.text: 175. Section 3 of the 2016 Act states that: ‘the appropriate Government shall ensure that the persons with disabilities enjoy the right to equality, life with dignity and respect for his or her integrity equally with others.’
points.article: Article 12: Equal Recognition before the Law
points.subsection: A. Equal Recognition as Persons before the Law

points.title: Section 13 of the 2016 Act provides for the right of persons with disabilities to own or inherit property and control their fin…
points.text: 176. Section 13 of the 2016 Act provides for the right of persons with disabilities to own or inherit property and control their financial affairs. Persons providing support to persons with disabilities are under the duty not to exercise undue influence, and to respect their dignity, autonomy, and privacy. Furthermore, should a conflict of interest arise between a person providing support and a person with disability over any matter, the latter shall have the final decision-making authority in such matter. A person with disability has the right to modify, alter, or dismantle any support arrangement and replace it with another.
points.article: Article 12: Equal Recognition before the Law

points.title: Furthermore, the Act has advanced the ‘limited guardianship’ model for the first time under S.
points.text: 177. Furthermore, the Act has advanced the ‘limited guardianship’ model for the first time under S. 14. Under this section, if a court is of the opinion that a PwD who has adequate support is still unable to make any legal decision, it may appoint a ‘limited guardian’ to such person. This relationship operated under joint decision-making and mutual understanding, is limited to a specific time period and subject matter, and final authority always remains with the PwD. Any PwD aggrieved with the appointment of a limited guardian also has the right to appeal the same.
points.article: Article 12: Equal Recognition before the Law

points.title: The limited guardianship model takes precedence over all other laws existing theretofore, including the guardianship models und…
points.text: 178. The limited guardianship model takes precedence over all other laws existing theretofore, including the guardianship models under the MHA and NTA which operated before.
points.article: Article 12: Equal Recognition before the Law

points.title: In addition to the right to exercise their legal capacity when it comes to financial affairs, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) h…
points.text: 179. In addition to the right to exercise their legal capacity when it comes to financial affairs, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) has endeavoured to make banking facilities accessible for PwDs. To this end, the RBI has issued guidelines in 2017, which make it obligatory for all banks across the nation to dedicate specific help counters for PwDs, streamline certain facilities (e.g. submission of documents, issuance of cheque books, etc.), assist persons who are physically impaired, and endeavour to provide door step banking to those who require it.
points.article: Article 12: Equal Recognition before the Law

points.title: In Pragya Prasun v.
points.text: 180. In Pragya Prasun v. Union of India, the Supreme Court noted that certain Digital Know Your Customer (KYC) processes excluded PwDs and instructed the relevant authorities to issue inclusive digital verification guidelines.
points.article: Article 12: Equal Recognition before the Law

points.title: Article 39A, a directive principle of state policy, provides that: ‘the State shall secure that the operation of the legal syst…
points.text: 181. Article 39A, a directive principle of state policy, provides that: ‘the State shall secure that the operation of the legal system promotes justice, on a basis of equal opportunity, and shall, in particular, provide free legal aid, by suitable legislation or schemes or in any other way, to ensure that opportunities for securing justice are not denied to any citizen by reason of economic or other disabilities.’

points.title: Article 22 (1) provides that any person arrested has the right to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of their choice.
points.text: 182. Article 22 (1) provides that any person arrested has the right to consult and be defended by a legal practitioner of their choice.

points.title: Under section 12 of the 2016 Act, the Government has the responsibility to ensure that PwDs are able to access ‘any court, trib…
points.text: 183. Under section 12 of the 2016 Act, the Government has the responsibility to ensure that PwDs are able to access ‘any court, tribunal, authority, commission or any other body having judicial or quasi-judicial or investigative powers without discrimination on the basis of disability.’ This includes measures to support the exercise of legal rights, a responsibility on the National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) and State Legal Services Authorities (SLSAs) to make provisions for reasonable accommodation to ensure that PwDs can access schemes, programmes and services, and making government services and processes accessible and inclusive.

points.title: Under Section 84 of the Act, the government must create or designate a particular court in each district for the trial of offen…
points.text: 184. Under Section 84 of the Act, the government must create or designate a particular court in each district for the trial of offences contained in the act so that speedy trial is ensured. Data collected shows that states have been largely compliant and designated existing courts for the purpose.

points.title: Furthermore, Section 27 of the MHA provides that a person with mental illnesses has the right to free legal services to exercis…
points.text: 185. Furthermore, Section 27 of the MHA provides that a person with mental illnesses has the right to free legal services to exercise any of the rights under the Act.

points.title: Under S.
points.text: 186. Under S. 12 (d) of the Legal Services Authorities (LSA) Act 1987, PwDs are entitled to free legal aid whenever they have to file or defend a case, as long as a prima facie case is made out. The authority providing this aid is the NALSA and SLSAs In 2024, NALSA released the NALSA (Legal Services to the Mentally Ill and Persons with Intellectual Disabilities) Scheme, which replaces its 2015 counterpart. This scheme is specifically meant to implement Article 13 of the UNCRPD, as is mentioned in its introduction.

points.title: Under the 2024 Scheme, each SLSA is responsible for setting up Legal Services Units for PwDs, known as Manonyays (LSUM), in eac…
points.text: 187. Under the 2024 Scheme, each SLSA is responsible for setting up Legal Services Units for PwDs, known as Manonyays (LSUM), in each district. Each of the LSUMs has at least six panel lawyers and ten para-legal volunteers. Persons who have experience in dealing with PwDs are given preference.

points.title: The appropriate District Legal Service Authority (DLSA) is responsible for conducting regular sensitisations at each LSUM (i.e.
points.text: 188. The appropriate District Legal Service Authority (DLSA) is responsible for conducting regular sensitisations at each LSUM (i.e. quarterly). The DLSA and LSUM together are also responsible for conducting awareness and outreach programmes in their respective jurisdictions. LSUMs are also meant to conduct legal services camps at various locations.

points.title: The 2024 Scheme also provides detailed guidelines to professionals involved in providing legal aid.
points.text: 189. The 2024 Scheme also provides detailed guidelines to professionals involved in providing legal aid.

points.title: Various provisions of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS), 2023, contain special provisions for persons with mental a…
points.text: 190. Various provisions of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS), 2023, contain special provisions for persons with mental and/ or physical disability. Under Sections 173, 179, and 195, the police cannot compel any PwD that they wish to examine as a witness to attend the police station and must instead go to them. Under Section 54, if any person arrested has a mental or physical disability, their identification by any witnesses must be done in the presence of a magistrate in a manner that they are comfortable with. Under Section 357, if a person does not understand the proceedings, though not because of mental illness, a court may proceed but must report its findings to the respective High Court which will give the final decision.
points.subsection: D. Procedural Accommodation to Protect PwDs

points.title: Under Section 367, if an accused person is found to have a mental illness, then the magistrate handling the case must assess wh…
points.text: 191. Under Section 367, if an accused person is found to have a mental illness, then the magistrate handling the case must assess whether such illness incapacitates them from entering into defence. If so, they must abstain from interrogating the accused but may hear submissions from legal representatives and acquit the accused if no prima facie case is made out. Otherwise, the magistrate must put a hold on the trial until the accused is in a position to enter defence.
points.subsection: D. Procedural Accommodation to Protect PwDs

points.title: A person with mental illness has the right to automatic bail, regardless of the offence, under Section 369.
points.text: 192. A person with mental illness has the right to automatic bail, regardless of the offence, under Section 369. Where a person is not let out on bail, they must be kept in a place where regular psychiatric treatment can be given.
points.subsection: D. Procedural Accommodation to Protect PwDs

points.title: Article 21 of the Constitution of India guarantees the Right to Life and states that ‘no person shall be deprived of his life o…
points.text: 193. Article 21 of the Constitution of India guarantees the Right to Life and states that ‘no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.’ In India, this right has been given an expansive interpretation by the Supreme Court and encompasses concomitant rights which are foundational to living a life with dignity and respect. All persons in India are entitled to their liberty and security.
points.article: Article 14: Liberty and Security of the Person

points.title: Further, Article 14 of the Constitution, which enshrines the Right to Equality before Law, states that ‘the State shall not den…
points.text: 194. Further, Article 14 of the Constitution, which enshrines the Right to Equality before Law, states that ‘the State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India.’ The Supreme Court has incorporated the doctrine of non-arbitrariness into this right, meaning that no person can be arbitrarily deprived of their fundamental rights.
points.article: Article 14: Liberty and Security of the Person

points.title: The Constitution provides a strong framework for the protection of the liberty of all citizens of India, including PwDs.
points.text: 195. The Constitution provides a strong framework for the protection of the liberty of all citizens of India, including PwDs. They have the right to life and liberty as do all other citizens and are protected by its arbitrary or discriminatory deprivation on the basis of their disability by virtue of the combined effect of the two aforementioned Constitutional provisions.
points.article: Article 14: Liberty and Security of the Person

points.title: If they are aggrieved by any action perceived to be a violation of their liberty, PwDs have the right to move the Supreme Court…
points.text: 196. If they are aggrieved by any action perceived to be a violation of their liberty, PwDs have the right to move the Supreme Court and/ or their jurisdictional High Court under Article 32 and Article 226 of the Constitution respectively.
points.article: Article 14: Liberty and Security of the Person

points.title: In order to protect the right of PwDs who come into contact with the criminal justice system, the Code of Criminal Procedure (C…
points.text: 197. In order to protect the right of PwDs who come into contact with the criminal justice system, the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC) — now the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS) — was amended (with the amendment coming into force in 2009) to provide safeguards to persons suffering from mental disability or mental retardation.
points.article: Article 14: Liberty and Security of the Person

points.title: In particular, if an accused person is of unsound mind or suffers from mental retardation and this fact is ensured by a civil s…
points.text: 198. In particular, if an accused person is of unsound mind or suffers from mental retardation and this fact is ensured by a civil surgeon, the magistrate can immediately discharge the accused if they find no prima facie case is made out against them. However, if a case is made out but the person is rendered incapable of making their defence due to unsoundness of mind or mental retardation, then the magistrate must postpone the proceeding for the period which is required for the treatment of the accused in the opinion of the surgeon.
points.article: Article 14: Liberty and Security of the Person

points.title: In order to facilitate this safeguard, the magistrate is given liberal powers to set bail in such cases, as long as the person…
points.text: 199. In order to facilitate this safeguard, the magistrate is given liberal powers to set bail in such cases, as long as the person is being sent for treatment. Furthermore, if a person is found guilty of a crime but has been acquitted solely on the ground of unsoundness of mind, they may direct that person to be detained in safe custody or kept under the supervision of a friend or relative.
points.article: Article 14: Liberty and Security of the Person

points.title: The Right to Life under Article 21 of the Constitution has been interpreted by the Supreme Court to include the right against t…
points.text: 200. The Right to Life under Article 21 of the Constitution has been interpreted by the Supreme Court to include the right against torture, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment. This right is an absolute one, meaning that any punishment or treatment deemed to be cruel, inhuman or degrading is not allowed at all and cannot be justified by any means. This is consistent with India’s commitments as a signatory to the Convention against Torture.
points.article: Article 15: Freedom from torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment

points.title: Like all other citizens of India, PwDs are constitutionally entitled to freedom from any form or torture and inhuman or degradi…
points.text: 201. Like all other citizens of India, PwDs are constitutionally entitled to freedom from any form or torture and inhuman or degrading treatment.
points.article: Article 15: Freedom from torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment

points.title: Section 81 of the MHA lays down that no mentally ill person shall be subjected during treatment to any indignity (whether physi…
points.text: 202. Section 81 of the MHA lays down that no mentally ill person shall be subjected during treatment to any indignity (whether physical or mental) or cruelty. Section 17 of NTA read with Rule 17 to the National Trust Rules, 2000 defines various acts of abuse and neglect on the part of the guardian of a PwD (this includes acts such as solitary confinement, chaining, beating and sexual abuse). These acts have been made punishable by removal of the guardian from the care of the patient.
points.article: Article 15: Freedom from torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment

points.title: Furthermore, in the 2016 Act, committing atrocities against PwDs has become a criminal offence punishable by a term which may e…
points.text: 203. Furthermore, in the 2016 Act, committing atrocities against PwDs has become a criminal offence punishable by a term which may extend up to five years along with a fine. This ensures that not only do PwDs remain safe from acts constituting torture and cruel or inhuman treatment from the state, but also from private individuals.
points.article: Article 15: Freedom from torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment

points.title: Article 21, the Right to Life, serves as the jurisprudential foundation for the prevention of exploitation of, violence against…
points.text: 204. Article 21, the Right to Life, serves as the jurisprudential foundation for the prevention of exploitation of, violence against and abuse of all persons, including PwDs. Article 23 prohibits human trafficking and forced labour, and Article 24 prohibits child labour — both of which are equally applicable to PwDs.
points.article: Article 16: Freedom from Exploitation, Violence and Abuse

points.title: Under Section 7 of the 2016 Act, the Government must take measures to protect PwDs from all forms of abuse, violence, and explo…
points.text: 205. Under Section 7 of the 2016 Act, the Government must take measures to protect PwDs from all forms of abuse, violence, and exploitation. Any person or organisation which has reason to believe that any such act is being committed against PwDs may report it to a magistrate or to the police, who can investigate the matter. They also have the duty to inform the person of their legal rights, access to legal aid, etc.
points.article: Article 16: Freedom from Exploitation, Violence and Abuse

points.title: Furthermore, the Act has created the following new offences to deter violence against PwDs, all of which are punishable by a mi…
points.text: 206. Furthermore, the Act has created the following new offences to deter violence against PwDs, all of which are punishable by a minimum of six months up to five years along with a fine: a. intentionally insults or intimidates with intent to humiliate a person with disability in any place within public view; b. assaults or uses force to any person with disability with intent to dishonour him or outrage the modesty of a woman with disability; c. having the actual charge or control over a person with disability voluntarily or knowingly denies food or fluids to him or her; d. being in a position to dominate the will of a child or woman with disability and uses that position to exploit her sexually; e. voluntarily injures, damages or interferes with the use of any limb or sense or any supporting device of a person with disability; f. performs, conducts or directs any medical procedure to be performed on a woman with disability which leads to or is likely to lead to termination of pregnancy without her express consent except in cases where medical procedure for termination of pregnancy is done in severe cases of disability and with the opinion of a registered medical practitioner and also with the consent of the guardian of the woman with disability
points.article: Article 16: Freedom from Exploitation, Violence and Abuse

points.title: The Domestic Violence (DV) Act, 2005, which protects ‘victims of violence of any kind occurring within the family’ extends to a…
points.text: 207. The Domestic Violence (DV) Act, 2005, which protects ‘victims of violence of any kind occurring within the family’ extends to all persons, including PwDs. In order to protect victims, the Act makes provisions for shelter homes, medical facilities, and protection orders. All of these remedies are equally available to PwDs.
points.article: Article 16: Freedom from Exploitation, Violence and Abuse

points.title: The Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012, is applicable to all persons below the age of eighteen, incl…
points.text: 208. The Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012, is applicable to all persons below the age of eighteen, including PwDs. The Act categorises any act of non-penetrative or penetrative sexual assault by taking advantage of a person’s mental or physical disability as aggravated sexual assault or penetrative sexual assault, which each carry a higher punishment than their non-aggaragtive counterparts. It further provides that magistrates may take the assistance of experts and special educators in the course of dealing with children with disabilities.
points.article: Article 16: Freedom from Exploitation, Violence and Abuse

points.title: In the case of Patan Jamal Vali v.
points.text: 209. In the case of Patan Jamal Vali v. State of Andhra Pradesh, the Supreme Court issued the following directions in order to protect PwDs who are part of the criminal justice system: a. The National Judicial Academy and state judicial academies are requested to sensitize trial and appellate judges to deal with cases involving survivors of sexual abuse. This training should acquaint judges with the special provisions, concerning such survivors, such as those outlined above. It should also cover guidance on the legal weight to be attached to the testimony of such witnesses/survivors, consistent with our holding above. Public prosecutors and standing counsel should also undergo similar training in this regard. The Bar Council of India can consider introducing courses in the LL.B program that cover these topics and the intersectional nature of violence more generally; b. Trained special educators and interpreters must be appointed to ensure the effective realization of the reasonable accommodations embodied in the Criminal Law Amendment Act, 2013. All police stations should maintain a database of such educators, interpreters and legal aid providers, in order to facilitate easy access and coordination; c. The National Crimes Record Bureau should seriously consider the possibility of maintaining disaggregated data on gender-based violence. Disability must be one of the variables on the basis of which such data must be maintained so that the scale of the problem can be mapped out and tailored remedial action can be taken; d. Police officers should be provided sensitization, on a regular basis, to deal with cases of sexual violence against women with disabilities, in an appropriate way. The training should cover the full life cycle of a case involving a disabled survivor, from enabling them to register complaints, obtain necessary accommodations, medical attention and suitable legal representation. This training should emphasize the importance of interacting directly with the disabled person concerned, as opposed to their care-taker or helper, in recognition of their agency; and e. Awareness-raising campaigns must be conducted, in accessible formats, to inform women and girls with disabilities
points.article: Article 16: Freedom from Exploitation, Violence and Abuse

points.title: Article 21 and Article 14 read together form the legal foundation for the protection of mental and physical integrity of all pe…
points.text: 210. Article 21 and Article 14 read together form the legal foundation for the protection of mental and physical integrity of all persons, including PwDs.
points.article: Article 17: Protecting the Integrity of the Person

points.title: The 2016 Act provides in S.
points.text: 211. The 2016 Act provides in S. 6 (2) that no PwD shall be the subject of any research without their free and informed consent obtained through an accessible means of communication and a Committee for Research on Disability which shall be made up of at least 50% of PwDs.
points.article: Article 17: Protecting the Integrity of the Person

points.title: Furthermore, under Section 10, it is incumbent upon the appropriate government to ensure that PwDs have access to information r…
points.text: 212. Furthermore, under Section 10, it is incumbent upon the appropriate government to ensure that PwDs have access to information regarding reproductive and family planning, and that no person is subject to any medical treatment which leads to their infertility without their free and informed consent.
points.article: Article 17: Protecting the Integrity of the Person

points.title: The Act makes it a punishable offence to conduct any medical treatment which leads to or is likely to lead to termination of pr…
points.text: 213. The Act makes it a punishable offence to conduct any medical treatment which leads to or is likely to lead to termination of pregnancy without the woman’s express consent under S. 92 (f).
points.article: Article 17: Protecting the Integrity of the Person

points.title: Article 19 (1) (d) and (e) guarantee the right to move freely throughout the territory of India and to reside and settle in any…
points.text: 214. Article 19 (1) (d) and (e) guarantee the right to move freely throughout the territory of India and to reside and settle in any part of the territory of India respectively. This right may be ‘reasonably restricted’ in the interests of the general public or for the protection of Scheduled Tribes.
points.article: Article 18: Liberty of Movement and Nationality

points.title: The Passports Act, 1967, governs matters relating to issuance of passports and transnational travel by all citizens of India, i…
points.text: 215. The Passports Act, 1967, governs matters relating to issuance of passports and transnational travel by all citizens of India, including PwDs. Section 6 of the Act lists the grounds on which issuance of passport may be denied to a person, and disability or any related factor is not contained within this list. To make the process of obtaining a passport more accessible, Passport Seva Kendras (PSKs) contain dedicated counters for helping PwDs in the application process. It is also possible to make appointments online.
points.article: Article 18: Liberty of Movement and Nationality

points.title: All persons born in India are registered under the Registration of Births and Deaths Act, 1969, including PwDs.
points.text: 216. All persons born in India are registered under the Registration of Births and Deaths Act, 1969, including PwDs.
points.article: Article 18: Liberty of Movement and Nationality

points.title: The Supreme Court of India has held that the right to shelter or adequate housing in a place of one’s choice is a fundamental h…
points.text: 217. The Supreme Court of India has held that the right to shelter or adequate housing in a place of one’s choice is a fundamental human right emanating from Article 21 of the Constitution which guarantees the right to life for all. ‘Adequate shelter’ was widely interpreted to include “not just a roof over one’s head but right to all the infrastructure, necessary to enable him to live and develop and develop as a human being”. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act 2016) further strengthened this interpretation by explicitly recognizing the right to live in the community under Section 16, with equal choices as others, and mandating that persons with disabilities shall not be obligated to live in any particular living arrangement. In Rajive Raturi v. Union of India, the Supreme Court explicitly ruled that Article 19 of the CRPD requires the state to take “positive steps to ensure that individuals can enjoy their rights fully, irrespective of disabilities”, extending the right to dignity and the right to a meaningful life under Article 21 to PWDs so as to allow them to enjoy the same freedoms and choices as others. In Akshat Baldwa & Ors. v Yash Raj Films & Ors., the Bombay High Court emphasized the entertainment industry’s obligation to ensure accessibility, directing cinema theatres to provide assistive devices and infrastructure for persons with visual and hearing impairments, recognizing access to cultural and recreational activities as a fundamental right.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community

points.title: Ensuring of public safety or maintenance of public order is upheld as a reasonable restriction on the right to residence under…
points.text: 218. Ensuring of public safety or maintenance of public order is upheld as a reasonable restriction on the right to residence under Article 19(1)(e) of the Constitution. The mandate of Article 19 UNCRPD is reinforced by the aforementioned constitutional jurisprudence, insofar as it recognizes the individual’s right to live at a place of his or her choice. The RPwD Act 2016 aligns with this by prohibiting any form of forced institutionalization and requiring that any restrictions on liberty be in accordance with the law and not based solely on the presence of disability (Section 16(2)). Regarding mandates for accessibility in housing, the Delhi High Court examined whether mandatory retrofitting applies to private residential complexes. The court found that current law confines mandatory accessibility obligations (Sections 44 to 46 of the RPwD Act, 2016) only to “public buildings,” meaning that retrofitting in private housing remains voluntary in India.16
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: B. Reasonable restrictions on the Right to Residence

points.title: Though PwDs in India primarily reside with their families, however, various factors contribute to their institutionalisation.
points.text: 219. Though PwDs in India primarily reside with their families, however, various factors contribute to their institutionalisation. The various parliamentary and court committees examining the conditions of psychiatric and other institutions found that inmates who were staying in institutions because of their families had failed to come back to them. The right to community life has been enshrined in the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (RPwD) Act, 2016, which states that persons with disabilities (PwDs) have the right to live in the community and should not be obligated to live in any particular arrangement. The Government is mandated to strive to provide PwDs with access to a range of in-house, residential, and other community support services, including personal assistance, respecting age and gender. The RPwD Act 2016 has fundamentally shifted the policy framework from the mixed institutional-community model of the earlier PWDA (1995) to a community-based, rights-centred approach. Section 16 of the RPwD Act 2016 explicitly mandates that persons with disabilities have the right to live independently and be included in the community, with choices equal to others. The Act requires the appropriate Government to take effective measures to facilitate persons with disabilities to (i) choose their place of residence and where and with whom they live on an equal basis with others and not be obliged to live in a particular living arrangement; (ii) have access to a range of in-home, residential and other community support services, including personal assistance necessary to support living and inclusion in the community, and to prevent isolation or segregation from the community, and; (iii) have community services and facilities available on an equal basis to persons with disabilities and responsive to their needs.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: C. Law and Policy Initiatives for Community Living

points.title: The Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY) provides 3% reservation for persons with disabilities in both rural (PMAY-Gramin) and urb…
points.text: 220. The Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY) provides 3% reservation for persons with disabilities in both rural (PMAY-Gramin) and urban (PMAY-Urban) components. PWDs and senior citizens are given priority in ground floor allotments. The Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan) launched in 2015 aims to achieve universal accessibility for persons with disabilities including built environments and housing. Under the PMAY-Gramin, State/UTs are urged to ensure that 5% of beneficiaries are PwDs, prioritizing women with benchmark disabilities. Furthermore, households containing any PwDs, and having no able-bodied adult member, are accorded an additional deprivation score to ensure priority during housing allotment.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: C. Law and Policy Initiatives for Community Living

points.title: The RPwD Act tasks the Central Government with formulating rules laying down the standards of accessibility for the physical en…
points.text: 221. The RPwD Act tasks the Central Government with formulating rules laying down the standards of accessibility for the physical environment, transportation, information and communications, and other facilities and services provided to the public in urban and rural areas. In addition to this, the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA) issued Model Building Bye-laws 2016 (including Chapter 8 for PwDs, the Elderly, and Children) and the Harmonised Guidelines and Standards for Universal Accessibility in India 2021. Under the Accessible India Campaign (AIC), accessibility retrofitting has been undertaken in government buildings. Official statistics indicate government buildings retrofitted number 41 in Chandigarh, 23 (out of 24 total) in Meghalaya, 43 in Mizoram, and 64 in Uttarakhand. States have issued accessible design standards, which cover dimensions and specifications for ramps, corridors, lifts suitable for wheelchair users, and accessible toilets. In the state of Chandigarh, 82.75% of schools have disabled- friendly toilets.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: C. Law and Policy Initiatives for Community Living

points.title: Higher Educational Institutions (HEIs) are explicitly required to end all forms of institutionalisation based on disability.
points.text: 222. Higher Educational Institutions (HEIs) are explicitly required to end all forms of institutionalisation based on disability. HEIs must maintain a cohesive inclusive environment where persons with disabilities (PwDs) are treated at par with all other individuals. The Right to Free and Compulsory Education (RTE) Act 2009, through an amendment made in 2012, explicitly allows children with severe and multiple disabilities the right to opt for home-based education.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: C. Law and Policy Initiatives for Community Living

points.title: The RPwD Act obligates the Government to take suitable measures to ensure access to transport.
points.text: 223. The RPwD Act obligates the Government to take suitable measures to ensure access to transport. The Delhi High Court has held that the procurement of standard floor buses is precluded by law, since the right to accessible transport facilities must be considered, meaning accessible, low-floor buses must be made available. The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) has guidelines on Disability-Inclusive Disaster Risk Reduction (2019). The RPwD Act, 2016 further requires NDMA and State/District DMAs to include PwDs in their activities, ensuring they have equal protection and safety in situations of risk. PwDs are given priority in emergencies in Uttarakhand, a state prone to natural disasters such as landslides and flash floods.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: C. Law and Policy Initiatives for Community Living

points.title: The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) formulated the Indian Accessibility Standard(s) for ICT Products…
points.text: 224. The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) formulated the Indian Accessibility Standard(s) for ICT Products and Services (IS 17802) to ensure digital platforms and public information are available in accessible formats. The SC directed the creation of inclusive digital KYC guidelines in line with universal design to prevent marginalization in financial services. As for cultural inclusion, Chandigarh’s Government Museum and Art Gallery designated ‘touch and feel’ items and uses descriptive labels in Braille. The right to access tourist places is recognized.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: C. Law and Policy Initiatives for Community Living

points.title: Chapter V of the RPwD Act provides comprehensive provisions for community rehabilitation.
points.text: 225. Chapter V of the RPwD Act provides comprehensive provisions for community rehabilitation. Section 27 requires establishment of comprehensive rehabilitation services in all districts, particularly in rural and remote areas. Section 28 mandates research in rehabilitation, and other issues necessary for empowering PwDs, which would include assistive devices, and technologies. Section 29 specifically addresses the promotion of sports, recreation and cultural life for PwDs.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community

points.title: The RPwD Act, 2016 specifically allows the appropriate Government and local authorities to grant financial assistance to non-Go…
points.text: 226. The RPwD Act, 2016 specifically allows the appropriate Government and local authorities to grant financial assistance to non-Governmental Organisations (NGOs) for the purposes of carrying out rehabilitation services and programmes. The Government may grant financial assistance to registered institutions to provide services and implement schemes in pursuance of the Act. This has been implemented in certain states. For instance, the Directorate of Social Welfare in Andaman & Nicobar Islands and the Director, Social Welfare in Meghalaya are notified as the competent authorities for the registration of institutions (NGOs/voluntary organizations) working in the disability sector under Section 49 of the RPwD Act, 2016. The state of Meghalaya provided between 9.30 lakhs and 30.00 lakhs annually as “Grant-in-aid to Voluntary Organisations” between 2017-2022.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community

points.title: As of 2024, there are over 300 DDRCs operational across India.
points.text: 227. As of 2024, there are over 300 DDRCs operational across India. The state of Mizoram established a DDRC in 2014, with total expenditure of Rs. 60 lakhs dedicated to the establishment, the yearly operating costs of which are approximately Rs. 56,000. The state of Uttarakhand also spent significant amounts on DDRCs, including Rs. 39.7 lakh in 2019-20. The state of Meghalaya’s District Resource Centre on Disability Affairs conducts sensitization training for on-ground social workers (ASHA/Aanganwadi) on the identification, rehabilitation, and causes of disabilities. Further, states regularly conduct training sessions for such Aanganwadi workers.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community

points.title: The National Career Service Centres for Differently Abled (NCSCS-DA) provide vocational guidance, career counselling, non-forma…
points.text: 228. The National Career Service Centres for Differently Abled (NCSCS-DA) provide vocational guidance, career counselling, non-formal vocational training, and referral services. Schemes such as RSETI (Rural Self Employment Training Institutes) provide training and settlement assistance to PwD candidates.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community

points.title: The Deendayal Disabled Rehabilitation Scheme (DDRS) provides financial assistance to NGOs for running rehabilitation service de…
points.text: 229. The Deendayal Disabled Rehabilitation Scheme (DDRS) provides financial assistance to NGOs for running rehabilitation service delivery centres including CBR programs. In Uttarakhand, NGOs receive grants under the DDRS Scheme. For example, in 2017-18, the total grant provided to four listed NGOs was over 117.77 lakhs. CBR India Network is a part of Asia Pacific CBR Network an initiative of World Health Organization (WHO). It has been formed to support the development and dissemination of CBR as a strategy for inclusive development to promote the needs and rights of every PwD in India, especially children, women and persons from remote areas of India. The CBR framework has been further strengthened through the National Action Plan for implementation of the RPwD Act 2016, which emphasizes community-based inclusive development (CBID) as an evolved concept beyond traditional CBR. The manner in which CBR can contribute in the realization of the CRPD was discussed at the First World Congress on CBR in November, 2012.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community

points.title: The State has a legal obligation under the RPwD Act, 2016 and the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, to ensure support, treatment, an…
points.text: 230. The State has a legal obligation under the RPwD Act, 2016 and the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, to ensure support, treatment, and accommodation for persons with mental disabilities, especially when families are unable to cope, upholding the state’s “parens patriae” duty.17
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: E. Mental Health and Community Living

points.title: The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, which came into force on May 29, 2018, has reformed mental health services in India with impli…
points.text: 231. The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, which came into force on May 29, 2018, has reformed mental health services in India with implications for community living. Section 18 of the Act guarantees the right of persons with mental illness to live in, be part of and not be segregated from society. Section 19 provides the right to live with and be a part of the family. Section 20 establishes the right to community living, mandating that every person with mental illness shall have a right to live in and be part of the community and shall not be segregated or isolated from society merely on the ground of their mental illness. The Act also prohibits the use of chaining or other cruel, inhuman and degrading treatment (Section 103) and mandates deinstitutionalization through supported living arrangements.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: E. Mental Health and Community Living

points.title: This has been implemented through delegated legislation at both the central and state level.
points.text: 232. This has been implemented through delegated legislation at both the central and state level. For instance, every insurer must provide for medical insurance for the treatment of mental illness on the same basis as is available for physical illness. Health insurers are mandated to design specific health insurance products for persons with mental illness. National Disaster Management Guidelines on Mental Health and Psychosocial Support Services (MHPSS) specify that services must be accessible to all, including PwDs. Facilities must undergo accessibility audits to eliminate physical, communication, and service-related barriers. The RPwD Act, in Section 24, provides for a care-giver allowance to persons with disabilities with high support needs. The state of Chandigarh has constituted a specialized legal services unit called Manonyay (LSUM) for Persons with Mental Illness and Intellectual Disabilities.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: E. Mental Health and Community Living

points.title: Section 13 of the RPwD Act 2016 introduced the concept of limited guardianship, obligating the Government to ensure that PwDs h…
points.text: 233. Section 13 of the RPwD Act 2016 introduced the concept of limited guardianship, obligating the Government to ensure that PwDs have the right, equally with others, to own or inherit property, control their financial affairs and have access to bank loans, mortgages and other forms of financial credit. PwDs who are unable to take legally binding decisions despite adequate support may be provided the support of a limited guardian. Limited guardianship is defined as a system of joint decision operating on mutual understanding and trust, limited to a specific period and situation, and must operate in accordance with the will of the person with disability. The State Government of Meghalaya has notified the Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities as the designated authority to provide support of a limited guardian to take legally binding decisions. Similarly, Mizoram and Chandigarh have designated an authority for limited guardianship. This represents a shift from the plenary guardianship model and aligns with Article 12 of the CRPD.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: F. Legal Capacity and Decision-Making Support

points.title: The 2016 Act mandates that persons with disabilities shall have the right to equal recognition before law and shall enjoy legal…
points.text: 234. The 2016 Act mandates that persons with disabilities shall have the right to equal recognition before law and shall enjoy legal capacity on an equal basis with others in all aspects of life. Where a person with disability has limited capacity, they shall be provided support of a limited guardian to exercise legal capacity, appointed by the District Court upon application. Where the RPwD Act, 2016 did not cover a person (e.g., one in a permanent vegetative state), the court may invoke its parens patriae jurisdiction to appoint a legal guardian to protect the person’s life, dignity, and property.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: F. Legal Capacity and Decision-Making Support

points.title: Legal Services Authorities (SLSA/DLSA) must provide free legal aid, assistance, and advice to all PwDs.
points.text: 235. Legal Services Authorities (SLSA/DLSA) must provide free legal aid, assistance, and advice to all PwDs. Chandigarh’s SLSA provided free legal aid to 89 disabled persons between 2017 and 2022. Mizoram reported 5127 beneficiaries under its legal aid schemes for PwDs and mentally ill/disabled persons.
points.article: Article 19: Living Independently and Being Included in the Community
points.subsection: F. Legal Capacity and Decision-Making Support

points.title: Article 19 (1) (c) of the Constitution of India recognizes that all citizens have the freedom of movement throughout the territ…
points.text: 236. Article 19 (1) (c) of the Constitution of India recognizes that all citizens have the freedom of movement throughout the territory of India. Under Section 40 of the RPwD Act, the Central Government is responsible for formulating rules setting the standards of accessibility for transportation. Under Section 40, the Central Government is mandated to formulate rules setting the standards of accessibility for the physical environment, transportation, and related systems in urban and rural areas. The Act specifies that “transportation systems” broadly include road, rail, air, and water transport, as well as para transit systems for last-mile connectivity and road/street infrastructure. Section 41 mandates the appropriate Government to take suitable measures to ensure accessibility, specifically by providing facilities for PwDs at transport hubs (including parking, toilets, ticketing counters, and machines) and ensuring that all modes of transport conform to design standards, including retrofitting existing modes where feasible.
points.subsection: A. Constitutional Jurisprudence and Legal Provisions

points.title: Section 41(2) obligates the appropriate Government to develop schemes promoting personal mobility at affordable cost, which inv…
points.text: 237. Section 41(2) obligates the appropriate Government to develop schemes promoting personal mobility at affordable cost, which involves providing incentives, concessions, retrofitting of vehicles, and personal mobility assistance. Separately, the law requires observance of accessibility norms for infrastructure development, requiring that no new establishment can be built without adhering to accessibility rules (Section 44). The appropriate Government is obligated under Section 43 to promote the development, production, and distribution of universally-designed consumer products. Courts have interpreted the principle of non-discrimination to mandate that transport allowances for employees with speech and hearing impairment must be at par with those with locomotory impairments, who may have added difficulty in personal mobility. Courts have upheld the right to travel safely and independently and the right to have access to adequate transport facilities as fundamental rights flowing from Article 21 read with Article 19(1) (d) and (e) of the Constitution.18 Even within infrastructure in educational institutions, specific mobility standards are mandated, such as providing ramps with a minimum width of 1.5 meters along with handrails and ensuring adequate space (1.2 meters) for wheelchair movement in areas like classrooms and toilets.
points.subsection: A. Constitutional Jurisprudence and Legal Provisions

points.title: Section 43 of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 requires the appropriate government to ensure accessible transp…
points.text: 238. Section 43 of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 requires the appropriate government to ensure accessible transportation and mobility for persons with disabilities. It mandates providing accessible facilities at bus stops, railway stations, and airports; ensuring all modes of transport meet prescribed accessibility standards, including retrofitting older vehicles where feasible; developing schemes to promote affordable personal mobility through incentives, concessions, and assistive devices; and ensuring roads are designed and maintained to support the mobility needs of persons with disabilities.

points.title: Under Section 41(1) of the RPwD Act, 2016, the government must ensure barrier-free access in all modes of transport.
points.text: 239. Under Section 41(1) of the RPwD Act, 2016, the government must ensure barrier-free access in all modes of transport. Following this, the Indian Railways provides extensive concessions and accessibility measures for persons with benchmark disabilities (40% and above), including 50% to 75% fare concessions across all classes. Additional measures include priority lower berth allotment, wheelchair assistance, accessible toilets, tactile tiles, Braille signage, ramps, elevators, accessible ticket counters, Braille route maps, and audio-visual announcement systems at major stations, all aligned with the 2021 Harmonized Guidelines for Universal Accessibility. These benefits are available upon production of a valid disability certificate or UDID card, with concessions varying by disability type and travel class. Many states now provide free or subsidized bus travel. For example, Meghalaya offers 50% fare concessions and reserved seating for PwDs, and have introduced low-floor, ramp- or lift-equipped buses under the Accessible India Campaign and Smart Cities Mission. The Delhi High Court has intervened to ensure accessible public transport is prioritized, observing that authorities are precluded from procuring standard-floor buses in favour of accessible low-floor buses to further accessible transport facilities. Additionally, buses are being equipped with priority seating and wheelchair spaces, while the Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act, 2019 strengthens accessibility by officially recognising ‘adapted vehicles’ used by PwDs. Bus stops across India are also being retrofitted with accessible features such as tactile paving, improved seating, and clear information displays.

points.title: India has issued orders to grant a transport allowance to visually and orthopedically impaired which shall be at double the rat…
points.text: 240. India has issued orders to grant a transport allowance to visually and orthopedically impaired which shall be at double the rate of the allowance, provided to the non-disabled employees of the organization. Various State Governments also provide conveyance allowance to employees who are PwDs. Transport allowance is now available to employees with benchmark disabilities across all disability categories as per the RPwD Act 2016, and not just to those with visual and orthopaedic impairments. Several state governments and Union Territories have implemented transport/escort allowance schemes for students with disabilities. For example, the Andaman and Nicobar Education Department provides transport/escort allowance to eligible Children with Special Needs (CwSN) students. Chandigarh’s Education Department has reported transport allowance expenditure of ₹3.50 lakhs for 140 beneficiaries in 2017-18, ₹3.194 lakhs for 123 beneficiaries in 2018-19, ₹4.06 lakhs for 122 beneficiaries in 2019-20, and ₹0.542 lakhs for 14 beneficiaries in 2021-22. Goa’s Education Department makes transportation facilities available by providing Transport Allowance and Escort Allowance to all students with disability in the State, along with school bus facilities. Mizoram’s School Education department provided transportation facilities for students with special needs amounting to ₹152.4618 lakhs during 2017-2022. Additionally, Uttarakhand’s Samagra Shiksha provides escort facilities for children having profound disability to ensure regular attendance.

points.title: The income ceiling and subsidy provisions have been revised in various schemes.
points.text: 241. The income ceiling and subsidy provisions have been revised in various schemes. For instance, under the scheme for purchase and fitting of aids and appliances, persons with monthly income up to INR 20,000 (approximately USD 240) are eligible for full assistance, and those with income between INR 20,000-25,000 receive 50% assistance.

points.title: PwDs who are owners of motorized vehicles and having an income within prescribed limits are sanctioned 50% subsidy on actual ex…
points.text: 242. PwDs who are owners of motorized vehicles and having an income within prescribed limits are sanctioned 50% subsidy on actual expenditure on purchase of petrol/diesel or receive other transport-related subsidies as per updated state and central government schemes.

points.title: Driving Licences: The Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 as amended, and the Central Motor Vehicle Rules have been significantly revised…
points.text: 243. Driving Licences: The Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 as amended, and the Central Motor Vehicle Rules have been significantly revised to enhance accessibility for persons with disabilities. The issuance of driving licence to persons with hearing impairment has been subject for discussion. The National Association of the Deaf moved the Delhi High Court, to determine whether a person with hearing impairment who had an international licence was entitled to the right to a driving licence in India. Subsequent to various judicial interventions and policy reforms, persons with hearing impairment can now obtain driving licenses for both private and transport vehicles, subject to meeting medical fitness requirements as per the Central Motor Vehicle Rules. The Ministry of Road Transport and Highways has issued guidelines for medical examination of persons with disabilities applying for driving licenses (GSR 784(E) dated 2017 and subsequent amendments), and persons with various physical disabilities including those affecting limbs can obtain licenses with appropriate vehicle adaptations, certified by authorized agencies.

points.title: Airport Accessibility: Major airports have been made accessible with ramps, elevators, accessible toilets, and tactile pathways…
points.text: 244. Airport Accessibility: Major airports have been made accessible with ramps, elevators, accessible toilets, and tactile pathways conforming to accessibility standards under the Accessible India Campaign. Examples include the accessible airport in Goa and Lengpui Airport in Mizoram, which features disabled- friendly lifts and toilets. Airports provide wheelchair assistance, priority boarding, and designated seating areas for persons with disabilities, and several airlines offer concessional fares for persons with benchmark disabilities. The carriage of mobility aids and assistive devices is permitted without additional charge, and audio-visual announcement systems and accessible signage have been installed at major airports.

points.title: Accessible Roads and Pedestrian Infrastructure: Judicial guidance and policy directives mandate the inclusion of auditory signa…
points.text: 245. Accessible Roads and Pedestrian Infrastructure: Judicial guidance and policy directives mandate the inclusion of auditory signals at red lights to assist persons with visual impairments, the installation of warning blocks on footpaths and pedestrian crossings, the construction of footpaths with tactile paving, ramps at curb cuts, and barrier-free crossings, as well as compliance with the MoHUA Harmonized Guidelines and Standards for Universal Accessibility in India 2021 for all new road construction and retrofitting projects.

points.title: Mobility in Sports and Recreation: The RPwD Act 2016 under Sections 29 and 30 mandates measures to ensure persons with disabili…
points.text: 246. Mobility in Sports and Recreation: The RPwD Act 2016 under Sections 29 and 30 mandates measures to ensure persons with disabilities enjoy access to sports, recreation and tourism on equal basis with others. The Guidelines for Accessible Sports Complex and Residential Facilities for Sports Persons with Disabilities require accessible transport services to be provided for PwDs to attend practice sessions, sports events, and travel from accommodation areas to event venues. Sports facilities must be equipped with accessible pathways, entrances, and transport connectivity. Trained guides or volunteers provide physical assistance during sporting activities and training sessions.

points.title: Artificial Limbs Manufacturing Corporation of India (ALIMCO): Government of India has established ALIMCO with the motto of rest…
points.text: 247. Artificial Limbs Manufacturing Corporation of India (ALIMCO): Government of India has established ALIMCO with the motto of restoration of dignity. It is an ISO 9001:2015 certified company, and is reputed to be the largest manufacturing organization of artificial limbs and rehabilitation aids in entire South Asia. The products are priced to be affordable to the common person. ALIMCO manufactures products which aid people with various disabilities as defined under the RPwD Act 2016, covering 21 specified disabilities. ALIMCO produces advanced assistive devices including motorized wheelchairs, tricycles with battery support, specialized racing wheelchairs for sports, and customized prosthetics and prostheses. It also makes digital assistive devices such as screen readers, Braille displays, DAISY players, refreshable Braille devices, and communication aids for persons with speech and hearing disabilities. ALIMCO has established regional centres including, for example, a Regional Resource Centre at Zoram Medical College in Mizoram.
