points.subsection: C. Facilitating access to quality mobility aids, devices, assistive technologies and forms of live assistance at affordable cost

points.title: Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids/Appliances (ADIP) Scheme: The main objective of this Scheme is to a…
points.text: 248. Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids/Appliances (ADIP) Scheme: The main objective of this Scheme is to assist needy PwDs in procuring durable, sophisticated and scientifically manufactured, modern, standard aids and appliances that conform to Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) specifications that can promote their physical, social and psychological rehabilitation, by reducing the effects of disabilities and enhance their economic potential. ALIMCO serves as the principal implementing agency under the ADIP scheme, conducting regular assessment and distribution camps across states and Union Territories. The scheme is mandated under Section 24(3)(f) of the RPwD Act 2016, which requires the appropriate Government to provide schemes for aids and appliances, medicine, diagnostic services, and corrective surgery free of cost or at concessional rates for persons with disabilities, subject to such income limit as may be notified by the appropriate Government. The income ceiling for eligibility has been revised to INR 20,000 per month (approximately USD 240) for full assistance and INR 25,000 per month for 50% assistance as of 2018, with periodic revisions. The list of aids and appliances has been expanded to cover all 21 disability categories under the RPwD Act 2016. Camps are organized in every district at least once a year for assessment and distribution of aids and appliances. For example, Meghalaya distributed assistive devices such as wheelchairs, hearing aids, walking sticks, and Teaching Learning Materials (TLMs) to around 34 PwDs during Mega Camps between 2017-2022. The scheme is now implemented through a wider network including NGOs registered under the scheme, National Institutes under the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities, ALIMCO, and Composite Regional Centres. States like Chandigarh, Goa, and Meghalaya actively implement the ADIP Scheme through their Social Welfare Departments in collaboration with ALIMCO. Other state-level examples of implementation include Uttarakhand making an allotment of ₹88.00 lakh in the financial year 2024-25, and 1,558 artificial limbs being provided to disabled people, with a provision of ₹63.00 lakh made for FY 2025-26, and Meghalaya’s Social Welfare Department providing aids and appliances through regular distribution programmes.
points.subsection: C. Facilitating access to quality mobility aids, devices, assistive technologies and forms of live assistance at affordable cost

points.title: The Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities under the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment and Department…
points.text: 249. The Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities under the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment and Department of School Education and Literacy have signed Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) which has developed a Cost Sharing Scheme in order to provide aids and appliances to children in schools. Under this scheme, ALIMCO conducts assessment and distribution camps across the country in association with various State implementing agencies for provision of aids and appliances to children with special needs in the 6-14 age group and extended age groups under various programs. Under the Samagra Shiksha Abhiyan (SSA), 2.69 million Children with Special Needs (CwSN) requiring aids and appliances have been provided with assistive devices. ALIMCO has been a major implementing agency for providing aids and appliances to children with disabilities in schools.
points.subsection: C. Facilitating access to quality mobility aids, devices, assistive technologies and forms of live assistance at affordable cost

points.title: Research and development projects focus on developing indigenous, affordable, and culturally- appropriate assistive technologie…
points.text: 250. Research and development projects focus on developing indigenous, affordable, and culturally- appropriate assistive technologies aligned with the 21 disability categories under the RPwD Act 2016. The concept of Universal Design is explicitly recognized under Section 2(ze) of the RPwD Act 2016, applying to assistive devices including advanced technologies for particular groups of PwDs. Section 43 of the RPwD Act 2016 mandates that the appropriate Government shall take measures to promote the development, production and distribution of universally designed consumer products for general use for persons with disabilities, ensuring that entities producing mobility aids, devices and assistive technologies take into account all aspects of mobility for persons with disabilities.
points.subsection: C. Facilitating access to quality mobility aids, devices, assistive technologies and forms of live assistance at affordable cost

points.title: Research and development aimed at enhancing habilitation and rehabilitation are mandated to be initiated through individuals an…
points.text: 251. Research and development aimed at enhancing habilitation and rehabilitation are mandated to be initiated through individuals and institutions. This work occurs through premier institutions – the All India Council for Technical Education (AICTE) has developed courses aimed at sensitizing architecture and civil engineering students toward creation of accessible built environments. AICTE encourages student projects, research & rehabilitation focused on disability issues and challenges. IGNOU organizes camps for assessment and distribution of Assistive Devices in collaboration with ALIMCO (Artificial Limbs Manufacturing Corporation of India) under ADIP Scheme.
points.subsection: C. Facilitating access to quality mobility aids, devices, assistive technologies and forms of live assistance at affordable cost

points.title: Section 5 of the RPwD Act 2016 mandates that appropriate Government shall take effective measures to facilitate persons with di…
points.text: 252. Section 5 of the RPwD Act 2016 mandates that appropriate Government shall take effective measures to facilitate persons with disabilities to have access to a range of in-home, residential and other community support services, including personal assistance necessary to support living and inclusion in the community.

points.title: With regards to personal assistance in educational settings, the State of Andaman and Nicobar permits parents/guardians to atte…
points.text: 253. With regards to personal assistance in educational settings, the State of Andaman and Nicobar permits parents/guardians to attend CwSN students within the school premises, and maintains helpers to provide assistance to students with disabilities. Mizoram arranges volunteers to accompany PwD students in 4 colleges within the state. The state of Chandigarh has maintained an average of 15 attendants working yearly between 2018-19 and 2021-22 for students with special needs, with related expenditure ranging from ₹9.36 lakhs to ₹16.37 lakhs. In Uttarakhand, the state provides escort facilities for children having profound disability to ensure regular attendance at schools.

points.title: Sections 28 and 47 of the RPwD Act 2016 mandates research, development and capacity building in rehabilitation.
points.text: 254. Sections 28 and 47 of the RPwD Act 2016 mandates research, development and capacity building in rehabilitation.
points.subsection: E. Training in Mobility Skills and Capacity Building

points.title: Training of Trainers and Specialist Staff: The AICTE Guidelines propose developing SWAYAM Courses for training teachers in incl…
points.text: 255. Training of Trainers and Specialist Staff: The AICTE Guidelines propose developing SWAYAM Courses for training teachers in inclusion and accommodation solutions. AICTE Disability Support Units (DSU) conduct Training of Trainers and Training & Capacity Building Programs for faculty, administration teams and other stakeholders on disability inclusion. Andaman and Nicobar provides training or sensitization to Asha/Anganwadi workers, primary health workers, village level workers, and school teachers related to the identification, rehabilitation, and causes of disabilities. Chandigarh has conducted training sessions to sensitize Anganwadi workers regarding disabilities, with data indicating 6,469 teachers trained cumulatively between 2017-2022, with annual budgets designated for this purpose. Goa conducts regular training and sensitization of health staff related to identification, causes, and rehabilitation of PwDs. Specific trainings have been held for Anganwadi workers on awareness and early detection of deafness. Uttarakhand sensitizes teachers through the NISHTHA program. Sign language training was provided to 329 teachers, with an additional 95 teachers receiving advance level sign language training.
points.subsection: E. Training in Mobility Skills and Capacity Building

points.title: Capacity Building for Sports and Recreation Professionals: Capacity building training is mandated for coaches, staff, volunteer…
points.text: 256. Capacity Building for Sports and Recreation Professionals: Capacity building training is mandated for coaches, staff, volunteers, and all personnel coming in direct contact with sports persons with disabilities. Training covers disability etiquettes, equipment handling, guiding in wayfinding, and supporting PwDs in transfers. A provision for Sports Coach Training to senior players with disabilities is provided to develop indigenous coaching expertise.
points.subsection: E. Training in Mobility Skills and Capacity Building

points.title: Training for Persons with Disabilities: AICTE institutions provide training to students/faculty with print disability on how to…
points.text: 257. Training for Persons with Disabilities: AICTE institutions provide training to students/faculty with print disability on how to use digital book reading devices and assistive technologies. The Education Dept. in Andaman and Nicobar provides training in Braille and Sign language to students and teachers.
points.subsection: E. Training in Mobility Skills and Capacity Building

points.title: The right to freedom of expression and opinion is critical for the enjoyment of other human rights by PwDs.
points.text: 258. The right to freedom of expression and opinion is critical for the enjoyment of other human rights by PwDs. Article 19 (1)(a) of the Constitution of India states that all citizens shall have freedom of speech and expression. However, reasonable restrictions may be put by the Constitution but specifies the grounds on which such restriction can take place. The Supreme Court of India has emphasized the primacy of this right by not allowing it to be restricted on any ground other than that which has been specified in Article 19(2) of Constitution of India.
points.article: Article 21 - Freedom of expression and opinion, and access to information

points.title: The above interpretations are equally extendable to PwDs to enable them to exercise their rights under
points.text: 259. The above interpretations are equally extendable to PwDs to enable them to exercise their rights under
points.article: Article 21 - Freedom of expression and opinion, and access to information

points.title: The Supreme Court has strengthened these protections through landmark judgments.
points.text: 260. The Supreme Court has strengthened these protections through landmark judgments. In 2024, the Supreme Court held that accessibility is integral to the exercise of fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution, encompassing physical environments, transport, information, communication, and digital platforms. In 2022, the Court emphasized the right to access judgments delivered by courts and tribunals, directing institutions to avoid improperly placed watermarks and to upload accessible, digitally signed versions rather than scanned copies to support users relying on screen readers. 19 In the Pragya Prasun case (2025), the Supreme Court upheld the right to accessible and inclusive digital services for persons with disabilities and directed the formulation of inclusive digital KYC guidelines in line with Web Content Accessibility Guidelines and universal design principles.
points.article: Article 21 - Freedom of expression and opinion, and access to information

points.title: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act), which became operational on 19 April 2017, comprehensively mandat…
points.text: 261. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act), which became operational on 19 April 2017, comprehensively mandates accessibility in information and communication technology (ICT). Section 40 of the Act specifically includes ICT within its scope of accessibility requirements, while Section 42 mandates equal access to websites, digital content, and electronic media for persons with disabilities. All government documents, services, and public information are required to be made available digitally to be accessible.
points.article: Article 21 - Freedom of expression and opinion, and access to information

points.title: Rule 15 of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Rules, 2017 provides for accessibility of information and communication tech…
points.text: 262. Rule 15 of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Rules, 2017 provides for accessibility of information and communication technology. The Act defines ‘communication’ broadly under Section 2(f) to include languages, display of text, Braille, tactile communication, large print, accessible multimedia, as well as written, audio, plain-language, human-reader and augmentative and alternative modes, including accessible information and communication technology.
points.article: Article 21 - Freedom of expression and opinion, and access to information

points.title: The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) formulated the Indian Accessibility Standard(s) for ICT Products…
points.text: 263. The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) formulated the Indian Accessibility Standard(s) for ICT Products and Services (IS 17802), enforced by the RPwD (Amendment) Rules 2023. The standards mandate that information intended for the general public be available in accessible formats, and includes specific guidelines for readability, colour contrast, and screen reader compatibility. The MeitY standards cover all functional performance requirements arising from all disabilities listed in the RPwD Act, 2016.
points.article: Article 21 - Freedom of expression and opinion, and access to information

points.title: The Government has adopted the Guidelines for Indian Government Websites (GIGW) 3.0, developed by the National Informatics Cent…
points.text: 264. The Government has adopted the Guidelines for Indian Government Websites (GIGW) 3.0, developed by the National Informatics Centre under the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology. GIGW 3.0 incorporates the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 standards and mandates that all government websites, web applications, portals, and mobile applications comply with these accessibility standards. These guidelines are based on international practices including ISO 23026 and WCAG 2.0 of W3C, ensuring compliance with global quality measures. The website of the Ministry of Statistics & Programme Implementation (MoSPI) is reported as user-friendly for persons with disabilities as per GIGW guidelines of MeitY.
points.article: Article 21 - Freedom of expression and opinion, and access to information

points.title: GIGW 3.0 provides comprehensive frameworks for mobile app accessibility and usability, including integration with public digita…
points.text: 265. GIGW 3.0 provides comprehensive frameworks for mobile app accessibility and usability, including integration with public digital infrastructure such as DigiLocker, Aadhaar-based identity systems, and AI- based language translation. The guidelines also incorporate a dedicated chapter on cybersecurity developed by CERT-In to ensure security alongside accessibility.
points.article: Article 21 - Freedom of expression and opinion, and access to information

points.title: The Ministry of Information & Broadcasting directed all its media units to follow the GIGW 3.0 guidelines regarding accessibili…
points.text: 266. The Ministry of Information & Broadcasting directed all its media units to follow the GIGW 3.0 guidelines regarding accessibility of websites and to appoint an Accessibility Auditor. Out of 27 websites/portals of the Ministry and its Media Units, 13 are accessible and have obtained STQC certificate, 10 are in the process of obtaining the certificate, and 4 are under redevelopment.

points.title: All AICTE (All India Council for Technical Education) approved institutions are mandated to pursue or establish subscription to…
points.text: 267. All AICTE (All India Council for Technical Education) approved institutions are mandated to pursue or establish subscription to online sources of accessible format books such as Sugamya Pustakalaya, Bookshare, and ABC global book service, and register each student with print disability on these platforms. AICTE mandates providing a digital book reading device (such as a laptop with screen reading software, smart phone, refreshable braille display, DAISY player, etc.) to each student/faculty with print disability free of cost or on subsidy.

points.text: 268. The Indira Gandhi National Open University (IGNOU) is a member of Sugamya Pustakalaya, where students with blindness, low vision, or other print disabilities can access around 3.5 lakh books. The IGNOU website is W3C compliant and accessible for persons with disabilities.

points.title: Learning materials are provided to parents/guardians of Children with Special Needs (CwSN) in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands.
points.text: 269. Learning materials are provided to parents/guardians of Children with Special Needs (CwSN) in the Andaman and Nicobar Islands. In Chandigarh, CwSN are provided Braille and Large Print Books based on their needs. The expenditure for Braille/Large Print Books in Chandigarh between 2017-2022 was approximately ₹14.71 lakhs for 539 beneficiaries. In Goa, various facilities are provided at the Krishandas Shama Goa State Central Library, including a Braille section and image magnifier.

points.title: The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) has issued advisories to early warning agencies (IMD, etc.) to ensure that al…
points.text: 270. The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) has issued advisories to early warning agencies (IMD, etc.) to ensure that alerts and warnings are accessible to persons with disabilities, including through text messages, audio alerts, sign language interpreters, and pictorial formats. In the context of Mental Health and Psychosocial Support Services (MHPSS) during disasters, communication materials must be available in accessible formats such as Braille, sign language, and easy-to-read formats. All outreach and information sharing in disaster management must be carried out through multiple communication channels and formats to accommodate visual, hearing, or cognitive impairments (e.g., large-print, Braille, audio versions, screen readers, captioned video IECs, sign language, simplified text).

points.title: State-level implementation has also taken place.
points.text: 277. State-level implementation has also taken place. The state of Chandigarh has directed all Heads of Departments, Boards, and Corporations to appoint a sign language interpreter during meetings, seminars, workshops, and press briefings. Meghalaya similarly mandates the provision of sign language interpretation in all meetings & programmes since 2017. Uttarakhand provides sign language interpretation in seminars, workshops, and other events like World Disability Day. Furthermore, sign language training was given to 329 teachers, and 95 teachers received advance level sign language training. The State Commissioner for persons with disabilities in Mizoram has requested all Departments to engage a sign language interpreter in all official programmes. In Andaman and Nicobar Islands, training regarding Braille and Sign language is provided to Teachers and Students. In Goa, some special schools have been using Indian Sign Language for their students with Hearing Impairment. The implementation of the National Education Policy in Goa proposes to offer Indian Sign Language as a third language.

points.title: Doordarshan (DD) has implemented comprehensive accessibility features.
points.text: 279. Doordarshan (DD) has implemented comprehensive accessibility features. For instance, DD News broadcasts two daily 15-minute bulletins for hearing impaired persons with sign language interpretation. Sign language interpretation has been provided by DD News for special broadcasts like Cabinet briefings. DD Bharati telecasts sign language versions of events of national importance such as the Hon’ble President’s messages, Independence Day Ceremony, Republic Day Ceremony, and the Hon’ble Prime Minister’s monthly interaction programme ‘Mann Ki Baat’. Doordarshan channels also telecast scrolls and highlights on the screen in National and Regional Networks, making news accessible for persons with hearing impairments.

points.title: The Ministry formulated ‘Accessibility Standards for Persons with Disabilities in Television Programmes’.
points.text: 280. The Ministry formulated ‘Accessibility Standards for Persons with Disabilities in Television Programmes’. All news channels are required to take the best efforts to carry at least one news bulletin with sign language interpretation per day, and all TV channels are recommended to run at least one program per week with subtitles or captioning. Service providers may choose between open or closed captions, subtitles, or sign language.
points.article: Article 21 - Freedom of expression and opinion, and access to information

points.title: The Ministry constituted a Core Group to examine challenges in implementing accessibility standards, such as the lack of skille…
points.text: 281. The Ministry constituted a Core Group to examine challenges in implementing accessibility standards, such as the lack of skilled sign-language interpreters, subtitling of regional and foreign language content, and accessibility provision in live and deferred live content. Accessibility Standards are currently implemented on a ‘best effort basis’. The Ministry requests all television news channels (including private channels) to relay at least one news programme weekly in accessible format and seeks monthly reports from Broadcasters Associations on accessibility programmes.

points.text: 282. The Monthly Accessible Programme data shows increasing compliance. Seven General Entertainment Channels (GEC) are providing all shows and movies in an accessible format. January 2025 (77 Channels) 4,223 accessible programs broadcast (910 GEC, 3,313 News). February 2025 (75 Channels) 4,244 accessible programs broadcast (3,387 GEC, 857 News). March 2025 (67 Channels): 3,717 accessible programs broadcast (946 GEC, 2,771 News).

points.title: Content creators and owners are responsible for creating accessible content and delivering it to service providers.
points.text: 283. Content creators and owners are responsible for creating accessible content and delivering it to service providers. Live broadcasts, unscripted shows, advertisements, live sports, award shows, reality shows, and live debates are exempted from these requirements. To support private broadcasters, Doordarshan initially provided its sign language news bulletins free of cost, allowing time for private channels to develop their own sign language capabilities.

points.title: The Indian Sign Language Research and Training Centre (ISLRTC) was established on 28 September 2015 as an autonomous organizati…
points.text: 284. The Indian Sign Language Research and Training Centre (ISLRTC) was established on 28 September 2015 as an autonomous organization under the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities (DEPwD), Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment. ISLRTC serves as the primary institution for research, training, and promotion of Indian Sign Language (ISL).

points.title: ISLRTC organizes the National Indian Sign Language Competition annually and celebrates International Day of Sign Languages on 2…
points.text: 285. ISLRTC organizes the National Indian Sign Language Competition annually and celebrates International Day of Sign Languages on 23 September each year. In 2024, the theme was “Sign up for Sign Language Rights,” and in 2025, it was “No Human Rights Without Sign Language Rights.” These events emphasize the crucial role of sign language in ensuring equality, inclusion, and dignity for persons who are deaf. Despite these efforts, Indian Sign Language is not yet recognized as an official language under the Indian Constitution, unlike the 22 languages currently recognized.

points.text: 286. MeitY’s IS 17802 recognizes the importance of sign languages and promotes their use, setting specific criteria for video content accessibility that requires sign language interpretation. The National Language Technology Mission (DIGITAL INDIA BHASHINI), launched in July 2022, provides AI-driven language technology solutions, including technologies for Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR). These technologies are integrated into the Bhashini Mobile App for free use.

points.title: The National Institute for Empowerment of Persons with Visual Disabilities (NIEPVD), formerly known as National Institute for t…
points.text: 288. The National Institute for Empowerment of Persons with Visual Disabilities (NIEPVD), formerly known as National Institute for the Visually Handicapped (NIVH), is perceived as a cornerstone in Braille Development. NIEPVD has been extensively involved in the standardization of Bharti Braille, a system that corresponds to all the official languages of the country and notation systems for Mathematics and Science. It has also contributed to Braille contractions and abbreviations and shorthand systems for many Indian languages.
points.article: Article 21 - Freedom of expression and opinion, and access to information

points.title: For popularization of Braille, several steps have been taken such as short-term training courses and distance education modules.
points.text: 289. For popularization of Braille, several steps have been taken such as short-term training courses and distance education modules. In order to consolidate and regulate standards of Braille production, design of Braille appliances including braille conversion software, the Government established the Braille Council of India in 2008 drawing its membership from across the sectors with adequate representation of the blind persons in them. The Braille Development Unit serves as a Secretariat of Braille Council of India.
points.article: Article 21 - Freedom of expression and opinion, and access to information

points.title: Central Braille Press: Established in 1951 in Dehradun, the Central Braille Press is the first press in India and one of the ol…
points.text: 290. Central Braille Press: Established in 1951 in Dehradun, the Central Braille Press is the first press in India and one of the oldest Braille presses in Asia. The press combines traditional printing methods with modern computerized high-speed Braille embossers. A Regional Centre was established in 2008 in Chennai, featuring high-speed computerized Braille embossers.

points.text: 291. Since 2014, under the SIPDA (Scheme for Implementation of Persons with Disabilities Act) Scheme of DEPwD, 25 Braille Presses have been modernized. The Central Braille Press produces educational materials in 14 languages including Assamese, Garo, Bangla, English, Kannada, Hindi, Khasi, Punjabi, Lusai, Nagamese, Tamil, Sanskrit, Telugu, and Urdu, providing Braille books and educational materials free of cost from class 1 to 12 under the SIPDA Scheme.

points.title: Recent technological advances include the production of E-PUB books compatible with screen readers and Braille display devices,…
points.text: 292. Recent technological advances include the production of E-PUB books compatible with screen readers and Braille display devices, supporting the Accessible India Campaign. The press is equipped with 2D printers (View Plus Machine) and 3D printers (Pratham 3.0) that provide tactile diagrams and 3D models to support science, mathematics, and geography education for visually impaired students. In 2024-25, the press reported a production value of Rs. 1,51,80,552 and sales value of Rs. 1,78,99,365.

points.title: The Department of Financial Services (DFS) has formulated Accessibility Standard Guidelines for infrastructure and services in…
points.text: 295. The Department of Financial Services (DFS) has formulated Accessibility Standard Guidelines for infrastructure and services in Financial Institutions across India to ensure equitable access to services by persons with disabilities. These guidelines mandate that financial institutions (i) assist with filling forms and provide magnifying glasses for low-vision users, (ii) ensure website accessibility through screen readers, adjustable text sizes, and contrast settings, and (iii) provide services and documents in accessible formats, such as Braille-enabled credit and debit cards.

The Ministry is currently formulating guidelines for improving disabled-friendly accessibility features for content on OTT platforms.

points.title: Article 22 of the UNCRPD recognizes the right of PwDs for the protection of their private life, reputation and honor qua the ri…
points.text: 300. Article 22 of the UNCRPD recognizes the right of PwDs for the protection of their private life, reputation and honor qua the right to privacy. The Indian Supreme Court has read the right to privacy into the right to life and personal liberty under Article 21 of the Constitution of India.

points.title: Section 23 of the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 provides that a person with mental illness shall have the right to confidentialit…
points.text: 301. Section 23 of the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 provides that a person with mental illness shall have the right to confidentiality in respect of his mental health, mental healthcare, treatment and physical healthcare. All health professionals providing care or treatment to a person with mental illness shall have a duty to keep all such information confidential which has been obtained during care or treatment, with exceptions including release to nominated representatives, other mental health professionals, when necessary to protect any person from harm or violence, upon order by a Mental Health Review Board or court, and in the interests of public safety and security. Section 24 of the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 further provides that no photograph or any other information relating to a person with mental illness undergoing treatment shall be released to the media without the person's consent. The Act also provides that the right to confidentiality applies to all information stored in electronic or digital format.

points.title: In the context of health services, the National Disaster Management Guidelines on Mental Health and Psychosocial Support Servic…
points.text: 302. In the context of health services, the National Disaster Management Guidelines on Mental Health and Psychosocial Support Services (MHPSS) requires that staff be sensitized to the rights of PwDs and trained to avoid discrimination or use of stigmatizing language in all service settings. This measure serves to protect PwDs from communication that may harm their honour and reputation. C. Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023

points.title: The Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023 is a new legislative framework for protecting personal data of all indivi…
points.text: 303. The Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023 is a new legislative framework for protecting personal data of all individuals in India, including persons with disabilities. Section 2(j) of the DPDP Act defines "data principal" to include persons with disabilities and their lawful guardians. Section 9 of the DPDP Act mandates that before processing any personal data of a person with a disability who has a lawful guardian, the data fiduciary must obtain verifiable consent from the lawful guardian. However, concerns have been raised regarding the implementation of this provision, particularly regarding data minimization principles, the definition of "persons with disabilities," and the risk of infantilizing persons with disabilities by conflating their provisions with those applicable to children. D. Digital Accessibility Standards - IS 17802 and Enforcement
points.subsection: C. Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023

points.title: In December 2021, the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) published IS 17802, India's official standard for ensuring digital acces…
points.text: 304. In December 2021, the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) published IS 17802, India's official standard for ensuring digital accessibility in Information and Communication Technology (ICT) products and services. This standard was formally incorporated into the RPWD Rules via the 2023 amendment and became legally enforceable under Indian law. In a landmark enforcement action in February 2025, 155 private and government organizations, including several central ministries, were penalized for non-compliance with the digital accessibility mandates of the RPWD Act. This development signals the Government's commitment to enforcing digital accessibility standards and emphasizes that digital accessibility is a legal obligation, not optional, for both public and private entities.
points.subsection: D. Digital Accessibility Standards - IS 17802 and Enforcement

points.title: The Indian Accessibility Standard (IS 17802) lays emphasis on privacy aspects, specifically through Functional Performance Requ…
points.text: 305. The Indian Accessibility Standard (IS 17802) lays emphasis on privacy aspects, specifically through Functional Performance Requirements under clause 4.2.11, titled "Privacy". This standard mandates that where ICT provides features for accessibility, the ICT must maintain the privacy of users of these features at the same level as other users. Design features noted as contributing toward meeting this clause include enabling the connection of personal headsets for private listening, not providing a spoken version of characters being masked, and enabling user control of legal, financial, and personal data.
points.subsection: D. Digital Accessibility Standards - IS 17802 and Enforcement

points.title: In 2025, the Supreme Court of India issued a landmark judgment in the cases Pragya Prasun v.
points.text: 306. In 2025, the Supreme Court of India issued a landmark judgment in the cases Pragya Prasun v. Union of India and Amar Jain v. Union of India & Ors. delivering 20 binding directions to overhaul the digital architecture for service delivery with inclusivity and accessibility at the center. The Court directed the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) to issue new guidelines that incorporate alternative methods to verify "liveness" or live photographs, moving beyond the default "blinking of eyes" method. Entities must now accept thumb impressions as valid authentication for visually impaired users during the e-KYC process. This judgment ensures that persons with visual impairments and acid attack survivors can access digital financial services without discrimination. E. Protection of Personal, Health, and Rehabilitation Information
points.subsection: D. Digital Accessibility Standards - IS 17802 and Enforcement

points.title: The Right to Medical/Health Insurance for persons with all disabilities was upheld by the Delhi High Court, which directed modi…
points.text: 307. The Right to Medical/Health Insurance for persons with all disabilities was upheld by the Delhi High Court, which directed modifications to terminology like 'substandard lives' in regulations to ensure insurance policies do not discriminate based on physical or intellectual disabilities, promoting inclusivity and equality in accessing sensitive health services.
points.subsection: E. Protection of Personal, Health, and Rehabilitation Information

points.title: The Madras High Court emphasized that PwDs are entitled to the right to equality, life with dignity, and respect for their inte…
points.text: 308. The Madras High Court emphasized that PwDs are entitled to the right to equality, life with dignity, and respect for their integrity equally with others, specifically noting that obtaining a disability certificate should occur without any hassle or difficulty. The health infrastructure should be malleable enough to address the adequate needs of every PwD. F. Protection of Honor, Reputation, Family, and Home
points.subsection: E. Protection of Personal, Health, and Rehabilitation Information

points.title: Non-discrimination and Dignity: The Telangana High Court stated that discrimination against any person based on disability is a…
points.text: 309. Non-discrimination and Dignity: The Telangana High Court stated that discrimination against any person based on disability is a violation of their inherent dignity, honour, and self-esteem.
points.subsection: F. Protection of Honor, Reputation, Family, and Home

points.title: The Supreme Court noted the importance of the right to dignity and respect in all aspects, including air travel, and stressed t…
points.text: 310. The Supreme Court noted the importance of the right to dignity and respect in all aspects, including air travel, and stressed the duty of individuals and organizations/airlines to be sensitive and accommodative. The Supreme Court held that the testimony of a witness with disability cannot be considered weak or inferior to that of their able-bodied counterparts solely due to their disability.
points.subsection: F. Protection of Honor, Reputation, Family, and Home

points.title: Discriminatory provisions against leprosy affected persons were omitted from relevant acts (including the Divorce Act, Special…
points.text: 311. Discriminatory provisions against leprosy affected persons were omitted from relevant acts (including the Divorce Act, Special Marriage Act, and Hindu Marriage Act) through the Personal Laws (Amendment) Act, 2019, which came into force on March 1, 2019, protecting their honor and status in civil law.
points.subsection: F. Protection of Honor, Reputation, Family, and Home

points.title: The Kerala High Court affirmed that an employee who acquires a disability during service cannot be demoted or dismissed solely…
points.text: 312. The Kerala High Court affirmed that an employee who acquires a disability during service cannot be demoted or dismissed solely on that ground, ensuring continued employment and equal pay and supporting the maintenance of dignity and livelihood. G. Family, Home, and Financial Autonomy
points.subsection: F. Protection of Honor, Reputation, Family, and Home

points.title: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act) contains explicit provisions regarding the Home and family, statin…
points.text: 313. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act) contains explicit provisions regarding the Home and family, stating that no child with disability shall be separated from his or her parents on the ground of disability except by a competent court order, in the best interest of the child. If parents are unable to provide care, the court must prioritize placement with near relations, or failing that, within a community or family setting.
points.subsection: G. Family, Home, and Financial Autonomy

points.title: The RPwD Act mandates that PwDs have the right, equally with others, to own or inherit property, control their financial affair…
points.text: 314. The RPwD Act mandates that PwDs have the right, equally with others, to own or inherit property, control their financial affairs, and have access to bank loans, mortgages, and other financial credit. Policies exist to accommodate government employees who are caregivers of PwDs, potentially exempting them from routine transfers to provide stability to the family/home environment.
points.subsection: G. Family, Home, and Financial Autonomy

points.title: In cases where a person with disability requires assistance but is unable to take legally binding decisions, the RPwD Act allow…
points.text: 315. In cases where a person with disability requires assistance but is unable to take legally binding decisions, the RPwD Act allows for the provision of limited guardianship, emphasizing that support provided must respect the person's autonomy, dignity, and privacy. The appropriate government must designate an authority for this purpose. For instance, Uttar Pradesh has notified the authority to provide limited guardianship. H. Legal Protection
points.subsection: G. Family, Home, and Financial Autonomy

points.title: States Parties must ensure that PwDs have the right to protection of the law against unlawful interference or attacks.
points.text: 316. States Parties must ensure that PwDs have the right to protection of the law against unlawful interference or attacks. The RPwD Act, 2016 provides for the designation of Special Courts (under Section 84) and the appointment of a Special Public Prosecutor (under Section 85) to ensure speedy trial of cases instituted under the Act.

points.title: This provision is being implemented in Indian states.
points.text: 317. This provision is being implemented in Indian states. Union Territory of Andaman & Nicobar designated the court of Additional District and Session Judge, Port Blair, and Mayabunder, as the Special Court for RPwD Act cases as of August 23, 2019. Special Public Prosecutors were also appointed on the same date. Similarly, Chandigarh has designated a Special Court and appointed a Public Prosecutor. Uttar Pradesh has designated the court of the seventh Additional Sessions Judge in each district as the Special Court and appointed senior prosecution officers as Special Public Prosecutors, and Uttarakhand has also designated a Special Court and appointed a Public Prosecutor. Article 23 – Respect for Home and the Family A. Constitutional Mandate on the Right to Marry

points.title: Family is an integral component of the social fabric of a society.
points.text: 318. Family is an integral component of the social fabric of a society. Even as the expanded interpretation of Article 21 of the Indian Constitution would encompass the right to marry within the right to life, the Supreme Court has ruled that an unrestricted and absolute right to marry cannot be claimed. The Court has however acknowledged that such restrictions should be formulated in disability neutral terms.
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family
points.subsection: A. Constitutional Mandate on the Right to Marry

points.title: In India, marriages are governed by religious personal laws or by Special Marriage Act.
points.text: 319. In India, marriages are governed by religious personal laws or by Special Marriage Act. Depending upon the law opted, the marriage and consequences such as divorce, maintenance, guardianship, trusteeship, adoption etc. are regulated either by the religious personal law or the secular civil law. B. Statutory Provisions for Marriage and Divorce

points.subsection: A. Constitutional Mandate on the Right to Marry

points.title: For most personal laws, a sound mind and the ability to understand the implications of marriage are a pre-requisite for a valid…
points.text: 320. For most personal laws, a sound mind and the ability to understand the implications of marriage are a pre-requisite for a valid marriage. However under Muslim Law, the marriage of a person with a mental disability is valid, if consent is given by the guardian of such person on his or her behalf. Unsoundness of mind, which is incurable or which is coupled with behaviour of a kind and that is not reasonable to expect the petitioner to stay with the respondent is the standard formulation to permit divorce.
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family
points.subsection: B. Statutory Provisions for Marriage and Divorce

points.title: Discriminatory provisions against leprosy-affected persons in marriage, divorce, and adoption laws were removed through the Per…
points.text: 321. Discriminatory provisions against leprosy-affected persons in marriage, divorce, and adoption laws were removed through the Personal Laws (Amendment) Act, 2019 (Act 6 of 2019). The Amendment Act came into force on March 1, 2019. The five relevant Acts amended were the Divorce Act, 1869; the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act, 1939; the Special Marriage Act, 1954; the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955; and the Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act, 1956. C. Family Planning Education and Reproductive Rights
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family
points.subsection: B. Statutory Provisions for Marriage and Divorce

points.title: The National Population Policy, 2000 adopts a target free approach in administering family planning services.
points.text: 322. The National Population Policy, 2000 adopts a target free approach in administering family planning services. The policy aims to meet the reproductive and child health needs of the people, and address issues of child survival, maternal health, and contraception. PwDs are beneficiaries of these policies as members of the general population.
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family
points.subsection: C. Family Planning Education and Reproductive Rights

points.title: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 mandates that the appropriate Government must ensure that persons with disabi…
points.text: 323. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 mandates that the appropriate Government must ensure that persons with disabilities have access to appropriate information regarding reproductive and family planning (Section 10(1)). The Act prohibits subjecting any person with disability to any medical procedure which leads to infertility without his or her free and informed consent (Section 10(2)). Inputs concerning implementation of reproductive rights and retention of fertility (Article 23, parts b and c) are the concern and responsibility of the Ministry of Women and Child Development (MoWCD).
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family
points.subsection: C. Family Planning Education and Reproductive Rights

points.title: Further the Supreme Court of India has upheld the right of motherhood of a girl with intellectual disability who desires to kee…
points.text: 324. Further the Supreme Court of India has upheld the right of motherhood of a girl with intellectual disability who desires to keep her child in the landmark case Suchita Srivastava vs Chandigarh Administration (2009). The Court took note of the UNCRPD "in respecting the personal autonomy of mentally retarded persons with regard to the reproductive choice". Subsequently, in 2024-2025, the Bombay High Court has further recognized the reproductive autonomy of women with intellectual disabilities, confirming that persons with borderline, mild or moderate intellectual disability are capable of being good parents and possess social and emotional capacities that enable them to be good mothers. The Supreme Court has held that persons with intellectual disability cannot be deprived of motherhood without their informed consent, and that any restrictions on reproductive rights must be based on a threat of grave injury to the physical or mental health of the woman. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 in Sections 10, 13 and 92 recognizes that to be a mother is the personal right of a woman with disability and such rights cannot be curtailed. Section 14 of the RPWD Act and the National Trust Act provide that persons with disability unable to take legally binding decisions may be provided support through a "limited guardian" to take such decisions on their behalf. D. Adoption & Custody of Children: Statutory Framework
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family
points.subsection: C. Family Planning Education and Reproductive Rights

points.title: The procedure for adoption of Indian children, both in-country and inter-country, is governed by guidelines governing Adoption…
points.text: 325. The procedure for adoption of Indian children, both in-country and inter-country, is governed by guidelines governing Adoption of Children, 2011. The process of adoption is completed by a legal order under the Juvenile Justice Care and Protection of Children Act, 2000, or the Hindu Adoption & Maintenance Act 1956. India has appointed the Central Adoption Resource Authority as the nodal agency in the country to provide noninstitutional care services to orphan, abandoned and surrendered children. This framework has been substantially updated by the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015, which came into force on 15 January 2016, replacing the 2000 Act. The 2015 Act introduces foster care as a formal institutional mechanism in India and streamlines adoption procedures with clear timelines for both in-country and inter-country adoption. The Act was further amended by the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Amendment Act, 2021, which came into force from 1 September 2022.
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family
points.subsection: D. Adoption & Custody of Children: Statutory Framework

points.title: The guidelines, governing Adoption of Children, 2011 expedites the rehabilitation of children with special needs through adoption.
points.text: 326. The guidelines, governing Adoption of Children, 2011 expedites the rehabilitation of children with special needs through adoption. Special software on such children, residing under protection of adoption agencies, has been developed as part of the web based Management System on Adoption – CARINGS. The guidelines governing the Adoption of children, 2011, also states that special care must be taken while addressing the adoption procedure of children with special needs and they must be given priority in the waiting list. Such children include children with disabilities, who have physical and medical conditions. Further, the Juvenile Justice Act, 2015 mandates that children with mental and physical disability, siblings and children above the age of five years should be given priority in in-country adoption, and establishes that disabled children and children who are physically and financially incapable are to be given priority for adoption. The Guidelines for Adoption from India, 2006 state that the clearance for inter-country adoption of children with special needs must be given within 10 days of the application made to the Adoption. E. Adoption Schemes and Programmes

points.text: 327. The Central Government has recognized the following agencies: a. Indian Placement Agencies - 73 (in various states) b. Foreign Placement Agencies Enlisted - 254 (in foreign countries) c. Voluntary Co-ordinating Agency in India - 13 (in various states) d. Scrutiny Agencies - 13 (in various states)

points.title: The Scheme of Assistance to Homes (Shishu Greh) For Children to Promote In-Country Adoption: this scheme provides support for i…
points.text: 328. The Scheme of Assistance to Homes (Shishu Greh) For Children to Promote In-Country Adoption: this scheme provides support for institutional care within the country for care and protection of infants and children up to 6 years of age who are either abandoned or orphaned/destitute, and their rehabilitation through In-Country adoption. Children with disabilities are generically included in these schemes. F. Custody of Children
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family

points.title: An important aspect of the right to home and family is the custody of children of parents who are divorced or separated.
points.text: 329. An important aspect of the right to home and family is the custody of children of parents who are divorced or separated. This aspect assumes a strong significance when one of the parents is a PwD. The law of custody of children is primarily governed by the Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, 1956 for parents who are governed by Hindu law and the Guardians and Wards Act, 1890 for parents who belong to other religions. Both legislations take into consideration the physical and mental well-being of the person, who might be appointed as a guardian of the child. The National Trust Act, 1999 provides for limited guardianship provisions that are more restrictive than general guardianship provisions. Limited guardianship operates on a spirit of mutuality between the person with disability and the guardian and is limited to either a specified period or a particular decision or situation, as per Section 14 of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016. The appointment of a guardian requires the opinion of the person with disability to prevail, with the disability person's preferences taking precedence over those of family members in matters of personal welfare. G. Guardianship and Legal Capacity
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family

points.title: The RPwD Act, 2016 includes a provision for limited guardianship (Section 14).
points.text: 330. The RPwD Act, 2016 includes a provision for limited guardianship (Section 14). A district court or designated authority (notified by the State Government) may appoint a limited guardian if a person with disability, despite support, is unable to take legally binding decisions. A person with disability aggrieved by the limited guardianship decision can prefer an appeal to an appellate authority as notified by the State Government.
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family

points.title: The appropriate Government must ensure that persons with disabilities enjoy the right, equally with others, to own or inherit p…
points.text: 331. The appropriate Government must ensure that persons with disabilities enjoy the right, equally with others, to own or inherit property, control their financial affairs, and have access to bank loans, mortgages, and other financial credit (Section 13(1)).
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family

points.title: This has been implemented in states.
points.text: 332. This has been implemented in states. The Union Territory administration of Andaman & Nicobar ensures the appointment of authority for limited guardianship when assistance is required, referencing the Guardianship Act. In Uttar Pradesh, an authority to provide limited guardianship was notified on August 28, 2018. In Chandigarh, an authority has been designated for providing limited guardianship appointments and for the registration of institutions working in the disability sector as per Section 14 of the RPwD Act, 2016. However, there are also impediments in implementation of limited guardianship. A case was filed in the Kerala High Court (2020) regarding a petitioner seeking appointment as a guardian for a person with disability (Shri Mahesh P.C.) under the National Trust Act, 1999, to manage fixed deposits, where the court noted delays by the Local Level Committee. H. Protection of Children and Prevention of Separation
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family

points.title: Under the RPwD Act, no child with disability shall be separated from his or her parents on the ground of disability except via…
points.text: 333. Under the RPwD Act, no child with disability shall be separated from his or her parents on the ground of disability except via a competent court order, only if required in the best interest of the child (Section 9(1)). If parents are unable to care for a child with disability, the court must place the child with near relations, or failing that, within the community in a family setting or, exceptionally, in a shelter home run by the government or an NGO. The appropriate Government and local authorities must ensure that children with disabilities enjoy their rights equally with others. I. Early Intervention and Identification (Ages 0-6)
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family
points.subsection: H. Protection of Children and Prevention of Separation

points.title: In Uttar Pradesh, the Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK) is implemented under the National Health Mission to identify and…
points.text: 334. In Uttar Pradesh, the Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK) is implemented under the National Health Mission to identify and provide timely referral and treatment for developmental delays/disabilities in children aged 0 to 6 years. Under RBSK in UP, ASHA workers and A.N.M. have been trained for early identification of congenital defects in children. The program identifies specific conditions including Vision Impairment, Hearing Impairment, Neuro-motor impairment, Motor delay, cognitive delay, Language delay, behaviour disorder (Autism), Learning disorder, and Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder. J. Legal Identity (Birth Registration):

points.text: 336. The Uttarakhand High Court (2018) specifically directed the State Government to register children with intellectual disabilities within six months of birth and ensure that no person with mental and intellectual disability is abandoned and left in lurch on the streets.
