points.text: 338. A 2014 Madras High Court case concerning PwDs in institutions highlighted concerns about their safety, directing the submission of monthly reports to the relevant authority regarding inmates and mandating that information on persons taken as inmates be furnished to the nearest police station.
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family

points.title: In a notable case (Case No.
points.text: 339. In a notable case (Case No. 202/2023) handled by the State Commissioner for PwDs in UP, recommendations were made to prevent a child from being dismissed from school due to disability. The directions recognised the need for non-discrimination in education, sports, entertainment, and other activities. Article 24 – Education ● Evolving Philosophy on Education of Children with Disabilities
points.article: Article 23 - Respect for Home and the Family

points.title: Article 45 of the Constitution of India directs the State to endeavour to provide free and compulsory education to all children…
points.text: 340. Article 45 of the Constitution of India directs the State to endeavour to provide free and compulsory education to all children up to the age of 14 years within a period of ten years from the commencement of the Constitution. The other policies related to education for children are: Policy Objective The National Policy on Education (NPE) (1986) Stressed the need for integrating children with and Program of Action (1992) special needs with other groups National Curriculum framework (NCERT, 2005) Commitment to inclusive education National Policy on Education (NPE), 1986 and the Envisaged some measures to integrate children Programme of Action (1992) with physical and mental impairment to mainstream educational institutions The National Action Plan for Inclusion in Emphasizes on the inclusion of children and young Education of Children and Youth with Disabilities PwDs in all general educational settings from Early (IECYD), developed by the MHRD (November Childhood to Higher Education 2005) National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 Promotes inclusive education through universal access and participation, flexible curricula, comprehensive teacher training and professional development, assistive technologies, inclusive infrastructure, competency-based assessment, and community participation. The NEP 2020 emphasizes early childhood care and education (ECCE) for children with disabilities, recognizing that early identification and intervention play vital roles in supporting development.

points.title: The provision of inclusive educational opportunities is recognized as an affirmative obligation of the Government, aiming to ma…
points.text: 341. The provision of inclusive educational opportunities is recognized as an affirmative obligation of the Government, aiming to make persons with disabilities (PwDs) self-sufficient and financially independent. The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 is in complete consonance with the provisions of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (RPwD) Act, 2016, and places the highest priority on ensuring the inclusion and equal participation of children with disabilities (CwSN) in Early Childhood Care & Education (ECCE) and the schooling system, enabling them to participate fully from the Foundational Stage to higher education.

points.title: The goal of the Action Plan is –to ensure the inclusion of children and youth with disabilities in all available general educat…
points.text: 342. The goal of the Action Plan is –to ensure the inclusion of children and youth with disabilities in all available general educational settings, by providing them with a learning environment that is available, accessible, affordable and appropriate. The literacy rate amongst PwDs is lower when compared to the general population. Whereas the literacy rate of the general population is 74.04% that for PwDs is about 40%. In Uttarakhand, the PwDs literacy rate as per Census 2011 shows Male - 54.52% and Female -69.45%. The Twelfth Plan has proposed several social, educational and employment provisions for the self-reliance of PwDs. Education plays a pivotal role in socio-economic empowerment of PwDs. ● Elementary Education

points.title: Right to Education and Children with Disabilities: Section 26 of the PWDA requires Central, State and Local Governments to ensu…
points.text: 343. Right to Education and Children with Disabilities: Section 26 of the PWDA requires Central, State and Local Governments to ensure "that every child with disability has access to free education appropriate environment up to ...the age of 18 years"; to promote the integration of students with disabilities in the mainstream schools; "setting up special schools in government and private sector for those in need to special education" and "to equip the special schools ... with vocational training facilities. " The PWDA provides multiple educational options for children with disabilities.

points.title: The educational rights of children with disabilities were addressed in the proviso to Section 3 of Right of Children to Free an…
points.text: 344. The educational rights of children with disabilities were addressed in the proviso to Section 3 of Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act (RTE), 2009 "provided that a child suffering from disability as defined in clause (i) of section 2 of the PWDA shall have the right to pursue free and compulsory elementary education in accordance with the provisions of chapter V of the said Act". By reason of this provision children with disabilities secured the right to free and compulsory education upto the age of 18 years. The Right to Free and Compulsory Education (RTE) Act 2009 mandates free and compulsory elementary education for all children, including Children with Special Needs (CwSN). The Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education (Amendment) Bill, 2024 seeks to further align the RTE Act with the evolving disability rights framework by amending the definition of “child with disability” to include persons with disabilities as defined in the RPWD Act, 2016, benchmark disabilities, children with high support needs, and children with multiple disabilities. This amendment aims to reduce inconsistencies and provide greater clarity on what inclusion means in terms of quality education for children with disabilities, addressing on-ground implementation challenges.

points.title: An amendment to the RTE Act in September 2022 prescribed the Pupil-Teacher Ratio (PTR) for Special Educators in Inclusive Schools.
points.text: 345. An amendment to the RTE Act in September 2022 prescribed the Pupil-Teacher Ratio (PTR) for Special Educators in Inclusive Schools. The RTE Act mandates a Pupil-Teacher Ratio for Special Education Teachers: One for every 10 CwSN enrolled in Class I–V, and one for every 15 CwSN enrolled in Class VI– VIII.

points.title: Additionally, the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education (Amendment) Rules, 2024, notified in December 2024, introd…
points.text: 346. Additionally, the Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education (Amendment) Rules, 2024, notified in December 2024, introduced provisions allowing states to conduct regular examinations for Classes 5 and 8 at the end of the academic year. Students who fail these exams will receive additional instruction and an opportunity for re-examination within two months. If they still fail to meet promotion criteria, they may be held back in Class 5 or Class 8. This represents a significant shift from the previous no-detention policy.

points.title: Children with disabilities such as autism, mental retardation and cerebral palsy have been included under the RTE Act and the f…
points.text: 347. Children with disabilities such as autism, mental retardation and cerebral palsy have been included under the RTE Act and the following amendments to the RTE Act were made under Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education (Amendment) Act, 2012: a. Including children with disabilities within the meaning of 'children belonging to disadvantaged groups'. b. Including children with autism, Cerebral Palsy, Mental Retardation and Multiple Disabilities as per the National Trust for Welfare of Persons with Autism, Cerebral Palsy, Mental Retardation and Multiple Disabilities Act, 1999 to pursue free and compulsory elementary education under the RTE Act. c. Clarifying under Section 3(1) that every child of the age of six from fourteen years, including children belonging to disadvantaged groups and weaker sections, shall have the right to free and compulsory education in neighbourhood schools till completion of elementary education. d. Incorporating a provision that "a child with 'multiple disabilities' referred to in clause (h) of section 2 of the National Trust for Welfare of Persons with Autism, Cerebral Palsy, Mental Retardation and Multiple Disabilities Act, 1999, and a child with 'severe disability' referred to in clause (o) of section 2 of the same Act will also have the right to opt for home-based education". ● Enrollment and Non-Exclusion from General Education System

points.title: The RPwD Act, 2016 outlines specific duties for educational institutions to ensure CwSN are not excluded from the general syste…
points.text: 348. The RPwD Act, 2016 outlines specific duties for educational institutions to ensure CwSN are not excluded from the general system: Educational institutions funded or recognized by the Government shall admit CwSN without discrimination and provide education and opportunities for sports and recreation activities equally with others. For children with benchmark disabilities, the RPwD Act, 2016, mandates free education in a neighborhood school, or in a special school, of their choice, until the age of eighteen years. a. In Andaman & Nicobar Islands (A&N), all 754 identified CwSN children in the age group of 3-18 are enrolled in mainstream education. The dropout rate in school education is reported as Nil. The enrollment breakdown for A&N Islands (Total 754) is: Pre-primary: 40; Primary: 248; Upper Primary: 208; Secondary: 152; Senior Secondary: 106. b. In Chandigarh, 3686 CWSN were enrolled in Government and Government Aided schools (2023-24). c. In Meghalaya, 2459 PwDs students were admitted to Government and Government Aided Schools (UDISE+ 2023-24). d. In Uttarakhand, 2754 CWSN are enrolled at the elementary level and 500 CWSN are enrolled at the secondary level. e. In Uttar Pradesh, enrollment data shows 580 students at Primary level and 933 at Secondary level. f. In Goa, under Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan, total PwDs beneficiaries between 2017–2022 has increased from 858 in 2017-18 to 1651 in 2021-22. ● Provision of Assistive Devices and Accessible Infrastructure

points.title: Children With Special Needs (CWsN) are being provided with assistive devices and barrier free access.
points.text: 349. Children With Special Needs (CWsN) are being provided with assistive devices and barrier free access. 3.301 million CWsN (81.26%) requiring aids and appliances under Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan (SSA), have been provided with assistive devices through various modes. SSA programme ensures that every child in the age group of 6-14 years with special needs, irrespective of the kind, category and degree of disability, is provided with a meaningful and quality education. Under the Samagra Shiksha initiative (which consolidated multiple centrally sponsored education schemes), therapeutic interventions at the block level are provided. Under Samagra Shiksha, individualized support is provided through therapeutic interventions at the block level, and Braille books and large print books are provided for children with visual impairment. Additionally, the Government notified the Accessibility Code for Educational Institutions on January 10, 2024, which was notified in the RPWD Act Rules on 20 June 2024. The Code examines physical barriers and information and communication barriers to school facility access for children with special needs and provides child-friendly standards with cost-effective solutions for both existing and new buildings. The Ministry of Education Gazette Notified the Accessibility Code of Educational Institutions (January 10, 2024), which provides cross-disabilities and child-friendly standards and cost-effective solutions for existing and new buildings.

points.title: Schools are being made more disabled friendly by incorporating barrier free features in their designs.
points.text: 350. Schools are being made more disabled friendly by incorporating barrier free features in their designs. Another recent initiative of SSA has been to provide disabled friendly toilets. So far, 82.33% schools have been equipped with ramps and handrails and 14.82% schools have been provided with disabled friendly toilets under SSA. In Chandigarh, 94.64% of schools have ramps and handrails, and 82.75% of schools have disabled-friendly toilets. In Uttar Pradesh, ramps are available in 132,275 Government schools, and disabled-friendly toilets are available in 107,554 Government schools (through convergence with Gram panchayats). In Mizoram, ramps with hand-railings were constructed in 114 Government Schools (2017– 2022), and CwSN toilets were constructed in 264 Government Schools. The Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan), launched by the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities on 3 December 2015, mandates accessibility standards in public institutions including educational facilities. The campaign focuses on three verticals: built environment, transportation systems, and information and communication technology (ICT). Rule 15 of the RPWD Rules, 2017 has been amended in June 2024 to expand its scope to include educational institutions, mandating institutions to identify barriers preventing children with disabilities from fully participating in educational settings and encouraging development and implementation of cost-effective, sustainable accessibility solutions. ● Transport, Escort, and Individualized Support Services

points.title: Under Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan (in Chandigarh and Goa, 2017-2022), PwDs were beneficiaries of Transport/Escort Allowance, Therapeu…
points.text: 351. Under Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan (in Chandigarh and Goa, 2017-2022), PwDs were beneficiaries of Transport/Escort Allowance, Therapeutic Support, and Home Based Education for those with severe/profound disabilities. In Chandigarh, financial data shows expenditure on Transport Allowance (e.g., Rs. 3.50 lakhs to 140 beneficiaries in 2017-18) and funds for the Presence of Attendants (e.g., Rs. 9.58 lakhs for 15 attendants in 2018-19).

points.title: In Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Transport/Escort allowance is provided to eligible CwSN students.
points.text: 352. In Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Transport/Escort allowance is provided to eligible CwSN students. Parents/guardians are permitted to attend CwSN students within the school premises, and Helpers are engaged in 9 Block Resource Rooms.

points.title: In Uttar Pradesh, Transport Allowance is approved for 980 CwSN and Escort Allowance for 745 CwSN (at Rs.
points.text: 353. In Uttar Pradesh, Transport Allowance is approved for 980 CwSN and Escort Allowance for 745 CwSN (at Rs. 5000 per CwSN, total outlay Rs. 49.00 lakh and Rs. 37.25 lakh, respectively) for the year 2025-26.

points.title: A Handbook on Home-Based Education for Parents of children with severe/multiple disabilities was provided to 10,181 parents in…
points.text: 354. A Handbook on Home-Based Education for Parents of children with severe/multiple disabilities was provided to 10,181 parents in Uttar Pradesh in 2022-23.

points.title: In Mizoram (2017-2022), Braille sheets and Braille Text books were provided to government schools, with an expenditure of Rs.
points.text: 355. In Mizoram (2017-2022), Braille sheets and Braille Text books were provided to government schools, with an expenditure of Rs. 54.571 lakhs for paper/stationary and Rs. 0.32648 lakhs for Braille textbooks.

points.title: In Andaman & Nicobar Islands, CwSN centric teaching methods are adopted to enhance the learning outcome of students.
points.text: 356. In Andaman & Nicobar Islands, CwSN centric teaching methods are adopted to enhance the learning outcome of students. In Chandigarh, curriculum modifications are implemented based on Individualized Education Plans (IEPs).

points.title: The AICTE Guidelines mandate the provision of scribes for students who have difficulty writing (including visual impairments) a…
points.text: 357. The AICTE Guidelines mandate the provision of scribes for students who have difficulty writing (including visual impairments) and an extension of time up to 20 minutes per hour.

points.title: The Supreme Court affirmed that reasonable accommodation must be extended to persons with disabilities regardless of whether th…
points.text: 358. The Supreme Court affirmed that reasonable accommodation must be extended to persons with disabilities regardless of whether they have a benchmark disability, stressing the non-discriminatory nature of support. The Kerala High Court ruled that benefits like a scribe and extra time should be extended to a student with dyslexia and borderline IQ (25% disability), extending provisions to those below the 40% benchmark. Judicial orders reinforce that denying a reasonable accommodation, such as providing a scribe, constitutes discrimination, irrespective of whether the disability is classified as "benchmark" (e.g., for dysgraphia or writer's cramp).

points.title: Teacher Training under SSA: According to the Ministry of Human Resource Development (MHRD) Annual Report 2013-14, under SSA in…
points.text: 359. Teacher Training under SSA: According to the Ministry of Human Resource Development (MHRD) Annual Report 2013-14, under SSA in 2013-14, 3.2 million teachers have been approved by MHRD for in-service training and 0.2 million teachers for induction training. The Teacher preparation in Special Education (TEPSE) Scheme is meant for assisting the Department of Education to launch special education teacher preparation programmes to prepare special teachers to teach children with disabilities in both special and inclusive settings. The scheme provides financial assistance to offer B.Ed. and M.Ed. degree courses with specialization in one of the disability areas. As per the Annual Report of Ministry of Human Resource and Development, 2013-14, 43 'Special Awards' have been earmarked for the teachers of following categories:

points.title: Each award carries with it a certificate of merit, a cash award of 416 US Dollar and a Silver Medal.
points.text: 360. Each award carries with it a certificate of merit, a cash award of 416 US Dollar and a Silver Medal. 16 teachers for promoting education of children with disabilities in integrated/ inclusive education in schools were awarded with the National Award. The National Education Policy 2020 further emphasizes teacher training and continuous professional development (CPD) for inclusive education. The policy mandates that educators be proficient in at least two languages and introduces specialized shorter local teacher education programs and post-B.Ed. certification courses for special educators, accessible through open and distance learning platforms. The Integrated Special and Inclusive Teacher Education Program (ISITEP), developed by the Rehabilitation Council of India in alignment with NEP 2020, provides a comprehensive curriculum framework for teacher preparation addressing disability-specific and inclusive education needs. NEP 2020 also recommends that specialized training programs equip teachers with skills to support students with disabilities using assistive technology, inclusive pedagogy, and creating inclusive learning environments.

points.title: The National Initiative for School Heads' and Teachers' Holistic Advancement (NISHTHA) program includes a dedicated module on i…
points.text: 361. The National Initiative for School Heads' and Teachers' Holistic Advancement (NISHTHA) program includes a dedicated module on inclusive education to build competencies among teachers and school principals. NCERT regularly organizes capacity building programs for teachers on various themes, including school accessibility, Universal Design for Learning (UDL), differential instructions, and supportive services. NCERT conducts a live interaction series called "Teaching Learning Interventions for Inclusive Classrooms" where each episode includes a mandatory ISL interpreter.

points.title: The Delhi High Court directed all recognized aided and unaided private schools in Delhi to appoint Special Educators and mandat…
points.text: 362. The Delhi High Court directed all recognized aided and unaided private schools in Delhi to appoint Special Educators and mandated the State to ensure their prompt provision.

points.title: In Andaman & Nicobar Islands, a proposal was submitted to the Ministry of Education for the creation of 99 Special Educator posts.
points.text: 363. In Andaman & Nicobar Islands, a proposal was submitted to the Ministry of Education for the creation of 99 Special Educator posts. The administration currently employs 12 PSRT, 12 Special Educators, and 16 BRP (CwSN). In Chandigarh, 63 Special Educators are employed under the Samagra Shiksha scheme. In Uttarakhand, 380 regular posts for Special Education Teachers have been sanctioned for elementary education and 285 posts for secondary education. Sign language training was given for 329 teachers, and 95 teachers received advanced level sign language training. In Uttar Pradesh (Basic Education), training on Braille reading-writing, sign language, and intellectual disability was provided to 2167 special educators in 2020-21. Training on Cross disability was provided to 2062 special educators in 2021-22. In Mizoram, 4 Special Educators at the Secondary stage and 1 at the Elementary Education level were recruited between 2017-2022, with an additional 4 Special Educators recruited in 2023 to strengthen Inclusive Education. Inclusive Education is taught as part of the curriculum in B.Ed. (4th Semester) and M.Ed. programs in 1 institution.

points.title: Status of education of children with disabilities through Open Schooling: The National Institute of Open Schooling an autonomou…
points.text: 364. Status of education of children with disabilities through Open Schooling: The National Institute of Open Schooling an autonomous organization under MHRD provides education to CWSN through 85 Special Accredited Institutions for the Education of Disadvantaged located in different states. Special provisions are made for the examination of learners with disabilities. Computers with adapted hardware are allowed. Where required, examinations are conducted at the residence of learners. The National Institute of Open Schooling (NIOS) introduced Indian Sign Language (ISL) as a language subject at the Secondary level for Deaf and Hard of Hearing Learners.

points.title: The Central Advisory Board of Education (CABE) is the highest advisory body to the Central and State government in the field of…
points.text: 365. The Central Advisory Board of Education (CABE) is the highest advisory body to the Central and State government in the field of education. The committee report on the Universalization of Secondary Education (June, 2005) recommended that the guiding principle of Universal Secondary Education should be Universal Access, Equality and Social Justice, relevance and Development, and Structural and Curricular Considerations. The CABE Committee Report on "Girls' Education and the Common School System" has recommended making the curriculum flexible and appropriate to accommodate the diversity of school children, including those with disability in both cognitive and noncognitive areas.

points.title: National Means-cum-Merit Scholarship (NMMS) Scheme was launched in June 2008 with a provision to award 1 lakh scholarships ever…
points.text: 366. National Means-cum-Merit Scholarship (NMMS) Scheme was launched in June 2008 with a provision to award 1 lakh scholarships every year to selected candidates in Classes IX–XII. Some State Government also provide scholarships to students with disabilities from standard I to VIII i.e. upto 14 years.

points.title: KendriyaVidyalaya/NavodayaVidyalaya: Under this 3% reservation in admission and exemption from payment of Vidyalaya Vikas Nidhi…
points.text: 367: KendriyaVidyalaya/NavodayaVidyalaya: Under this 3% reservation in admission and exemption from payment of Vidyalaya Vikas Nidhi and tuition fee is provided to students with disabilities, covered in the PWDA.

points.title: Upgradation of Merit of ST Students: The objective of the scheme is to upgrade the merit of ST students by providing them remed…
points.text: 368: Upgradation of Merit of ST Students: The objective of the scheme is to upgrade the merit of ST students by providing them remedials and special coaching in classes from IX to XII. It targets 3% disabled SC/ST students.

points.title: All Government institutions of higher education and institutions receiving aid shall reserve not less than five per cent.
points.text: 369. All Government institutions of higher education and institutions receiving aid shall reserve not less than five per cent. seats for persons with benchmark disabilities under the RPwD Act, 2016.

points.title: University Grants Commission has issued instructions to all universities and colleges for providing 3% reservation (horizontall…
points.text: 370. University Grants Commission has issued instructions to all universities and colleges for providing 3% reservation (horizontally) in admission for PwD students, reservation of 3% for the PwDs in the appointment of Lecturers and in admission of to courses, relaxation of 5% for NET admission, and relaxation in upper age limit upto 5 years to PwDs in admissions. 320 disabled students are enrolled in higher education in Uttarakhand. In Uttar Pradesh, 2703 students with disabilities are enrolled in higher education.

points.title: All AICTE approved institutions are mandated to implement reservation related to the admission of PwD students.
points.text: 371. All AICTE approved institutions are mandated to implement reservation related to the admission of PwD students. In Chandigarh, 5% reservation is given in admissions in Higher Education Institutions (HEIs). In Uttarakhand, all higher education institutes are implementing the mandatory 5% reservation for PwDs. Uttar Pradesh has also implemented a 5% reservation for PwDs in Higher Education Institutions.

points.text: 372. Schemes and programmes by UGC:

points.title: The implementation of Barrier-free environment provisions is an essential requirement for granting approval to technical instit…
points.text: 373. The implementation of Barrier-free environment provisions is an essential requirement for granting approval to technical institutions by the AICTE. In Uttar Pradesh Higher Education, campus accessibility efforts include an accessibility ramp for smooth entry, and internal spaces designed with wide, obstruction-free aisles and open reading zones for unhindered wheelchair access. A curated collection of Braille books and other accessible learning materials is available. In Mizoram, Braille in library software is available at one college, and Braille signboards are available at two colleges.

points.title: Upgradation of existing Polytechnics to integrate physically challenged persons: This Scheme has been formulated with the aim t…
points.text: 374. Upgradation of existing Polytechnics to integrate physically challenged persons: This Scheme has been formulated with the aim to integrate physically PwDs into maintaining technical and vocational education. 50 existing polytechnics have been selected for upgradation and each polytechnic is allowed to admit 25 students in diploma programmes whereas under non-formal programmes (upto six months duration) about 100 students are provided vocational training. In the polytechnics, the enrolment of PwD students during the year 2013-14 was as detailed in the following table.

points.title: Indira Gandhi National Open University (IGNOU): IGNOU has been established by an Act of Parliament in 1985.
points.text: 375. Indira Gandhi National Open University (IGNOU): IGNOU has been established by an Act of Parliament in 1985. Open and Distance Learning system in the Indira Gandhi National Open University (IGNOU) set up the National Centre for Disability Studies in 2006. IGNOU provides several diploma and certificate courses for PwDs. The measures taken by IGNOU, to ensure the involvement of persons with disabilities in the higher education and various courses offered by IGNOU, are annexed at Annexure 3. IGNOU has established a special study centre for students with disabilities and is a member of Sugamya Pustakalaya, allowing students with blindness, low vision, or any other print disability to access around 3.5 lakh books.

points.title: Establishment of Equal Opportunity Cells in Universities: To make colleges and universities more responsive to the needs and co…
points.text: 376. Establishment of Equal Opportunity Cells in Universities: To make colleges and universities more responsive to the needs and constraints of the disadvantaged social groups, the Commission has financed Institutions to establish Equal Opportunity Cells to see the effective implementation of policies and programmes for disadvantaged groups and to provide guidance and counselling in academic, financial, social and other matters.

points.title: National Mission on Education through Information and Communication Technology: The Mission launched by the Ministry of Human R…
points.text: 377. National Mission on Education through Information and Communication Technology: The Mission launched by the Ministry of Human Resource Development has been envisaged for the benefit of all the learners in Higher Education Institutions. In order to facilitate the PwD students, the focused interventions have been incorporated in the Mission are Unicode font to provide support in various languages and for visually impaired students. The National Education Policy 2020 emphasizes the utilization of platforms like SWAYAM and DIKSHA to upskill teachers and provide accessible online learning to students with disabilities.

points.title: National Scholarships for PwD: Under this scheme, the MoSJE awards 500 new scholarships every year for pursuing post matriculat…
points.text: 378. National Scholarships for PwD: Under this scheme, the MoSJE awards 500 new scholarships every year for pursuing post matriculation, professional and technical courses of more than one year duration. With respect to students with cerebral palsy, mental retardation, multiple disabilities and people with profound or severe hearing impairment, scholarships are awarded for pursuing studies from IX standard onwards.

points.title: Students with 40% or more disabilities whose monthly family income does not exceed 250 USD are eligible for scholarship.
points.text: 379. Students with 40% or more disabilities whose monthly family income does not exceed 250 USD are eligible for scholarship. The scholarship is provided to the students pursuing Graduate and Post Graduate level courses; pursuing diploma and certificate level professional courses. In addition, the students are also reimbursed with the course fee. Financial assistance under the scheme is also given for computer editing software for blind/ deaf graduate and postgraduate students pursuing professional courses and for support access software for cerebral palsy students.

points.title: GyanPrabha Scholarship Scheme: Under the scheme, financial assistance is provided to the disabled to pursue vocational training…
points.text: 380. GyanPrabha Scholarship Scheme: Under the scheme, financial assistance is provided to the disabled to pursue vocational training/professional courses leading to skill development and employment for PwDs. The scheme was launched to provide financial assistance with the monthly family income limit of 250 US Dollar and the condition of minimum 50% marks for renewal of the scholarship for every year has been waived.

points.title: Scholarship and other schemes of State Governments for Higher Education: Various state governments extend scholarships to PwDs…
points.text: 381. Scholarship and other schemes of State Governments for Higher Education: Various state governments extend scholarships to PwDs for Graduation, Post Graduation and Engineering and Professional courses that range from 2 US Dollar to 25 US Dollar per month. Besides these Readers Allowance from 1.61 US Dollar to 26 US Dollar per month and Hostel allowance is offered ranging from 1.61 US Dollar to 3.87 US Dollar per month is for graduation, post graduation and professional courses.

points.title: The Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Grameen Kaushalya Yojana (DDU-GKY) program focuses on rural poor youth, emphasizing sustainable employ…
points.text: 382. The Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Grameen Kaushalya Yojana (DDU-GKY) program focuses on rural poor youth, emphasizing sustainable employment.
points.subsection: F. Vocational Training and Lifelong Learning

points.title: The Rural Self Employment Training Institutes (RSETIs) are mandated to provide equal access and training opportunities to PwD c…
points.text: 383. The Rural Self Employment Training Institutes (RSETIs) are mandated to provide equal access and training opportunities to PwD candidates across all eligible trades. They are required to ensure barrier-free infrastructure and provide necessary support like sign language interpreters and assistive devices.
points.subsection: F. Vocational Training and Lifelong Learning

points.title: The AICTE has developed curriculum modules (Courses I, II, and III) focusing on Disability, Accessibility, and Universal Design…
points.text: 384. The AICTE has developed curriculum modules (Courses I, II, and III) focusing on Disability, Accessibility, and Universal Design for Civil Engineering and Architecture students, aimed at promoting awareness of creating accessible built environments.
points.subsection: F. Vocational Training and Lifelong Learning

points.title: The NIHH runs the Indian Sign Language Cell for the development of teaching materials to teach Indian Sign Language (ISL), trai…
points.text: 385. The NIHH runs the Indian Sign Language Cell for the development of teaching materials to teach Indian Sign Language (ISL), training of ISL interpreters, to train deaf persons to become sign language teachers, sign language training for hearing staff at educational institutions, for hearing handicapped parents and family members and documentation of sign language vocabulary used in different regions of the country and their variations. Building on these initiatives, the Government of India launched the PM e-Vidya DTH ISL Channel No. 31 on 6 December 2024. This dedicated channel provides education content in Indian Sign Language for children with hearing impairments, special educators, and ISL interpreters. The channel broadcasts NCERT textbook-based learning content and focuses on career guidance, skill training, mental health, communication skills, and class-specific curricular content. The channel aligns with NEP 2020's vision of creating an inclusive educational environment and supports the standardization of ISL across India as a medium of instruction and as a school subject in its own right.
points.subsection: G. Facilitating learning through Augmentative and Alternative Modes

points.title: The proceedings of meetings/conferences/programmes are supported with sign language interpretation in Higher Education Institut…
points.text: 386. The proceedings of meetings/conferences/programmes are supported with sign language interpretation in Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) advised by the UGC. A letter was issued on July 29, 2022 in Chandigarh, directing all Heads of Departments, Boards, and Corporations to appoint a sign language interpreter during meetings, seminars, workshops, and press briefings. In Uttarakhand, sign language interpretation is mandatory in seminars, workshops, and events like World Disability Day. Special Schools run by NGOs are contributing to the promotion of Indian Sign Language. The feasibility of offering Indian Sign Language as the third language is proposed in the State of Goa.
points.subsection: G. Facilitating learning through Augmentative and Alternative Modes

points.title: Article 47 of the Constitution of India lays down that “the State shall regard rise in the level of nutrition and the standard…
points.text: 387. Article 47 of the Constitution of India lays down that “the State shall regard rise in the level of nutrition and the standard of living of its people and the improvement of public health …among its primary duties.” Though this article is a directive principle, the Supreme Court of India has viewed right to medical care and medical aid to be integral components of the right to life. The Patna High Court has further affirmed that the right to health is fundamental, ordering the state of Bihar to ensure Thalassemia treatment and blood bank facilities are available in all districts.
points.subsection: A. Constitutional Provisions, Law and Policies

points.title: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act), which came into force on 19 April 2017, replaced the Persons with…
points.text: 388. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act), which came into force on 19 April 2017, replaced the Persons with Disabilities Act, 1995. The 2016 Act expanded the definition of disabilities from 7 to 21 categories, including mental illness, autism spectrum disorders, specific learning disabilities, blood disorders (thalassemia, haemophilia, and sickle cell disease), dwarfism, muscular dystrophy, acid attack victims, and Parkinson’s disease. The RPwD Act adopts a rights-based, biopsychosocial model of disability in line with India’s obligations under the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD), ratified in 2007.
points.subsection: A. Constitutional Provisions, Law and Policies

points.title: The National Health Policy 2017 replaced the 2002 policy and aims to achieve the highest possible level of health and wellbeing…
points.text: 389. The National Health Policy 2017 replaced the 2002 policy and aims to achieve the highest possible level of health and wellbeing for all at all ages, through preventive and promotive health care orientation and universal access to good quality health care services without financial hardship. The policy explicitly recognizes disability as a neglected health issue requiring focused research and intervention, and recommends expansion of interventions to include early detection and response to early childhood development delays and disability. The policy commits to building community support and offering good healthcare to vulnerable sections including persons with disabilities.
points.subsection: A. Constitutional Provisions, Law and Policies

points.title: The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, which came into force on 29 May 2018, provides a rights-based statutory framework for mental h…
points.text: 390. The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, which came into force on 29 May 2018, provides a rights-based statutory framework for mental health in India. The Act strengthens equality and equity in provision of mental healthcare services, decriminalizes attempted suicide (previously punishable under Section 309 of the Indian Penal Code), and introduces key provisions including the right to advance directives allowing persons to specify their treatment preferences, the concept of nominated representatives, and the requirement that persons with mental illness be treated equally with persons with physical illness in healthcare provision. The Act mandates insurers to provide medical insurance for mental illness on the same basis as physical ailments. Central and State Mental Health Authorities and Mental Health Review Boards have been established under this Act. Judicial enforcement has reinforced state obligations in this area; for instance, the Madras High Court ruled in Gurunatha v. Deputy Director that the State is legally obligated to provide custody and medical care for persons with severe mental illness when families are unable to manage.
points.subsection: A. Constitutional Provisions, Law and Policies

points.title: Affordable Health: General health services are the primary responsibility of the States along with the Central Government focus…
points.text: 391. Affordable Health: General health services are the primary responsibility of the States along with the Central Government focusing on medical education, drugs, population stabilisation and disease control. The RPwD Act, 2016 under Section 25 mandates that both government and private hospitals must provide healthcare and ensure all areas are accessible to persons with disabilities. The appropriate governments and local authorities are required to:
points.subsection: A. Constitutional Provisions, Law and Policies

points.title: Providing health care in proximity to community: The National Health Mission (NHM), comprising the National Rural Health Missio…
points.text: 392. Providing health care in proximity to community: The National Health Mission (NHM), comprising the National Rural Health Mission and the National Urban Health Mission, continues to provide quality healthcare in both rural and urban areas. The Ayushman Bharat programme, launched in 2018, has two components: (i) Health and Wellness Centres providing comprehensive primary healthcare, and (ii) Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PM-JAY), the world’s largest health assurance scheme providing coverage of Rs. 5 lakhs per family per year for secondary and tertiary care hospitalization to over 12 crore poor and vulnerable families (approximately 55 crore beneficiaries). PM-JAY covers pre-existing diseases from day one and has no cap on family size or age. Under the SECC-2011 criteria, families with “disabled member and no able-bodied adult member” are included. In September 2024, the scheme was expanded to cover all senior citizens aged 70 and above regardless of income. However, advocacy groups have noted that approximately 80% of persons with disabilities lack health insurance coverage, and over 50% of disability-related insurance applications face rejection.
points.subsection: A. Constitutional Provisions, Law and Policies

points.title: The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) issued Master Circulars in 2024 directing all health and li…
points.text: 393. The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) issued Master Circulars in 2024 directing all health and life insurers to offer specific products for persons with disabilities, individuals with HIV/AIDS, and those with mental illness. Insurers are prohibited from narrowing coverage beyond minimum standards set by model products and must publicly disclose their underwriting philosophy for these groups. Judicial intervention has been instrumental in advancing insurance rights for PwDs; the Delhi High Court in Saurabh Shukla v. Max Bupa ruled that PwDs are entitled to health insurance and directed IRDAI to remove discriminatory terminology such as “substandard lives” from regulations.

points.title: India has stressed on early identification and prevention of disabilities through multiple national programmes.
points.text: 394. India has stressed on early identification and prevention of disabilities through multiple national programmes. The Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK), launched under the National Health Mission, provides systematic Child Health Screening and Early Intervention Services covering children from birth to 18 years for the “4 Ds” – Defects at Birth, Diseases in Children, Deficiency Conditions, and Developmental Delays including Disabilities. The programme provides for twice-yearly screenings in Anganwadis for children aged 0-6 years, and annual screenings in government and government-aided schools for children aged 6-18 years, covering approximately 27 crore children being screened annually. Dedicated teams comprising two medical officers (one male, one female), one ANM/Staff Nurse, and one Pharmacist with designated vehicles are deployed, and District Early Intervention Centres (DEICs) are established for comprehensive assessment and management of identified cases. DEICs have been deployed at the state level. In Uttar Pradesh, DEICs provide physiotherapy, speech therapy, and parental counselling services. A Paediatric Neuro Rehabilitation Centre has been established at Goa Medical College, and a DEIC has been established in the new OPD block of the Government Multispecialty Hospital in Chandigarh. Children diagnosed with conditions receive free treatment including surgical interventions at tertiary level.

points.title: Chapter V of the RPwD Act, 2016 on Social Security, Health, Rehabilitation and Recreation provides that within the limits of th…
points.text: 395. Chapter V of the RPwD Act, 2016 on Social Security, Health, Rehabilitation and Recreation provides that within the limits of their economic capacity and development, the appropriate Governments and the local authorities, with a view to preventing the occurrence of disabilities, shall:

points.title: Training and Capacity Building: Regular sensitization programmes are conducted for ASHA (Accredited Social Health Activist) and…
points.text: 396. Training and Capacity Building: Regular sensitization programmes are conducted for ASHA (Accredited Social Health Activist) and Anganwadi workers, primary health workers, and teachers on the identification and causes of disabilities. National Institutes under the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities continue to provide specialized services including genetic counselling, rehabilitation, and awareness programmes on reproductive health for persons with disabilities.

points.title: The National Trust continues to implement early intervention and other welfare schemes for persons with autism, cerebral palsy,…
points.text: 397. The National Trust continues to implement early intervention and other welfare schemes for persons with autism, cerebral palsy, intellectual disability, and multiple disabilities under the National Trust Act, 1999.

points.title: States report year-wise expenditure on several national health initiatives relevant to disability prevention and care:
points.text: 398. States report year-wise expenditure on several national health initiatives relevant to disability prevention and care:
points.subsection: D. Financial Allocations and Public Health Programmes

points.title: Scheme of Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids and Appliances (ADIP Scheme): The ADIP Scheme, in operati…
points.text: 399. Scheme of Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids and Appliances (ADIP Scheme): The ADIP Scheme, in operation since 1981 and last revised in September 2024, provides durable, scientifically manufactured aids and appliances to persons with disabilities to promote physical, social and psychological rehabilitation. The scheme covers persons holding 40% benchmark disability certificate with monthly income not exceeding Rs. 30,000. The ARJUN (ADIP & RVY Joint Interface for Unique Nomination) portal enables online applications. Benefits include:
points.subsection: D. Financial Allocations and Public Health Programmes

points.title: Janani Suraksha Yojana (JSY) under the National Health Mission is a safe motherhood intervention scheme, implemented with the o…
points.text: 400. Janani Suraksha Yojana (JSY) under the National Health Mission is a safe motherhood intervention scheme, implemented with the objective of reducing maternal and neo-natal mortality by promoting institutional delivery among poor pregnant women. India’s Maternal Mortality Ratio has declined from 254 per 100,000 live births in 2004-2006 to 97 per 100,000 in 2018-2020, with institutional deliveries increasing from approximately 47% in 2005-06 to 88% in recent years. Accredited Social Health Activists (ASHAs) serve as critical links in implementing the scheme. Additionally, Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK), launched in 2011, entitles every pregnant woman to free delivery including caesarean section in public health institutions, along with free transport, diagnostics, medicines, blood, consumables and diet. The scheme covers women with disabilities generically; there is no explicit inclusion. Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan (PMSMA) provides pregnant women a fixed day, free of cost assured and quality antenatal checkup on the 9th of every month.
points.subsection: E. Maternal and Early Childhood Care

points.title: The Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojana (RSBY) has been subsumed under the Ayushman Bharat - Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PM-JA…
points.text: 401. The Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojana (RSBY) has been subsumed under the Ayushman Bharat - Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PM-JAY) launched in September 2018. PM-JAY provides cashless healthcare coverage of up to Rs. 5 lakhs per eligible family per year for secondary and tertiary hospitalization to approximately 12 crore families constituting the bottom 40% of India’s population. The scheme beneficiary base includes poor and vulnerable families identified in the Socio-Economic Caste Census (SECC-2011), which incorporates deprivation criteria including “disabled member and no able-bodied adult member.” PM-JAY covers treatment across over 25,000 empanelled hospitals (both government and private) throughout the country.
points.subsection: E. Maternal and Early Childhood Care

points.title: Niramaya Scheme is a health insurance scheme dedicated specifically to the welfare of persons with autism, cerebral palsy, inte…
points.text: 402. Niramaya Scheme is a health insurance scheme dedicated specifically to the welfare of persons with autism, cerebral palsy, intellectual disability and multiple disabilities under the National Trust Act. The Insurance cover is up to Rs. 1.0 lakh on reimbursement basis. Persons can enrol by paying a premium of Rs. 500 (APL categories) or Rs. 250 (BPL categories) for new enrolment, and Rs. 250 or Rs. 50 respectively for renewal. The scheme provides comprehensive coverage including OPD treatment, medicines, diagnostic tests, regular medical check-ups, dental care, corrective surgeries, ongoing therapies, alternative medicine, and transportation costs. No pre-insurance medical tests are required. All persons with disabilities under the National Trust Act with valid UDID card are eligible.
points.subsection: E. Maternal and Early Childhood Care

points.title: The Unique Disability ID (UDID) Project, launched by the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities, creates a comp…
points.text: 403. The Unique Disability ID (UDID) Project, launched by the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities, creates a comprehensive national database for persons with disabilities. The project issues Universal ID Cards and Disability Certificates through a centralized web application (swavlambancard.gov.in). The UDID card serves as a single document of identification for availing various government benefits and schemes, eliminating the need for multiple documents. Disability certificates are issued within one month of application through designated medical authorities and boards. The system facilitates streamlined delivery of government benefits across all 21 categories of disabilities recognized under the RPwD Act, 2016.
points.subsection: E. Maternal and Early Childhood Care

points.title: The Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan), launched on 3 December 2015, aims to achieve universal accessibility fo…
points.text: 404. The Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan), launched on 3 December 2015, aims to achieve universal accessibility for persons with disabilities across three domains: built environment, transportation, and information and communication technology. Progress achieved includes:
points.subsection: E. Maternal and Early Childhood Care

points.title: The campaign’s objectives have been integrated into the Scheme for Implementation of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Ac…
points.text: 405. The campaign’s objectives have been integrated into the Scheme for Implementation of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act (SIPDA). The Sugamya Bharat App enables citizens to report accessibility-related issues in 23 languages.
points.subsection: E. Maternal and Early Childhood Care

points.title: Psychosocial Support in Disaster Management: The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) Guidelines of 2023 emphasize tha…
points.text: 406. Psychosocial Support in Disaster Management: The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) Guidelines of 2023 emphasize that Mental Health and Psychosocial Support Services (MHPSS) must be accessible through Braille, sign language, and easy-to-read formats during all phases of disaster management, ensuring persons with disabilities receive appropriate support during emergencies.
points.subsection: E. Maternal and Early Childhood Care

points.title: The Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities released a Draft National Policy for Persons with Disabilities in 20…
points.text: 407. The Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities released a Draft National Policy for Persons with Disabilities in 2022 to replace the 2006 policy. The draft policy proposes comprehensive interventions in disability prevention, education, healthcare, social security, and accessibility. On health, the draft calls for a comprehensive national programme on prevention of disabilities beyond traditional causes to include malnourishment, medical negligence, socio-cultural factors, and impairment caused by disasters. The draft recommends that the National Health Programme be upscaled to address disability-specific healthcare needs, inclusion of disability modules in MBBS and other medical courses, and involvement of PwDs in planning and developing healthcare services.
points.subsection: E. Maternal and Early Childhood Care

points.title: Indian Medical Council (Professional conduct, Etiquette and Ethics) Regulations, 2002 vide Rule 2.3 mandates that physicians mu…
points.text: 408. Indian Medical Council (Professional conduct, Etiquette and Ethics) Regulations, 2002 vide Rule 2.3 mandates that physicians must ensure patients have adequate knowledge of their condition. The RPwD Act, 2016 specifically provides that no person with disability shall be subjected to medical procedure without free and informed consent (Section 8), and no person with disability shall be subjected to research without informed consent. The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 reinforces informed consent as a fundamental principle, providing that every person with mental illness has a right to make decisions regarding their healthcare and treatment given appropriate knowledge, and introduces advance directives allowing persons to specify their future treatment preferences while they have capacity to do so.
points.subsection: E. Maternal and Early Childhood Care

points.title: The RPwD Act, 2016 recognizes the legal capacity of persons with disabilities on an equal basis with others in all aspects of l…
points.text: 409. The RPwD Act, 2016 recognizes the legal capacity of persons with disabilities on an equal basis with others in all aspects of life, including the right to own or inherit property, control financial affairs, and access financial credit. The Act introduces the concept of limited guardianship for persons who require support in making legally binding decisions, replacing the previous system of plenary guardianship. A limited guardian takes decisions on behalf of the person with disability in consultation with such person, for specific periods and specific decisions. The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 similarly emphasizes capacity assessment and provides that every person, including persons with mental illness, shall be deemed to have capacity to make decisions regarding their mental healthcare unless determined otherwise.
points.subsection: E. Maternal and Early Childhood Care

points.title: Section 2 (za) of the RPwD Act, 2016 defined rehabilitation as “a process aimed at enabling PwDs to reach and maintain their op…
points.text: 410. Section 2 (za) of the RPwD Act, 2016 defined rehabilitation as “a process aimed at enabling PwDs to reach and maintain their optimal physical, sensory, intellectual, psychiatric or social functional levels”. Section 66(1) of the RPwD Act, 2016 required that “the Appropriate Governments and the local authorities shall within the limits of their economic capacity and development undertake or cause to be undertaken rehabilitation of all persons with disabilities”. The RPwD Act expanded recognized disabilities from 7 to 21 categories, including autism spectrum disorder, cerebral palsy, chronic neurological conditions, and multiple disabilities, thereby providing a more comprehensive framework for rehabilitation services.

points.title: Under Section 25 of the RPwD Act, 2016, appropriate Governments are mandated to undertake or cause to be undertaken surveys, in…
points.text: 411. Under Section 25 of the RPwD Act, 2016, appropriate Governments are mandated to undertake or cause to be undertaken surveys, investigation and research concerning matters relating to the extent, nature and prevention of disabilities, and development of human resources including technologies for provision of rehabilitation services. The Act also mandates the provision of free healthcare in the vicinity of PwDs, particularly in rural areas, and recognizes their right to live in the community with access to a range of in-house and residential community support services to prevent isolation. Section 26 mandates research for prevention, early detection, cure and rehabilitation of persons with disabilities.

points.title: The Seven National Institutes under DEPwD, provide expert support and develop human resources; provide rehabilitation services;…
points.text: 412. The Seven National Institutes under DEPwD, provide expert support and develop human resources; provide rehabilitation services; and undertake research and development. Annexure 5 outline the Rehabilitation activities of National Institutes under DEPwD and National Institutes under MoHFW are at Annexure 6. The Twelfth Five Year Plan recognises the pivotal role of National Institutes in empowerment of PwDs and therefore aims to strengthen their operation as Centres of Excellence.

points.title: Composite Regional Centres (CRCs): The CRCs have been established with the support of the Central Government to facilitate the…
points.text: 413. Composite Regional Centres (CRCs): The CRCs have been established with the support of the Central Government to facilitate the creation of the required infrastructure for the capacity building, awareness generation, training of professionals, service delivery and sharing innovative knowledge developed by National Institutes and Training Centres with State Governments. CRCs are functional at central, state, district and village levels. From 2020-21, CRCs were integrated into the funding structure of National Institutes under revised scheme guidelines.

points.title: Rehabilitation Efforts for Persons with Mental Illness: The National Mental Health Programme, has allocated 78 million USD (Rs…
points.text: 414. Rehabilitation Efforts for Persons with Mental Illness: The National Mental Health Programme, has allocated 78 million USD (Rs 470 crore) for human resource development through 11 Institutions of Excellence across the country. These centres are being established by upgrading and strengthening identified mental health institutes, mental hospitals and departments of psychiatry. The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 came into force alongside the RPwD Act, establishing Mental Health Review Boards at the district level and emphasizing community-based mental health services and rehabilitation. Under the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) guidelines issued in 2023, Mental Health and Psychosocial Support Services (MHPSS) must be integrated into all phases of disaster management (prevention, response, and recovery) and remain available through the rehabilitation and reconstruction phases.

points.title: Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK): This national program conducts systematic screening for early identification of disabi…
points.text: 415. Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK): This national program conducts systematic screening for early identification of disabilities - twice-yearly screenings in Anganwadis for children aged 0-6 years and annual screenings in schools for ages 6-18 years. The program identifies the “4 Ds”: Deficiencies, Diseases, Disabilities, and Developmental Delays.

points.title: Identified cases are referred to DEICs where multidisciplinary teams of specialists manage treatment and rehabilitation.
points.text: 416. Identified cases are referred to DEICs where multidisciplinary teams of specialists manage treatment and rehabilitation. States have also constituted Assessment Boards to determine “high support needs” for persons with benchmark disabilities, facilitating specialized intervention and social security benefits including caregiver allowances. Regular training is conducted for ASHA (Accredited Social Health Activist) and Anganwadi workers on identifying disabilities and understanding their causes to strengthen grassroots-level early identification.

points.title: To implement the rehabilitation measures for persons with disabilities, DEPwD has devised the following schemes:
points.text: 417. To implement the rehabilitation measures for persons with disabilities, DEPwD has devised the following schemes:

points.title: National Career Service Centres for Differently Abled (NCSCs-DA): The Ministry of Labour & Employment has established 24 centre…
points.text: 418. National Career Service Centres for Differently Abled (NCSCs-DA): The Ministry of Labour & Employment has established 24 centres across India in major cities including Delhi, Mumbai, Guwahati, and Agartala. These centres evaluate the residual capacities of PwDs and provide vocational guidance, career counselling, and non-formal vocational training tailored to individual abilities.

points.title: Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Grameen Kaushalya Yojana (DDU-GKY): This placement-linked skill development program mandates 5% social inc…
points.text: 419. Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Grameen Kaushalya Yojana (DDU-GKY): This placement-linked skill development program mandates 5% social inclusion for PwDs and has trained over 13,400 PwD candidates in the last decade, promoting their integration into rural livelihood programs.

points.title: Rural Self Employment Training Institutes (RSETIs): These institutes provide barrier-free infrastructure and flexible training…
points.text: 420. Rural Self Employment Training Institutes (RSETIs): These institutes provide barrier-free infrastructure and flexible training modules, including sign language interpreters, to promote sustainable livelihoods for PwDs in rural areas.

points.title: Under the RPwD Act, 2016 and Samagra Shiksha program, schools are required to provide free assistive materials to students with…
points.text: 421. Under the RPwD Act, 2016 and Samagra Shiksha program, schools are required to provide free assistive materials to students with benchmark disabilities up to age 18, including:

points.title: The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 and Samagra Shiksha prioritize the recruitment of special educators with cross-disabil…
points.text: 422. The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 and Samagra Shiksha prioritize the recruitment of special educators with cross-disability training. State-level implementation includes Uttarakhand training 329 teachers in basic sign language and 95 in advanced levels.

points.title: Other National Trust Schemes continue to include:
points.text: 423. Other National Trust Schemes continue to include:

points.title: Bhashini Platform: The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology’s (MeitY) National Language Technology Mission provid…
points.text: 424. Bhashini Platform: The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology’s (MeitY) National Language Technology Mission provides AI-driven solutions to assist PwDs in communication and digital participation, including:

points.title: ICT Accessibility Standards: The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) published National Standard IS 17802, which mandates accessib…
points.text: 425. ICT Accessibility Standards: The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) published National Standard IS 17802, which mandates accessibility requirements for websites, mobile applications and stationary terminals including ATMs and ticketing machines.

points.title: The Constitution of India acknowledges the significance of work and employment in Article 39 of the Directive Principles which…
points.text: 426. The Constitution of India acknowledges the significance of work and employment in Article 39 of the Directive Principles which mandated the State to ensure “that the citizens, men and women equally, have the right to an adequate means of livelihood”; that “there is equal pay for equal work for both men and women”; and that “the health and strength of workers, men and women and the tender age of children are not abused and that citizens are not forced by economic necessity to enter into avocations unsuited to their age or strength”. Article 41 requires that the “State shall, within the limits of its economic capacity and development, make effective provision for securing the right to work…” and Article 42 requires that “the State shall make provision for securing just and humane conditions of work…”.

points.title: As per the 2011 census, of the total population of PwDs, approximately 36% constitutes the workers group whereas, the remaining…
points.text: 427. As per the 2011 census, of the total population of PwDs, approximately 36% constitutes the workers group whereas, the remaining 63% are non-workers.

points.title: Section 34 of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 provides that every government establishment shall reserve not…
points.text: 428. Section 34 of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 provides that every government establishment shall reserve not less than 4% of total vacancies for persons of PwDs with benchmark disabilities across 21 specified disabilities, distributed as: (a) one percent for persons with benchmark disabilities including blindness and low vision; (b) one percent for persons with deaf and hard of hearing; (c) one percent for persons with locomotor disability including cerebral palsy, leprosy cured, dwarfism, acid attack victims; and (d) one percent for persons with autism, intellectual disability, specific learning disability, mental illness, multiple disabilities including deaf-blindness, and other specified disabilities; in the posts identified for each disability through an expert committee that periodically reviews and identifies suitable posts.

points.title: Under the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, the legal framework governing employment of persons with disabilities…
points.text: 429. Under the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, the legal framework governing employment of persons with disabilities is significantly strengthened. Section 20(1) prohibits discrimination by government establishments in all aspects of employment, including recruitment, promotion, service conditions, and career advancement. Section 20(2) mandates provision of reasonable accommodation and a barrier-free, conducive work environment, with courts recognising denial of such accommodation during selection processes (for example, failure to provide sign-language interpreters) as a violation of the Act. Section 20(4) ensures job retention with pay protection by prohibiting dismissal or reduction in rank of employees who acquire a disability during service and requiring their transfer to an equivalent post with the same pay and benefits, or placement on a supernumerary post until a suitable position is available, rather than mere grant of invalid pension, and Section 21 requires every establishment, both public and private, to notify and register an Equal Opportunity Policy specifying facilities and amenities for persons with disabilities, while government establishments must additionally appoint a Grievance Redressal Officer to address complaints relating to non-compliance with employment rights provisions.

points.title: In March 2024, the Supreme Court of India, in Recruitment of Visually Impaired in Judicial Services v.
points.text: 430. In March 2024, the Supreme Court of India, in Recruitment of Visually Impaired in Judicial Services v. Registrar General, High Court of Madhya Pradesh, delivered a landmark judgment affirming that no candidate may be denied consideration for judicial service recruitment solely on the basis of disability, and that eligibility must be assessed on functional ability to perform duties rather than disability per se. The Court further reiterated that persons with disabilities are entitled to reservation in promotion, recognising affirmative action as essential to ensuring substantive equality, and clarified that promotion cannot be denied solely due to disability where the individual is otherwise eligible. This position is reinforced by the Madhya Pradesh Rights of Persons with Disabilities Rules, 2017, which provide for a 6% reservation in state services, underscoring that disability alone cannot operate as a disqualifying criterion in public employment.

points.title: The Department of Personnel and Training, in 2022, issued clarification regarding the concept of “own merit” for persons with b…
points.text: 431. The Department of Personnel and Training, in 2022, issued clarification regarding the concept of “own merit” for persons with benchmark disabilities. Candidates selected on their own merit (without availing reservation benefits) are not counted against the reservation quota, thereby ensuring additional representation beyond the mandated percentages.

points.title: The government periodically identifies posts suitable for different categories of PwDs through an expert committee mechanism.
points.text: 432. The government periodically identifies posts suitable for different categories of PwDs through an expert committee mechanism. This ensures that the 4% reservation is distributed meaningfully across diverse job roles rather than limited to traditional clerical positions.

points.title: Various concessions are given to PwDs under the RPWD Act, 2016 and DoPT guidelines.
points.text: 433. Various concessions are given to PwDs under the RPWD Act, 2016 and DoPT guidelines. The relaxation in age limit is provided irrespective of the fact whether the post is reserved or not, and the post is identified to be suitable for PwDs. Apart from age relaxation, PwDs get exemption from payment of application fee and examination fee and relaxation in eligibility standards. Additionally, persons with benchmark disabilities receive relaxation of up to 10 years in the upper age limit for direct recruitment to government posts (15 years for SC/ST candidates with disabilities).

points.title: Equality of Opportunity through open competition: The right to equality enshrined in Article 14 and the right to equality of op…
points.text: 434. Equality of Opportunity through open competition: The right to equality enshrined in Article 14 and the right to equality of opportunity in matters of employment under Article 16 of the Constitution accords similar opportunity to PwDs. The reservation provided in Section 33 of PWDA, in no way prevents a PwD from acquiring a position through open competition. This ensures equal opportunity and participation of PwDs in government jobs and is in line with the fundamental rights guaranteed to every citizen under Articles 14 and 16 of the Constitution of India.

points.title: Section 20 of the RPWD Act, 2016 inter-alia prohibits establishments from dispensing with the services of or reducing the rank…
points.text: 435. Section 20 of the RPWD Act, 2016 inter-alia prohibits establishments from dispensing with the services of or reducing the rank of any employee who acquires disability during service without giving such employee an opportunity of being heard.

points.title: Judicial interpretation has expanded protection against discrimination in employment by extending non-discrimination safeguards…
points.text: 436. Judicial interpretation has expanded protection against discrimination in employment by extending non-discrimination safeguards to HIV-positive persons, holding that they cannot be denied employment or promotion where they are otherwise physically fit to perform the role. The Supreme Court has further clarified that the principle of reasonable accommodation is not confined to benchmark disabilities and applies even to conditions such as colour blindness, so long as the accommodation sought does not impose a disproportionate or undue burden on the employer. Additionally, courts have consistently held that failure to provide reasonable accommodation during recruitment and selection processes (such as the absence of sign language interpreters during interviews) amounts to discrimination and constitutes a violation of the Act. The Calcutta High Court, in 2024, held that transfer and posting of employees with disabilities must not result in loss of seniority and must reasonably account for disability-related needs, such as access to medical facilities and proximity to family support systems.

points.title: Just and favourable conditions of work: As per Section 20(4) of the RPWD Act, 2016, employers are duty bound to continue the em…
points.text: 437. Just and favourable conditions of work: As per Section 20(4) of the RPWD Act, 2016, employers are duty bound to continue the employee on leave till such time as they stand superannuated and pay them wages and salaries accordingly, even when they are held to be 100% incapacitated. Critically, it is not sufficient to merely grant invalid pension; if the person is still within their service period, they must be either: (a) shifted to another post with the same pay scale and service benefits, or (b) kept on a supernumerary post until a suitable position becomes available. Section 20 casts obligation on the employer to protect an employee acquiring disability during service, even if the employee is unaware and asks for retirement.

points.title: Equal Remuneration and Benefits: Section 21 of the RPWD Act, 2016 mandates that persons with disabilities shall not be discrimi…
points.text: 438. Equal Remuneration and Benefits: Section 21 of the RPWD Act, 2016 mandates that persons with disabilities shall not be discriminated against in matters relating to employment, including:

points.title: The Workmen’s Compensation Act (WCA), 1923 provides that in case personal injury is caused to a workman by accident arising out…
points.text: 439. The Workmen’s Compensation Act (WCA), 1923 provides that in case personal injury is caused to a workman by accident arising out of or in the course of his employment, his employer shall be liable to pay compensation. The percentage of total disability suffered, workman’s wages at the time of debilitation, loss of earning capacity on account of the disablement are some of the important criteria considered by Courts whilst determining compensation.

points.title: Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme (MGNREGS): The disabled or differently-able persons defined under the…
points.text: 440. Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme (MGNREGS): The disabled or differently-able persons defined under the RPWD Act, 2016 as persons with benchmark disabilities, the severity of which is 40% and above as certified by competent authority are considered as special category of vulnerable persons for the purposes of MGNREGS.

points.title: Under MGNREGS, at least one labour-intensive work suitable for disabled persons must be available at all times in every Gram Pa…
points.text: 441. Under MGNREGS, at least one labour-intensive work suitable for disabled persons must be available at all times in every Gram Panchayat. Works such as supplying drinking water at work sites, taking care of children at crèches, land levelling, etc. are specifically identified as suitable for PwDs. A separate schedule of rates is finalised for PwDs to ensure fair compensation and improve their participation through productive work tailored to their capabilities. From 2017 to 2022, between 4.6 lakh and 6 lakh PwDs worked under MGNREGS annually, demonstrating significant participation in rural employment guarantee schemes.

points.title: Differently-abled persons are given preference for appointment as mates for MGNREGA works and as workers for providing drinking…
points.text: 442. Differently-abled persons are given preference for appointment as mates for MGNREGA works and as workers for providing drinking water, managing crèches, etc. at work sites. They are paid wages equal to other persons employed in MGNREGA works.

points.title: Programmatic entitlements under Section 43 of the RPWD Act, 2016 provide that “the appropriate Governments and local authoritie…
points.text: 443. Programmatic entitlements under Section 43 of the RPWD Act, 2016 provide that “the appropriate Governments and local authorities shall formulate and implement schemes and programmes to ensure not less than five per cent in all government-run or government-funded schemes for the benefit of PwDs.”

points.title: National Rural Livelihood Mission (NRLM): The most vulnerable households including Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups, singl…
points.text: 444. National Rural Livelihood Mission (NRLM): The most vulnerable households including Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups, single women, women-headed households, PwDs, landless, and migrant labour receive special focus under NRLM. The mission ensures at least 3% coverage of vulnerable sections are PwDs, supported through priority access to self-help groups, preferential lending via community institutions, tailored skill development and entrepreneurship training, and assistance with market linkage.

points.title: Vocational training: 21 Vocational Rehabilitation Centres for Handicapped (VRCs) have been established by Ministry of Labour &…
points.text: 445. Vocational training: 21 Vocational Rehabilitation Centres for Handicapped (VRCs) have been established by Ministry of Labour & Employment to evaluate residual capacity of PwDs to unfold their social, economic, psychological and vocational potentialities. Seven skill training workshops have been added to seven VRCs for skill upgradation of unemployed youth with disabilities. 11 Rural Rehabilitation Extension Centres (RRECs) have been set up in 11 blocks under five VRCs to provide services to PwDs in rural areas.

points.title: The National Career Service Centres for Differently Abled (NCSCs-DA), established by the Ministry of Labour & Employment, opera…
points.text: 446.
