The National Career Service Centres for Differently Abled (NCSCs-DA), established by the Ministry of Labour & Employment, operate 24 specialised centres across India to support persons with disabilities through disability-specific vocational guidance and career counselling, non-formal vocational training, accessible job-matching with reasonable accommodations, pre-placement employability training, and assistance in entering the competitive labour market.

points.title: Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Grameen Kaushalya Yojana (DDU-GKY): This placement-linked skill training program includes a mandatory 5% s…
points.text: 447. Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Grameen Kaushalya Yojana (DDU-GKY): This placement-linked skill training program includes a mandatory 5% social inclusion quota for PwDs. Performance data demonstrates that as of May 2025, 13,484 PwD candidates have been trained, and 4,751 candidates have been successfully placed in employment. Training under the program is provided in market-relevant sectors with employer partnerships, and post-placement tracking ensures support in job retention.

points.title: Rural Self Employment Training Institutes (RSETIs): Currently, 611 RSETIs across India offer training in 70 approved courses to…
points.text: 448. Rural Self Employment Training Institutes (RSETIs): Currently, 611 RSETIs across India offer training in 70 approved courses to help rural youth, including PwDs, start their own enterprises. RSETIs are mandated to provide barrier-free infrastructure (including ramps, accessible toilets, and tactile pathways), and support services like sign language interpreters during training sessions. They provide customized training modules considering different disability types, and even deploy post-training handholding support for business establishments, including maintaining linkages with banks for credit access.

points.title: Skill Development Initiative on Modular Employable Skill (MES): has been developed in consultation with Industry, State Governm…
points.text: 449. Skill Development Initiative on Modular Employable Skill (MES): has been developed in consultation with Industry, State Government and Experts in the area of Vocational Training. MES aims at achieving minimum skills set and allows skills upgradation and lifelong learning opportunities for PwDs.

points.title: National Skill Development Policy has been formulated in February, 2009 by the Ministry of Labour & Employment.
points.text: 450. National Skill Development Policy has been formulated in February, 2009 by the Ministry of Labour & Employment. It aims at increase in productivity of workforce both in the organized and the unorganized sectors, seeking increase in participation of youth, women, disabled and other disadvantaged sections and to synergize efforts of various sectors and reform the present system

points.title: Under the guidelines of the All India Council for Technical Education (AICTE), technical institutions are mandated to establish…
points.text: 451. Under the guidelines of the All India Council for Technical Education (AICTE), technical institutions are mandated to establish Equal Opportunity Facilitation Cells (EOFCs) to support students with disabilities by providing targeted assistance in training and placements, ensuring accessibility of laboratories, workshops, and practical training facilities, coordinating inclusive internships with industry partners, monitoring career progression, and facilitating reasonable accommodations during examinations and assessments.

points.title: The Department of Adult Independent Living is a dedicated department of National Institute for the Mentally Handicapped which f…
points.text: 452. The Department of Adult Independent Living is a dedicated department of National Institute for the Mentally Handicapped which functions for developing human resource in the area of vocational training to people with mental retardation, developing service models and employment and taking up research and developmental activities

points.title: The National Abilympics Association of India was formed in May 2001 at the behest of the MoSJE and MoHRD.
points.text: 453. The National Abilympics Association of India was formed in May 2001 at the behest of the MoSJE and MoHRD. It was aimed to formulate, initiate and implement policies to propagate Abilympics in the country and to promote and organize meets for competition in vocational and living skills. The 8th AI was held in Seoul, South Korea on 25-30th September, 2011 and India won one bronze medal and three special prizes.

points.title: The Ali Yavar Jung National Institute of Speech and Hearing Disabilities (Divyangjan) (formerly known as the National Institute…
points.text: 454. The Ali Yavar Jung National Institute of Speech and Hearing Disabilities (Divyangjan) (formerly known as the National Institute for Hearing Handicapped) takes up the issues pertaining to reservation of jobs for PwDs with government and public sectors to fill the backlog vacancies reserved. It also creates awareness among private sector employers and conducts “Employers Meets” with the Chamber of Commerce and Industry.

points.title: Special Employment Exchanges: The Ministry of Labour and Employment has the responsibility for vocational training and economic…
points.text: 455. Special Employment Exchanges: The Ministry of Labour and Employment has the responsibility for vocational training and economic rehabilitation of PwDs. The National Employment Service through 23 Special Employment Exchanges assists PwDs (specifically) in obtaining gainful employment and another 914 general employment exchanges that cater to PwDs as well as people without such special needs. These exchanges have been progressively digitized and integrated with the National Career Service portal to improve job matching and tracking of placements.

points.title: Government has been promoting the self-employment of PwDs by providing vocational training and loans at concessional rates thro…
points.text: 456. Government has been promoting the self-employment of PwDs by providing vocational training and loans at concessional rates through National Divyangjan Finance and Development Corporation (NDFDC). NDFDC was established under the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment and functions as an apex institution for channelising funds to PwDs through State Channelising Agencies nominated by State Governments.
points.subsection: E. Promoting Opportunities for Self-Employment and Entrepreneurship

points.title: The NDFDC provides a range of financial and employment-support measures for persons with disabilities, including concessional l…
points.text: 457. The NDFDC provides a range of financial and employment-support measures for persons with disabilities, including concessional loans at interest rates as low as 5-6% for self-employment ventures, loan-cum-subsidy schemes for micro-financing, special schemes for women entrepreneurs with disabilities offering additional concessions, educational loans for higher and professional education, and working capital loans for expansion of existing businesses, along with organising job fairs and mega employment camps to facilitate direct linkages between employers and PwD job seekers.
points.subsection: E. Promoting Opportunities for Self-Employment and Entrepreneurship

points.title: Concessional Loans: Under Section 41 of RPWD Act, 2016, the appropriate government is mandated by law to formulate schemes incl…
points.text: 458. Concessional Loans: Under Section 41 of RPWD Act, 2016, the appropriate government is mandated by law to formulate schemes including loans at concessional rates of interest to facilitate and support self- employment ventures for persons with disabilities, providing legal backing to programs like NDFDC.
points.subsection: E. Promoting Opportunities for Self-Employment and Entrepreneurship

points.title: A circular was issued by the Reserve Bank of India whereby all the NBFCs were categorically told that there shall be no discrim…
points.text: 459. A circular was issued by the Reserve Bank of India whereby all the NBFCs were categorically told that there shall be no discrimination in extending products and facilities, including loan facilities to the physically/visually challenged applicants on grounds of disability and that they may also advise their branches to render all possible assistance to PwDs for availing of the various business facilities.
points.subsection: E. Promoting Opportunities for Self-Employment and Entrepreneurship

points.title: PM SVANidhi Scheme for Street Vendors: This scheme supports street vendors, including those with disabilities, by providing col…
points.text: 460. PM SVANidhi Scheme for Street Vendors: This scheme supports street vendors, including those with disabilities, by providing collateral-free working capital loans. As of March 2025, the scheme has empowered nearly 42,000 street vendors with disabilities, by providing loans of up to Rs. 10,000 in the first tranche, scaling up to Rs. 50,000 in subsequent tranches. The scheme further promotes formalisation through digital payment incentives, facilitates credit history building to improve future access to finance, and offers an interest subsidy of 7% on timely repayment.
points.subsection: E. Promoting Opportunities for Self-Employment and Entrepreneurship

points.title: The National Trust organizes job fairs and ability Melas: These provide an interface between employers and PwDs leading to plac…
points.text: 461. The National Trust organizes job fairs and ability Melas: These provide an interface between employers and PwDs leading to placements. The Job Fair is for persons with the four disabilities that National Trust focuses on (autism, mental retardation, cerebral palsy, and multiple disabilities), Vocational Rehabilitation Centre (VRC) stall responding to employment-related queries from candidates across other disabilities.
points.subsection: E. Promoting Opportunities for Self-Employment and Entrepreneurship

points.title: Uddyam Prabha (Incentive Scheme) is a scheme of National Trust to promote economic activities for self-employment of PwDs throu…
points.text: 462. Uddyam Prabha (Incentive Scheme) is a scheme of National Trust to promote economic activities for self-employment of PwDs through interest incentive (subsidy) of up to 5% for Below Poverty Line (BPL) families and 3% for other categories on loans of up to Rs. 5 lakh (approximately $6,000) for a period of 5 years or until the loan is fully repaid, whichever is earlier.
points.subsection: E. Promoting Opportunities for Self-Employment and Entrepreneurship

points.title: The RPWD Act, 2016 lays down, in Section 35, that proactive measures shall be taken to provide incentives, awards, tax exemptio…
points.text: 463. The RPWD Act, 2016 lays down, in Section 35, that proactive measures shall be taken to provide incentives, awards, tax exemptions etc. to encourage employment of PwDs in private sector. Section 35 directs the government to formulate schemes to incentivise private sector employers to ensure that at least 5% of their workforce comprises persons with benchmark disabilities.
points.subsection: F. Encouraging Employment in Private Sector

points.title: Job portals for persons with disabilities are specialised, and integrated with multiple platforms.
points.text: 464. Job portals for persons with disabilities are specialised, and integrated with multiple platforms. Examples include:
points.subsection: F. Encouraging Employment in Private Sector

points.title: The Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan includes specific mandates for employment accessibility across three verticals:
points.text: 465. The Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan includes specific mandates for employment accessibility across three verticals:
points.subsection: F. Encouraging Employment in Private Sector

points.title: India’s official digital accessibility standard, IS 17802, published by the Bureau of Indian Standards in December 2021, was in…
points.text: 466. India’s official digital accessibility standard, IS 17802, published by the Bureau of Indian Standards in December 2021, was incorporated into the RPwD Rules through the 2023 amendment, rendering compliance with accessibility requirements for ICT products and services legally enforceable. In a landmark enforcement action in February 2025, 155 private and government organisations, including several central ministries, were penalised for non-compliance, underscoring that digital accessibility is a statutory obligation rather than a voluntary measure. This shift was further reinforced by 2025 judgments of the Supreme Court of India in the e-KYC cases, which issued binding directions requiring the Reserve Bank of India to mandate alternative “liveness” verification methods beyond eye-blinking, require acceptance of thumb impressions for visually impaired users, and ensure accessible e-KYC processes, thereby safeguarding access to employment-linked financial services such as salary accounts, EPF, ESI, and banking facilities.
points.subsection: F. Encouraging Employment in Private Sector

points.title: In Recruitment of Visually Impaired in Judicial Services v.
points.text: 467. In Recruitment of Visually Impaired in Judicial Services v. Registrar General, High Court of Madhya Pradesh, the Supreme Court of India held that persons with disabilities form a protected class entitled to equal access to employment, including judicial services. The Court ruled that disability alone cannot be a ground to deny recruitment or promotion, mandated reservation in promotion where applicable, required States to implement prescribed reservation percentages, and directed the removal of indirect and procedural barriers that undermine substantive equality in employment.
points.subsection: G. Judicial Developments on Employment Rights

points.title: In Mukesh Kumar v.
points.text: 468. In Mukesh Kumar v. National Power Training Institute & Ors., the Delhi High Court strengthened enforcement of employment rights under the RPwD Act by clarifying the jurisdiction and authority of the Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities (CCPD) in service-related matters, holding that CCPD directions are binding in nature, and that interim relief is available, reinforcing effective remedies against employment discrimination in line with Article 27 of the UNCRPD.
points.subsection: G. Judicial Developments on Employment Rights

points.title: The Bombay High Court has held that disability percentage alone cannot disqualify candidates from MBBS admissions.
points.text: 469. The Bombay High Court has held that disability percentage alone cannot disqualify candidates from MBBS admissions. Instead, the Court favoured individualized, functional assessments by expert medical boards in lieu of rigid disability thresholds that operate as indirect discrimination and impede access to the medical profession.
points.subsection: G. Judicial Developments on Employment Rights

points.title: Through suo motu proceedings, the Punjab and Haryana High Court has addressed inaccessible court infrastructure, seeking accoun…
points.text: 470. Through suo motu proceedings, the Punjab and Haryana High Court has addressed inaccessible court infrastructure, seeking accountability from state authorities for non-compliance with the RPWD Act.
points.subsection: G. Judicial Developments on Employment Rights

points.title: Employment data highlights persistent gaps in inclusion of persons with disabilities.
points.text: 471. Employment data highlights persistent gaps in inclusion of persons with disabilities. As per the 2011 Census, only about 36% of PwDs are workers, while nearly 75% fall within non-worker categories, indicating substantial labour market exclusion. This is compounded by a significant backlog in filling reserved posts across central and state government sectors. To address this, the Department of Personnel and Training (DoPT) monitors implementation through annual reports on reservation fulfilment, disability-wise break-ups across categories, special recruitment drives to clear backlogs, and mandatory quarterly compliance reports from all central ministries and departments.
points.subsection: G. Judicial Developments on Employment Rights

points.title: Enforcement of employment rights under the RPWD Act is anchored by the Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities (CCPD),…
points.text: 472. Enforcement of employment rights under the RPWD Act is anchored by the Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities (CCPD), which monitors implementation through powers to investigate employment discrimination complaints, issue binding recommendations, impose penalties for non- compliance, and initiate suo motu inquiries into systemic violations. These functions are mirrored by State Commissioners at the state level, while Grievance Redressal Officers are mandatorily appointed in every government establishment under Section 21 to address individual complaints. Compliance is further strengthened through annual audits that track implementation of the statutory 4% reservation for persons with disabilities.
points.subsection: G. Judicial Developments on Employment Rights

points.title: Article 38 (1) of the Constitution of India requires that “the State shall strive to promote the welfare of the people by secur…
points.text: 473. Article 38 (1) of the Constitution of India requires that “the State shall strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting as effectively as it may a social order in which justice, social, economic and political, shall inform all the institutions of the national life”. Article 38 (2) mandates that “the state shall, in particular, strive to minimize the inequalities in income, and endeavour to eliminate inequalities in status, facilities and opportunities not only amongst individuals but also amongst groups of people residing in different areas or engaged in different vocations”. Articles 39(a) and 41 of the Constitution of India require that the State should within the limits of its economic capacity make effective provision for securing the right to work, to education, and to public assistance in case of unemployment, old age, sickness, and disablement.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Section 43 of the RPWD Act requires that “the appropriate Governments and local authorities shall by notification frame schemes…
points.text: 474. Section 43 of the RPWD Act requires that “the appropriate Governments and local authorities shall by notification frame schemes in favour of PwDs, for the preferential allotment of land at concessional rates for- (a) House; (b) Setting up business; (c) Setting up special recreation centres; (d) Establishment of special schools; (e) Establishment of research centres; and (f) Establishment of factories by entrepreneurs with disabilities.”
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY): With a view to meet the housing needs of the poor, PMAY was launched in June 2015, replacing…
points.text: 475. Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY): With a view to meet the housing needs of the poor, PMAY was launched in June 2015, replacing the earlier Indira Awaas Yojana (IAY). The scheme has two components: Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana-Gramin (PMAY-G) for rural areas and Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana-Urban (PMAY-U) for urban areas. Under the scheme allotment of the house is done in the name of the female member of the households or in the joint names of husband and wife.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Under PMAY-U, guidelines mandate priority allotment of ground-floor or lower-floor units to families with persons with physical…
points.text: 476. Under PMAY-U, guidelines mandate priority allotment of ground-floor or lower-floor units to families with persons with physical disabilities, and require barrier-free access (such as ramps) in multi-storied and slum redevelopment projects. Nationwide, over 78,000 houses have been provided to persons with disabilities under PMAY-U. While there is no central reservation prescribed by the Government of India, States/UTs may adopt mechanisms for ensuring preference. State-level initiatives include Rajasthan’s Shivaji Nagar Housing Scheme (2014) which reserved 3% of houses for PwDs, categorized by visual, physical, and non-verbal disabilities.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Under PMAY-G, states are encouraged to ensure that 5% of beneficiaries are PwDs, with priority given to women with benchmark di…
points.text: 477. Under PMAY-G, states are encouraged to ensure that 5% of beneficiaries are PwDs, with priority given to women with benchmark disabilities. PwD households with no able-bodied adult members receive an additional deprivation score to ensure priority allotment. Financial assistance of Rs. 1.20 lakh is provided in plains and Rs. 1.30 lakh in hill states, union territories of J&K and Ladakh, North Eastern States and difficult areas. For PwDs unable to build their own homes, the state provides assistance through rural mason training programs or local Gram Panchayats. Ground floors in any housing scheme under PMAY are given preference for differently-abled and older persons. As of 2025, more than 3.34 crore houses have been sanctioned under PMAY. The scheme period for PMAY-U has been extended up to 31.12.2025 to complete sanctioned houses. PMAY-U 2.0 was launched providing financial assistance up to Rs. 2.5 lakh to individual eligible families.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM): Launched on 2 October 2014 to achieve Open Defecation Free (ODF) India by 2 October 2019.
points.text: 478. Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM): Launched on 2 October 2014 to achieve Open Defecation Free (ODF) India by 2 October 2019. The mission comprises two components: Swachh Bharat Mission-Gramin (SBM- G) and Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban (SBM-U). Under SBM-G, there is a provision for incentive of Rs. 12,000/- for construction of Individual Household Latrine (IHHL) for all BPL households and identified APL households (SC/ST households, households with physically disabled person, landless labourers with homestead, small and marginal farmers and women headed households). The unit cost was enhanced from Rs. 10,000/- to Rs. 12,000/- to provide for water availability, including for storing, hand-washing and cleaning of toilets. Special provision for disabled-friendly latrines in households continues, with an incentive amount provided to all persons with physical disabilities for construction of disabled friendly latrines.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Draft accessibility standards for Community Toilets in Rural areas have been developed, providing for ramps with slope of 1:15…
points.text: 479. Draft accessibility standards for Community Toilets in Rural areas have been developed, providing for ramps with slope of 1:15 or less, clear width of pathway minimum 1200mm, contrast colour bands, tactile tiles, and Braille facilities. However, inclusion and accessibility remain a challenge, with only 18% of CSCs in Bihar, 9% in Uttar Pradesh and 5% in Rajasthan including design elements suitable for differently-abled persons. Phase II of SBM-G (2019-20) intended to enact the Leave No One Behind (LNOB) principle and provide Community Sanitary Complexes to ensure universal sanitation coverage, though implementation of disability-inclusive design remains incomplete. According to estimates, the SBM prevented 300,000 deaths and avoided 14 million Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) between 2014 and 2019.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: The Swadhar Scheme was launched in 2002 by the MoHRD to address the specific vulnerability of women in difficult circumstances…
points.text: 480. The Swadhar Scheme was launched in 2002 by the MoHRD to address the specific vulnerability of women in difficult circumstances through a Home-based holistic and integrated approach. The scheme extends to women with intellectual disabilities who are without any support of family or relatives.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: National Rural Drinking Water Programme (NRDWP): The NRDWP, 2009 [Note: This program has since been subsumed under Jal Jeevan M…
points.text: 481. National Rural Drinking Water Programme (NRDWP): The NRDWP, 2009 [Note: This program has since been subsumed under Jal Jeevan Mission launched in 2019 with the goal to provide functional household tap connection to every rural household by 2024] is a programme of the GoI under which the financial and technical assistance is provided to State Governments for the provision of safe drinking water facilities in all rural habitations of the country. The goal of the NRDWP is to cover all rural households with safe and adequate drinking water supply, giving priority to those habitations, where drinking water sources have chemical (Arsenic and fluoride) contamination, or where sufficient quantity of safe drinking water is not available. One of the essential components of Bharat Nirman, a programme for building rural infrastructure, is on providing drinking water supply facilities in rural areas. The focus under NRDWP is shifting from the provision of handpumps to provision of piped water supply in more habitations with a view to reduce the burden of collecting and fetching drinking water on women, children and PwDs.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: As far as Right to Food is concerned, the schemes namely Annapurna and Nutritional Programme for Adolescent Girls are being imp…
points.text: 482. As far as Right to Food is concerned, the schemes namely Annapurna and Nutritional Programme for Adolescent Girls are being implemented by the Ministries of Rural Development and Women and Child Development and are targeted at senior citizens and adolescent girls respectively.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: As per the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, Section 52 provides that for the PwDs, following steps will be taken…
points.text: 483. As per the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, Section 52 provides that for the PwDs, following steps will be taken to provide adequate Social Security:- (i) A system of regular review of the policies of tax relief granted to the persons with disabilities will be put in place so, that the necessary income tax and other tax relief remain available to PwDs. (ii) State Governments and UT Administrations will be encouraged to rationalize the amount of pension and unemployment allowance for PwDs. (iii) Life Insurance Corporation of India has been providing insurance cover to persons with specific type of disabilities.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Pension Schemes: In accordance to Article 41 of the Constitution of India that directs the states to provide public assistance…
points.text: 484. Pension Schemes: In accordance to Article 41 of the Constitution of India that directs the states to provide public assistance to its citizens in case of unemployment, old age, sickness and disablement and in other cases of undeserved want within the limit of its economic capacity and development, the National Social Assistance Programme (NSAP) came into effect from 15th August, 1995. In February 2009, under NSAP – the Indira Gandhi National Disability Pension Scheme (IGNDPS) was added. IGNDPS provides assistance to persons with severe or multiple disabilities (80% or higher disability) between the age group of 18-79 years belonging to households living below poverty line (BPL). The Central Government contribution under IGNDPS is Rs. 300/- per month per beneficiary for those aged 18-79, and Rs. 500/- per month for those aged 80 and above. However, States and Union Territories supplement this amount with their own contributions, resulting in total pension amounts varying significantly across states.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: State-specific disability pension data for the 2017–2022 period indicates a wide variation in benefit levels and coverage.
points.text: 485. State-specific disability pension data for the 2017–2022 period indicates a wide variation in benefit levels and coverage. Andaman and Nicobar provides a disability allowance of ₹2,500 per month, covering 4,120 beneficiaries as of January 2025. Chandigarh offers ₹1,000 per month for persons with 40–70% disability and ₹2,000 per month for those above 70%, benefiting 3,234 persons in 2021–22. Delhi provides a disability pension of ₹2,500 per month under its subsistence/financial allowance scheme. Goa, under the Dayanand Social Security Scheme, pays between ₹2,000 and ₹3,500 per month, reaching 11,879 beneficiaries during 2017–2022. The state of Haryana increased its pension to ₹3,000 per month by January 2024 from ₹1,400 in 2016. Maharashtra provides ₹1,500 per month under the Sanjay Gandhi Niradhar Anudan Yojana (₹300 central share and ₹1,200 state share). Meghalaya covered 13,205 beneficiaries in 2021–22 under the CM-Care Disability Pension scheme, and Uttar Pradesh provides a maintenance grant of ₹1,000 per month, reaching over 1.12 million beneficiaries in 2021–22.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: As of 2024-25, Rs.
points.text: 486. As of 2024-25, Rs. 243.74 crore was released to States and Union Territories under IGNDPS, with more than 8.33 lakh beneficiaries covered under this scheme. Digital Life Certification (DLC) mobile application for Aadhaar-based life verification of NSAP pension beneficiaries was launched on 15 July 2025 to ease the annual life verification process. The budget allocation for NSAP for the year 2025-26 stands at Rs. 9,652 crore.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Insurance schemes in organized sector: Most workers under the organized sector are covered under the institutionalized social s…
points.text: 487. Insurance schemes in organized sector: Most workers under the organized sector are covered under the institutionalized social security provided through Employees Provident Fund Organization (EPFO), and the Employees State Insurance Corporation (ESIC).
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Employees’ State Insurance Corporation (ESIC) Section 46 of the ESIC Act envisages following social security benefits: ˷ Tempor…
points.text: 488. Employees’ State Insurance Corporation (ESIC) Section 46 of the ESIC Act envisages following social security benefits: ˷ Temporary disablement benefit (TDB) : From day one of entering insurable employment & irrespective of having paid any contribution in case of employment injury. Temporary Disablement Benefit at the rate of 90% of wage is payable, as long as disability continues. ˷ Permanent disablement benefit (PDB) : The benefit is paid at the rate of 90% of wage in the form of monthly payment depending upon the extent of loss of earning capacity as certified by a Medical Board. ˷ Medical care is also provided to permanently disabled insured persons and their spouses on payment of a token annual premium of 2 US Dollar. Other benefits include vocational rehabilitation to permanently disabled insured person and physical rehabilitation in case of physical disablement due to employment injury.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Rajiv Gandhi Shramik Kalyan Yojana: The ESI Corporation introduced a scheme of unemployment allowance with effect from 2005 und…
points.text: 489. Rajiv Gandhi Shramik Kalyan Yojana: The ESI Corporation introduced a scheme of unemployment allowance with effect from 2005 under which insured persons are covered under the ESI Scheme for three years or more, and who lose their jobs due to closure of factory establishment or retrenchment or permanent disability, are provided unemployment allowance in cash equal to 50% of their wage up to one year. During this period, the insured person and his family are also eligible for medical care. In November 2019, the National Health Authority entered into partnership with ESIC, creating synergy between Ayushman Bharat- Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB PM-JAY) and Employees State Insurance Scheme (ESIS), enabling ESIC beneficiaries to access services at AB PM-JAY empanelled hospitals and vice versa. Those insured persons who undergo training at Vocational Training Centres run by the Ministry of Labour & Employment are also paid fee charged by the institution as well as expenses on their travel.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Unorganized Sector: To safeguard the interest of unorganized workers, Unorganized Workers Social Security Act, 2008 was enacted.
points.text: 490. Unorganized Sector: To safeguard the interest of unorganized workers, Unorganized Workers Social Security Act, 2008 was enacted. Section 3 of the Act provides that Central Government shall formulate and notify suitable welfare schemes for unorganized workers on matters relating to life and disability cover. Section 5 of the Act also provides the constitution of National Social Security Board which will recommend formulation of social security schemes for unorganised workers/categories of unorganised workers from time to time.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Ayushman Bharat-Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB PM-JAY): Launched on 23 September 2018, this is the world’s largest govern…
points.text: 491. Ayushman Bharat-Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB PM-JAY): Launched on 23 September 2018, this is the world’s largest government-funded health insurance scheme providing health coverage of Rs. 5 lakh per family per year for secondary and tertiary care hospitalisation to over 12 crore poor and vulnerable families (approximately 55 crore beneficiaries). Vulnerable groups including differently-abled individuals are covered under this scheme based on SECC 2011 criteria. The scheme provides cashless treatment at empanelled hospitals, covers pre-existing conditions from day one, and includes up to 3 days of pre-hospitalisation and 15 days of post-hospitalisation expenses. In September 2024, the Union Cabinet expanded eligibility under AB PM-JAY to include all senior citizens aged 70 years and above, irrespective of income and socio-economic categories, benefiting approximately 6 crore senior citizens. The scheme has convergence with ESIC and is administered by the National Health Authority.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Employment Reservation: Under the RPWD Act 2016, Section 34 mandates that every Government establishment shall appoint not less…
points.text: 492. Employment Reservation: Under the RPWD Act 2016, Section 34 mandates that every Government establishment shall appoint not less than 4% (increased from 3% under the previous PWD Act 1995) of the total number of vacancies in the cadre strength for persons with benchmark disabilities (40% or more disability). This 4% reservation is distributed as 1% each for: (a) blindness and low vision; (b) deaf and hard of hearing; (c) locomotor disability including cerebral palsy, leprosy cured, dwarfism, acid attack victims and muscular dystrophy; (d) autism, intellectual disability, specific learning disability and mental illness; (e) multiple disabilities from clauses (a) to (d) including deaf-blindness. The Act also provides for age relaxation in upper age limit for employment of persons with benchmark disabilities. In November 2024, the Centre issued comprehensive guidelines to streamline reservation and identification of posts for persons with disabilities, mandating periodic identification of such posts and formation of expert committees to assess them. Section 35 of the Act provides incentives to employers in the private sector to employ at least 5% of their workforce from persons with benchmark disabilities.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Vocational Training and Skill Development Programs: Multiple initiatives have been established to enhance livelihood opportunit…
points.text: 493. Vocational Training and Skill Development Programs: Multiple initiatives have been established to enhance livelihood opportunities for PwDs:
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Assistive Devices and Technology Support: The Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids and Appliances (ADIP)…
points.text: 494. Assistive Devices and Technology Support: The Assistance to Disabled Persons for Purchase/Fitting of Aids and Appliances (ADIP) Scheme, managed through Artificial Limbs Manufacturing Corporation of India (ALIMCO), provides aids and appliances to persons with disabilities. Uttar Pradesh distributed over 35,136 devices in 2024-25, and Uttarakhand has committed to providing prosthetic limbs to all PwDs within one year, with 1,558 already provided in 2024-25. This scheme ensures that persons with disabilities have access to disability-related services, assistive devices and other assistance as required under Article 28, Section 2(a) and (c) of the UNCRPD.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Unique Disability Identity (UDID) Card: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities (Amendment) Rules, 2024, issued in October 2024…
points.text: 495. Unique Disability Identity (UDID) Card: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities (Amendment) Rules, 2024, issued in October 2024, introduced streamlined processes for disability certificates and UDID cards. Individuals can now apply through the UDID Portal to their district medical authorities or medical facilities where they are undergoing treatment. The amendments introduced color-coded UDID cards: White for disabilities below 40%, Yellow for 40-79%, and Blue for 80% or higher disability. Medical authorities must issue disability certificates and UDID cards within three months of diagnosis. These amendments aim to improve accessibility and provide more efficient services for persons with disabilities.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Disability Categories Expanded: The RPWD Act 2016 increased the types of disabilities from 7 to 21, with power given to the Cen…
points.text: 496. Disability Categories Expanded: The RPWD Act 2016 increased the types of disabilities from 7 to 21, with power given to the Central Government to add more types. New categories include: Speech and Language Disability, Specific Learning Disability, Acid Attack Victims (for the first time), Dwarfism, Muscular Dystrophy, and three blood disorders - Thalassemia, Haemophilia and Sickle Cell disease. The Act defines “person with benchmark disability” as a person with not less than 40% of a specified disability.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Equal Access to Retirement Benefits and Employment Protections: Judicial and administrative actions ensure PwDs retain access t…
points.text: 497. Equal Access to Retirement Benefits and Employment Protections: Judicial and administrative actions ensure PwDs retain access to retirement and family benefits. The Gujarat High Court clarified that children with intellectual disabilities are eligible for family pensions even after age 25 if their disability prevents them from earning a living, ensuring compliance with Article 28, Section 2(e) of the UNCRPD. Under Section 20(4) of the RPWD Act 2016, termination of employees who acquire disabilities during service is prohibited, and they must be reassigned to suitable roles with the same pay scale and service benefits. The Madras High Court reinforced this by ordering reinstatement of a visually impaired policeman with alternative employment and pay protection, and similarly ruled that a railway employee’s pay scale could not be reduced after acquiring disability during service.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: Accessibility and Digital Initiatives: Under the RPWD Act 2016, comprehensive accessibility standards have been established.
points.text: 498. Accessibility and Digital Initiatives: Under the RPWD Act 2016, comprehensive accessibility standards have been established. The Guidelines for Indian Government Websites (GIGW) 3.0, developed in collaboration with NIC, CERT-In and STQC, are based on the RPWD Act 2016 and Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 to ensure that government websites and mobile apps meet accessibility standards. The Harmonized Guidelines and Standards for Universal Accessibility in India - 2021 were notified by the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs in October 2022, replacing earlier 2016 guidelines, to establish standards for barrier-free built environment. The Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan) continues to ensure accessibility in public buildings, transportation systems, and information and communication technology within prescribed timeframes.
points.article: Article 28: Adequate Standard of Living and Social Protection

points.title: The Preamble of the Indian Constitution declares India to be a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic.
points.text: 499. The Preamble of the Indian Constitution declares India to be a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic. This essentially means that every citizen has the right to vote and to stand for the election. This principle of adult suffrage has been further enunciated in Article 326 of the Constitution which states that, “The elections to the House of the People and to the Legislative Assembly of every State shall be on the basis of adult suffrage; that is to say, every person who is a citizen of India and who is not less than eighteen years of age on such date as may be fixed in that behalf by or under any law made by the appropriate Legislature and is not otherwise disqualified under this Constitution or any law made by the appropriate Legislature on the ground of non-residence, unsoundness of mind, crime or corrupt or illegal practice, shall be entitled to be registered as a voter at any such election.”
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: A. Constitutional Provisions on Political Representation

points.title: However pursuant to Article 102 (1) (b) of the Constitution “a person may be disqualified for being chosen and for being a memb…
points.text: 500. However pursuant to Article 102 (1) (b) of the Constitution “a person may be disqualified for being chosen and for being a member of either house of Parliament on the ground of unsound mind only after being so declared by a Competent Court”. Article 191 (1) (b) makes a similar provision for State Legislative Assemblies and Councils.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: A. Constitutional Provisions on Political Representation

points.title: The Supreme Court of India has declared that accessibility (physical, digital, and informational) is a fundamental right under…
points.text: 501. The Supreme Court of India has declared that accessibility (physical, digital, and informational) is a fundamental right under Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution. In Rajive Raturi vs Union of India, the Court reaffirmed that accessibility is a precondition for realizing fundamental rights, which includes the right to participate in public life. This constitutional interpretation establishes that barriers to political participation constitute violations of fundamental rights.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: Under Section 16 (1) (b) of the Representation of the People Act, 1950 a person may not be disqualified from registration in an…
points.text: 502. Under Section 16 (1) (b) of the Representation of the People Act, 1950 a person may not be disqualified from registration in an electoral roll if such person “is of unsound mind and stands so declared by a Competent Court”. Under Section 16(2) a person’s name may be removed from an electoral roll if he is so declared by a Competent Authority, after being registered on the electoral roll.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: B. Statutory Provisions on Electoral Rights

points.title: Section 11 of the RPwD Act, 2016 specifically mandates: “The Election Commission of India and the State Election Commissions sh…
points.text: 503. Section 11 of the RPwD Act, 2016 specifically mandates: “The Election Commission of India and the State Election Commissions shall ensure that all polling stations are accessible to persons with disabilities and all materials related to the electoral process are easily understandable by and accessible to them.” This statutory provision creates enforceable obligations on election authorities and designates the Election Commission of India as the key central authority responsible for the progress and monitoring of Article 29 implementation.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: B. Statutory Provisions on Electoral Rights

points.title: The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 establishes important principles regarding mental capacity and voting rights.
points.text: 504. The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 establishes important principles regarding mental capacity and voting rights. Section 2(s) defines “mental illness” while Section 4 creates a legal presumption that every person, including a person with mental illness, shall be deemed to have the capacity to make decisions regarding their mental health care or treatment. The Act clarifies that determination of mental illness alone does not imply or mean that a person is of “unsound mind” unless declared so by a competent court.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: C. Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 and Voting Capacity

points.title: This distinction between “mental illness” (a medical condition) and “unsound mind” (a legal finding) has been reinforced throug…
points.text: 505. This distinction between “mental illness” (a medical condition) and “unsound mind” (a legal finding) has been reinforced through judicial decisions and disability rights advocacy. The burden of proof lies with electoral officers to demonstrate that a person is of “unsound mind” through competent court declaration, not on persons with mental illness to prove their soundness of mind.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: C. Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 and Voting Capacity

points.title: However, persistent challenges remain in implementation.
points.text: 506. However, persistent challenges remain in implementation. Despite the Mental Healthcare Act’s provisions, no standardized assessment tools like the Competency Assessment Tool for Voting (CAT-V) used in other jurisdictions have been officially adopted in India. The absence of clear eligibility determination mechanisms, combined with continued use of the term “unsound mind” in the Representation of the People Act, 1950 and Constitutional provisions (Articles 102 and 191), creates ongoing legal ambiguities regarding voting rights for persons with intellectual and psychosocial disabilities. As of 2019, approximately 3 million Indians suffer from neurocognitive disorders, thereby facing potential marginalization from the electorate.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: C. Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 and Voting Capacity

points.title: In November 2025, the Kerala High Court in Jomon Jacob v.
points.text: 507. In November 2025, the Kerala High Court in Jomon Jacob v. State Election Commission clarified that persons residing in mental health rehabilitation facilities cannot be excluded from the electoral roll or made to vote separately unless a competent court has declared them to be of unsound mind under Section 74(1)(b) of the Kerala Municipality Act. The Court emphasized that without judicial declaration, assumptions about mental health cannot justify any interference with electoral rights, and that labelling persons as “mentally ill” creates social stigma and undermines their dignity.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: C. Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 and Voting Capacity

points.title: The Central government has been empowered to after any consultation with the Election Commission Rules that regulate “the manne…
points.text: 508. The Central government has been empowered to after any consultation with the Election Commission Rules that regulate “the manner in which votes are to be given both generally and in the case of illiterate voters under physical or other disability or voters not conversant with the language in which ballot papers are printed or voters under physical or other disability and the procedure as to voting to be followed at elections.”
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: D. Facilitating the Electoral Rights of Persons with Physical and Sensory Disabilities

points.title: Rule 25 and Rule 27G of the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961 provide that if an elector is unable due to illiteracy, blindness…
points.text: 509. Rule 25 and Rule 27G of the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961 provide that if an elector is unable due to illiteracy, blindness or other physical infirmity to record his vote on a postal ballot paper and sign the declaration, he shall take the same to an officer competent to attest his signature and request the officer to record his vote and sign his declaration on his behalf, for different types of elections. Rule 32, Rule 40A, Rule 49D(f) and Rule 49P(f) of the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961 provide that persons accompanying a blind or infirm elector who cannot move without help cannot be excluded from entering polling station. Further, Presiding Officers are required to ensure that physically challenged electors are given priority for entering the polling station, without having to wait in the queue for other electors.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: D. Facilitating the Electoral Rights of Persons with Physical and Sensory Disabilities

points.title: Where a person owing to blindness or other physical infirmity is unable to recognise the symbols on the ballot paper/ballot uni…
points.text: 510. Where a person owing to blindness or other physical infirmity is unable to recognise the symbols on the ballot paper/ballot unit of the voting machine or to record his vote thereon without assistance, the Presiding Officer should permit the elector to take with him a companion, who is not a minor to the voting compartment for recording the vote on the ballet paper on his behalf.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: D. Facilitating the Electoral Rights of Persons with Physical and Sensory Disabilities

points.title: The Handbook for Returning Officers (at Elections Where Electronic Voting Machines are Used) (2009) states that the list of pol…
points.text: 511. The Handbook for Returning Officers (at Elections Where Electronic Voting Machines are Used) (2009) states that the list of polling stations should be drawn up, as far as possible, to avoid inconvenience to the old and PwDs. It further stipulates that the polling stations should be set up in the ground floor of a building and ramps should be provided to ease entry of ‘physically challenged persons’.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: D. Facilitating the Electoral Rights of Persons with Physical and Sensory Disabilities

points.title: The 2019 Lok Sabha elections were designated as “Accessible Elections” by the Election Commission of India.
points.text: 512. The 2019 Lok Sabha elections were designated as “Accessible Elections” by the Election Commission of India. The Commission implemented comprehensive measures including: ●Mapping electors with disabilities to their nearest polling stations through Block Level Officers (BLOs) who identified PwD voters and maintained databases without forming separate or additional lists ●Installing ramps at polling stations ●Providing Braille-embossed Electoral Photo Identity Cards (EPIC) ●Introducing Braille-enabled candidate lists and ballot units ●Establishing wheelchairs at polling stations with provisions allowing wheelchairs to be taken inside polling stations ●Creating dedicated queues for persons with disabilities ●Deploying volunteers to assist voters with disabilities at booth entrances ●Ensuring all polling stations are situated on ground floors where feasible ●Providing shamianas (temporary shelters) at polling stations to protect voters from weather elements
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: The Election Commission constituted the National Advisory Committee on Accessible Elections (NACAE) and State Steering Committe…
points.text: 513. The Election Commission constituted the National Advisory Committee on Accessible Elections (NACAE) and State Steering Committees for Accessible Elections (SSCAE) to oversee implementation of accessibility measures. Working groups were established to facilitate inclusion of persons with disabilities at national, state, district, and assembly constituency levels. The Election Commission issued action plans and circulars to all Chief Election Officers detailing facilitations required at polling stations to ensure hassle-free voting by citizens with disabilities.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: For the 2024 Lok Sabha elections, the Election Commission introduced home voting on a pan-India basis for senior citizens above…
points.text: 514. For the 2024 Lok Sabha elections, the Election Commission introduced home voting on a pan-India basis for senior citizens above 85 years of age and persons with disabilities having 40% or more benchmark disability. Eligible voters complete Form 12D within five days of election notification, with PwD voters required to include a baseline disability certificate with their applications. A dedicated team including polling officials and security personnel visits the voter’s residence at a pre-scheduled time, with the entire process recorded via videography for transparency. Over 81 lakh voters above 85 years and more than 90 lakh registered PwD voters became eligible for this facility. In the first two phases of polling in 2024, voters in these categories successfully cast their votes from home.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: The Election Commission further launched the Saksham mobile application to facilitate easier voting for persons with disabilities.
points.text: 515. The Election Commission further launched the Saksham mobile application to facilitate easier voting for persons with disabilities. The app features voice-over prompts, high-contrast interface, and easy navigation features developed specifically for accessibility. It allows PwD voters to verify their names in electoral rolls, enroll themselves, and get their name, address, and photograph corrected. The PwD user enters their mobile number and address, and the information is transmitted to the Electoral Registration Officer (ERO) within minutes. The ERO allocates the case to a Booth Level Officer to visit the home of the PwD user to complete form filling. Users can track their application status using a reference number throughout the process. For visually impaired voters, the app includes a critical feature that reads out the complete candidate list with names and serial numbers, allowing voters to select their desired candidate and cast their vote with ease. As of 2024, over 3.1 million users have viewed their PwD status through the app, with 47,727 requests received for marking themselves as PwD. The application is available on Google Play Store.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: Polling stations across the country were mandated to provide assured minimum facilities including drinking water, toilets, clea…
points.text: 516. Polling stations across the country were mandated to provide assured minimum facilities including drinking water, toilets, clear signage, ramps or wheelchairs, helpdesks, Voter Facilitation Centres, sufficient lighting, and shed/shelter. The Election Commission issued detailed instructions to all states and union territories regarding these facilities and pushed for permanent implementation of AMF in schools as a lasting gift to students. Implementation monitoring was conducted through regular inspections and reporting mechanisms.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: Transport and Volunteer Support: Comprehensive pick-and-drop facilities were arranged for persons with disabilities and senior…
points.text: 517. Transport and Volunteer Support: Comprehensive pick-and-drop facilities were arranged for persons with disabilities and senior citizens on polling day during the 2024 Lok Sabha elections. Volunteers were stationed at polling booth entrances to guide voters, and wheelchairs were provided at every polling station. In some areas, wheelchair-accessible vans were provided by local entrepreneurs and organizations to transport disabled voters. For example, in Kandivali, Mumbai, a businessman provided a wheelchair- accessible van to transport disabled voters to polling stations during the 2024 Maharashtra assembly elections. In Bihar, the Election Commission arranged for “Raths” (chariots) to carry PwD voters to polling stations.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: Approximately 2,697 polling stations were set up across the country and managed exclusively by officials with disabilities duri…
points.text: 518. Approximately 2,697 polling stations were set up across the country and managed exclusively by officials with disabilities during the Lok Sabha Election 2024, with Uttar Pradesh establishing the highest number (302 such stations). This initiative demonstrated the capability of persons with disabilities to manage electoral operations and served as a model for inclusive employment practices.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: Special camps were organized to enroll transgender electors, sex workers, and Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups (PVTGs) in…
points.text: 519. Special camps were organized to enroll transgender electors, sex workers, and Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups (PVTGs) in collaboration with civil society organizations. Over 48,260 transgender individuals were enrolled nationwide as of 2024, with Tamil Nadu having the highest enrollment (8,467), followed by Uttar Pradesh (6,628) and Maharashtra (5,720). In Thane District, special camps were arranged in collaboration with civil society to enroll third gender electors and other marginalized communities. The Election Commission also took measures to reach homeless and nomadic groups who experience electoral exclusion due to lack of proof of residency, conducting special enrollment drives for these populations.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: Ms.
points.text: 520. Ms. Sheetal Devi, Arjuna Awardee and Para Archer, was appointed as the Election Commission’s National PwD Icon to motivate voters with disabilities to exercise their franchise in the Lok Sabha Election 2024. Eleven prominent personalities with disabilities were appointed as Ambassadors of ECI for taking part in various voter awareness initiatives and reaching out to PwD electors. State PwD Icons were designated across all states and union territories. As part of Systematic Voters’ Education and Electoral Participation (SVEEP) initiatives, the Commission organized a T-20 Cricket Match on 16th March, 2024 between the Indian Deaf Cricket Association (IDCA) and Delhi District Cricket Association (DDCA) to promote electoral participation among persons with disabilities.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: As of 2024, approximately 9.27 million voters with disabilities out of approximately 968 million eligible voters were registered.
points.text: 521. As of 2024, approximately 9.27 million voters with disabilities out of approximately 968 million eligible voters were registered.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: Disability Rights activists have imaginatively used courts to obtain their constitutional rights.
points.text: 522. Disability Rights activists have imaginatively used courts to obtain their constitutional rights. Thus a letter written by Disability Rights group was registered as a writ in April 2004 just before the Lok Sabha elections caused the Supreme Court to take up the accessibility demands of PwDs like provision of ramps, separate queues as well as Braille sheets in the Electronic Voting Machines. Concurrently, the Supreme Court ordered that the polling officials should be sensitized about the needs of the PwDs and wide publicity should be given to these measures so as to ensure that the PwDs population is made aware of it and should come out to exercise its franchise. To ensure easy accessibility for PwDs, DEPwD made arrangements for providing tricycles at polling stations during Lok Sabha and Legislative Assembly elections in Delhi, Lucknow, Varanasi, Kanpur and Allahabad. A handbook was prepared for providing Officers for guidance.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: The Supreme Court has taken further steps to ensure access to justice for persons with disabilities.
points.text: 523. The Supreme Court has taken further steps to ensure access to justice for persons with disabilities. Recognizing that accessibility to the legal system is essential for participation in public affairs, the Court has directed the construction of accessible toilet facilities in all courts across India and mandated the provision of accessible digital judgments for visually impaired individuals. These measures ensure that persons with disabilities can effectively exercise their right to access any court, tribunal, authority, commission or any other body having judicial or quasi-judicial or investigative powers without discrimination on the basis of disability, as guaranteed under Section 12 of the RPwD Act, 2016.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: In 2024, the Punjab and Haryana High Court took suo motu cognizance of the lack of infrastructure in judicial complexes which m…
points.text: 524. In 2024, the Punjab and Haryana High Court took suo motu cognizance of the lack of infrastructure in judicial complexes which may be inaccessible to persons with disabilities. The court sought status reports from the Governments of Punjab, Haryana and U.T. Chandigarh on the accessibility status of judicial complexes to persons with disabilities, keeping in mind the provisions of Sections 44, 45 and 46 of the RPwD Act, 2016.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: That PwDs are coming of age politically can also be deduced from the emergence of Vikyang Manchs (Federation of the Disabled pe…
points.text: 525. That PwDs are coming of age politically can also be deduced from the emergence of Vikyang Manchs (Federation of the Disabled persons) which are forums of PwDs active in the rural areas of the country. These forums show how disability rights advocacy are no longer an urban phenomenon.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life

points.title: India has established comprehensive statutory bodies under the RPwD Act, 2016 that require the direct participation of persons…
points.text: 526. India has established comprehensive statutory bodies under the RPwD Act, 2016 that require the direct participation of persons with disabilities or their representative organizations (OPDs) in the conduct of public affairs and policy formulation.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: G. Consultative Governance and Institutional Mechanisms for Public Participation

points.title: Central Advisory Board on Disability (Section 60): The Act provides for a broad-based Central Advisory Board on Disability to s…
points.text: 527. Central Advisory Board on Disability (Section 60): The Act provides for a broad-based Central Advisory Board on Disability to serve as the apex policy-making body at the Central level. The Board’s composition mandates substantial representation of persons with disabilities: ten Members representing non-governmental organizations concerned with disabilities or disabled persons organizations, with at least five being women and at least one person each from the Scheduled Castes and the Scheduled Tribes, up to three representatives of national level chambers of commerce and industry, the Chairperson of the Medical Council of India as ex-officio member, and the Joint Secretary to the Government of India dealing with disability policy as Member-Secretary.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: G. Consultative Governance and Institutional Mechanisms for Public Participation

points.title: State Advisory Boards on Disability (Section 66): Under the RPwD Act, 2016, all States and Union Territories are required to co…
points.text: 528. State Advisory Boards on Disability (Section 66): Under the RPwD Act, 2016, all States and Union Territories are required to constitute State Advisory Boards to facilitate policy evolution and monitor implementation. States confirming establishment of State Advisory Boards include Goa, Meghalaya, Chandigarh, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, and Rajasthan. These boards serve as formal platforms for persons with disabilities to influence state-level policy decisions and program design.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: G. Consultative Governance and Institutional Mechanisms for Public Participation

points.title: District-Level Committees (Section 72): The RPwD Act mandates the establishment of District-Level Committees to monitor impleme…
points.text: 529. District-Level Committees (Section 72): The RPwD Act mandates the establishment of District-Level Committees to monitor implementation at the grassroots level. These committees have been established uniformly in districts across India. All 75 districts in Uttar Pradesh have constituted District-Level Committees. All 11 districts in Meghalaya have established these committees. All 3 districts in Andaman & Nicobar Islands have functional committees. Goa and Rajasthan have also established committees throughout their districts. The committees are legally required to include a PwD as a formal member to advise district authorities on implementation of the Act and schemes. These committees serve as monitoring mechanisms for district-level compliance with accessibility requirements, including electoral accessibility.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: G. Consultative Governance and Institutional Mechanisms for Public Participation

points.title: State Committees for Research on Disability: Several states have established dedicated research committees to study disability…
points.text: 530. State Committees for Research on Disability: Several states have established dedicated research committees to study disability issues and inform policy. For example: Meghalaya’s State Committee for Research on Disability includes five members representing each of the five groups of specified disabilities, and Rajasthan has constituted a similar research committee with representation from persons with disabilities across disability categories. These committees conduct research, collect data, and provide evidence-based recommendations to state governments
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: G. Consultative Governance and Institutional Mechanisms for Public Participation

points.title: State Commissioners for Persons with Disabilities: Independent State Commissioners have been appointed in multiple states to pr…
points.text: 531. State Commissioners for Persons with Disabilities: Independent State Commissioners have been appointed in multiple states to protect the rights of persons with disabilities and ensure their participation in society is not hindered by discrimination. State Commissioners have the authority to investigate complaints, monitor implementation of the RPwD Act, and recommend remedial measures to state governments
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: G. Consultative Governance and Institutional Mechanisms for Public Participation

points.title: The Ministry of Panchayati Raj coordinates on Article 29 implementation, noting that the rights enshrined in Article 29 of the…
points.text: 532. The Ministry of Panchayati Raj coordinates on Article 29 implementation, noting that the rights enshrined in Article 29 of the UNCRPD are in complete consonance with the Constitution of India and the RPwD Act, 2016. The Ministry works to ensure that Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs) at village, block, and district levels are accessible and that persons with disabilities can participate meaningfully in local governance.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: G. Consultative Governance and Institutional Mechanisms for Public Participation

points.title: States actively encourage persons with disabilities to form and join organizations to represent their interests.
points.text: 533. States actively encourage persons with disabilities to form and join organizations to represent their interests. For example, Meghalaya has noted that several registered NGOs working on disability issues receive state grants to run rehabilitation and vocational training programs. These organizations serve as vehicles for collective advocacy and participation in public affairs, extending beyond urban centres to rural areas.
points.article: Article 29: Participation in Political and Public Life
points.subsection: G. Consultative Governance and Institutional Mechanisms for Public Participation

points.title: Article 29(1) of the Constitution of India recognizes “that any section of the citizens residing in the territory of India or a…
points.text: 534. Article 29(1) of the Constitution of India recognizes “that any section of the citizens residing in the territory of India or any part thereof having a distinct language, script or culture of its own shall have the right to conserve the same”. Article 51A of the Indian Constitution makes it the duty of every citizen of India “to value and preserve the rich heritage of own composite culture” and “to strive towards excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity”.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Section 16 of the RPwD Act mandates that all educational institutions provide sports and recreation opportunities equally to ch…
points.text: 535. Section 16 of the RPwD Act mandates that all educational institutions provide sports and recreation opportunities equally to children with disabilities, while Section 30 specifically guarantees the right of persons with disabilities to participate in sporting, recreational, and cultural life.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Libraries: In August 2016, the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities (DEPwD) launched Sugamya Pustakalaya, an…
points.text: 536. Libraries: In August 2016, the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities (DEPwD) launched Sugamya Pustakalaya, an online accessible library in collaboration with the National Institute for the Empowerment of Persons with Visual Disabilities (Divyangjan), member organizations of Daisy Forum of India, Bookshare, and TCS Access. This digital library provides a centralized collection of accessible materials from across the country, offering books in formats such as Braille, audio, and large print for persons with visual impairments and print disabilities. The Goa State Central Library has implemented comprehensive accessibility features including tactile paving for navigation, audio-tactile pictograms, Braille signage throughout the facility, and image magnifiers to assist persons with visual impairments in accessing reading materials and navigating the library independently.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Films: To safeguard and recognize the interests of the person with disabilities, the Central Board of Film Certification has a…
points.text: 537. Films: To safeguard and recognize the interests of the person with disabilities, the Central Board of Film Certification has a set of guidelines one of them mandates that while granting certificates to films, the Board must ensure that scenes showing abuse or ridicule of physically and mentally ‘handicapped’ persons are not presented needlessly.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Television and Broadcasting Accessibility: In 2019, the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (MIB) formulated the “Accessib…
points.text: 538. Television and Broadcasting Accessibility: In 2019, the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (MIB) formulated the “Accessibility Standards for Persons with Disabilities in Television Programmes” to mandate accessible content across channels. Doordarshan, India’s public broadcaster, telecasts weekly and daily news bulletins with sign language interpretation. Sign language interpretation is also provided for major national events including Republic Day ceremonies and the Prime Minister’s monthly radio address “Mann Ki Baat,” ensuring that the Deaf community has equal access to national programming and civic information.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Museum Accessibility: Several museums across India have implemented accessibility measures to enable persons with disabilities…
points.text: 539. Museum Accessibility: Several museums across India have implemented accessibility measures to enable persons with disabilities to engage with cultural heritage. The Government Museum and Art Gallery in Chandigarh features “touch and feel” objects and Braille labels on sculptures to assist visually impaired visitors in experiencing artworks through tactile exploration. Museums in Uttar Pradesh provide wheelchair lifts, ramps for barrier-free access, and organize educational tours specifically designed for specially-abled students to ensure inclusive cultural education.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Accessible Cultural Texts: The Madras High Court directed authorities to provide a Braille version of the Thirukkural, a classi…
points.text: 540. Accessible Cultural Texts: The Madras High Court directed authorities to provide a Braille version of the Thirukkural, a classical Tamil text of significant cultural importance, to ensure visually impaired individuals can independently enjoy and study their cultural heritage. This judicial intervention demonstrates the recognition of cultural rights as fundamental to the dignity and identity of persons with disabilities.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Monuments: In 2011, the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment (MoSJE) approved a grant of 0.83 million USD to the Archaeol…
points.text: 541. Monuments: In 2011, the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment (MoSJE) approved a grant of 0.83 million USD to the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) to make five of its World Heritage Sites - the Taj Mahal, Agra Fort, Fatehpur Sikri, Sun Temple-Konark, and Jami Masjid Champaner-Pavgadh and 25 other ticketed monuments accessible to PwDs. The funds are to be used to make access pathways, ramps, toilets, tactile maps, Braille signage, and modifications in ticket counters for PwDs. The ASI has also brought out the publication on World Heritage Properties, in Braille. The Madras High Court has also ordered the government to devise comprehensive programs making tourist destinations accessible to persons with disabilities and to publish a dedicated travel guide specifically for accessible tourist locations, enabling persons with disabilities to plan and undertake tourism activities independently and with dignity.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Barriers in terms of Intellectual Property Rights: Under the present legal copyright regime in India, the conversion and use of…
points.text: 542. Barriers in terms of Intellectual Property Rights: Under the present legal copyright regime in India, the conversion and use of the printed material in its converted form requires permission of the copyright holders. Whereas Section 52(a)(i) of the Copyright Act, 1957 allows reproduction of a copyrighted work for private use including research, such an exclusion does not include conversion and use of printed material for recreational purposes or for use of PwDs in accessible formats. The Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012 has introduced exceptions to copyright for physically challenged and compulsory license provision to make accessibility of work in all formats, specifically through Section 52(1)(zb), which allows authorized entities such as educational institutions and NGOs to make accessible format copies of books for beneficiaries without requiring permission from copyright holders. India signed the Marrakesh Treaty to Facilitate Access to Published Works for Persons Who Are Blind, Visually Impaired, or Otherwise Print Disabled on 30 April 2014 and became the first country to ratify it on 24 June 2014.
