This was adopted by the Diplomatic Conference to Conclude a Treaty to Facilitate Access to Published Works by Visually Impaired Persons and Persons with Print Disabilities in Marrakesh, on June 27, 2013, and entered into force globally on 30 September 2016. India’s early ratification demonstrated its commitment to addressing the global book famine, with the Copyright (Amendment) Act 2012 already being in harmony with the Treaty’s provisions.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Support for Artists with Disabilities: State governments have implemented schemes to support artists with disabilities.
points.text: 543. Support for Artists with Disabilities: State governments have implemented schemes to support artists with disabilities. Uttarakhand’s Sanskriti (Culture) Department provides a monthly pension to artists and writers with disabilities, recognizing their contribution to cultural life. Additionally, the department offers art gallery space at a special discount rate for artists with disabilities to display their work, providing them with platforms for cultural expression and economic opportunities through art.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Tourism: The Ministry of Tourism (MOT) has set up a system for implementation of minimum standards for accessibility of facilit…
points.text: 544. Tourism: The Ministry of Tourism (MOT) has set up a system for implementation of minimum standards for accessibility of facilities and services for the PwDs to be available in the hotels right from the project implementation stage. The Ministry of Tourism approves hotel projects and classifies operational hotels, convention centres etc. as per guidelines, laid down for this purpose. Provision has been made in the guidelines to keep the requirements of the differently able persons. The MOT has now made mandatory that classified hotels should have at least one dedicated room for the differently abled guest with wheel chair accessibility, suitable low height furniture, low peep hole, cupboard with sliding doors and low cloth hangers etc. The room is also required to have audible and visible (blinking light) alarm system. The bathroom is required to be wheelchair accessible with sliding door and suitable fixtures like low washbasin, low height toilet, and grab bars etc.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Public restrooms of classified hotels are required to be unisex, wheelchair accessible, with suitable fixtures of low height, g…
points.text: 545. Public restrooms of classified hotels are required to be unisex, wheelchair accessible, with suitable fixtures of low height, grab bars etc. Free accessibility has to be provided in all public areas and at least one restaurant in 5 Star and Star Deluxe hotels should have ramps with anti - slip floors at the entrance. The hotels are advised to have the rooms for the differently able guests to be located at the lowest level and closest to the elevator. They are also advised to sensitize all staff members of the hotel regarding the special requirements of the differently able persons so as to enable them to handle such guests with care. With a view to generate awareness and also to recognize the contribution, the MoT instituted an award to the hotels which provide best facilities for the differently able guests.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Divya Kala Mela: Since 2022, the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities (Divyangjan), in collaboration with the…
points.text: 546. Divya Kala Mela: Since 2022, the Department of Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities (Divyangjan), in collaboration with the National Divyangjan Finance and Development Corporation (NDFDC), has organized the Divya Kala Mela across multiple cities in India. As of December 2024, 22 editions of the Divya Kala Mela have been held in 21 cities including Delhi, Mumbai, Bhopal, Guwahati, Bengaluru, Pune, and Raipur. These events showcase products crafted by Divyang artisans, artists, and entrepreneurs, featuring home décor, lifestyle products, clothing, eco-friendly items, packaged foods, organic products, toys, jewellery, and paintings. Each Mela typically features over 100 Divyang participants from approximately 20 States and Union Territories. The Mela provides a national platform for economic empowerment, market access, and visibility for persons with disabilities, embodying the “Vocal for Local” initiative. Since inception, the Divya Kala Mela has generated over ₹14 crores in sales, directly contributing to the economic independence of participating artisans.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Each Divya Kala Mela features cultural programs titled “Divya Kala Shakti” showcasing performances by Divyang artists in music,…
points.text: 547. Each Divya Kala Mela features cultural programs titled “Divya Kala Shakti” showcasing performances by Divyang artists in music, dance, drama, and other performing arts, celebrating the theme “Ability in Disability.” The events also include Job Fairs (Divyangjan Rozgar Mela) and Loan Melas to provide financial assistance and employment opportunities to participants, fostering self-reliance and entrepreneurship among persons with disabilities.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Promotion of Linguistic and Cultural Identity of the Deaf Community: The National Institute of Open Schooling (NIOS) has introd…
points.text: 548. Promotion of Linguistic and Cultural Identity of the Deaf Community: The National Institute of Open Schooling (NIOS) has introduced Indian Sign Language (ISL) as a language subject at the secondary level, recognizing ISL as a legitimate language and promoting the linguistic identity of the Deaf community. The state of Goa has proposed offering ISL as a third language option under the National Education Policy, further mainstreaming sign language education and recognizing the distinct cultural and linguistic identity of Deaf persons.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: The Government has been collaborating with NGOs to raise awareness.
points.text: 549. The Government has been collaborating with NGOs to raise awareness. On August 12, 2011, the Union Minister of Tourism flagged off a “Beyond Barriers Incredible India Tour” by wheel-chair based persons in New Delhi. The Tour involved visits to 28 State Capitals and 40 cities in 81 days by a group of volunteers on wheel chairs.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Paralympic Committee of India: In the Year 2005, the Government recognized Paralympic Committee of India (PCI) at par with Indi…
points.text: 550. Paralympic Committee of India: In the Year 2005, the Government recognized Paralympic Committee of India (PCI) at par with Indian Olympic Association (IOA). From 2006, PCI started affiliating State Bodies & Federations and this paved way for uplifting Para Sports in India. PCI is dedicated for the development and upliftment of Physically Challenged Sports & Wheelchair Athletes with the aim and objectives inter alia to promote and contribute to the development of sport opportunities and competitions, from the start to elite level. PCI has been instrumental in India’s participation at various International Meets like Para Asian Games, World Competitions. For the first time, the Commonwealth Games 2010 hosted in Delhi saw sports PwDs compete in the same arena as mainstream sports. As a result of a Co-operative Agreement between The Commonwealth Games Federation and The International Paralympic Committee athletes with disabilities also got an opportunity to showcase their world-class sporting skills.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: India’s Paralympic Performance: India has witnessed unprecedented success in Paralympic sports.
points.text: 551. India’s Paralympic Performance: India has witnessed unprecedented success in Paralympic sports. At the Tokyo 2020 Paralympic Games (held in 2021), India won 19 medals (5 gold, 8 silver, 6 bronze), marking a historic achievement. This success was surpassed at the Paris 2024 Paralympic Games, where India achieved its highest-ever medal tally with 29 medals (7 gold, 9 silver, 13 bronze), competing in 12 sports disciplines with a contingent of 84 athletes. Notable achievements include Avani Lekhara becoming the first Indian woman athlete to win multiple Paralympic gold medals (winning gold in women’s 10m air rifle event in consecutive Games), Sumit Antil winning back-to-back gold medals in javelin throw F64 event and setting two Paralympic records in the process, Harvinder Singh winning India’s first Paralympic gold medal in archery, Kapil Parmar winning India’s first Paralympic bronze medal in judo, and Navdeep Singh winning gold in javelin throw F41 event. India also qualified for judo, rowing (mixed double sculls), and para-cycling for the first time at the Paris 2024 Games, demonstrating the broadening base of para-sports in the country.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: National Playing Fields Association of India (NPFAI): The Sports Ministry has signed an MOU with Fields in Trust, UK, for the f…
points.text: 552. National Playing Fields Association of India (NPFAI): The Sports Ministry has signed an MOU with Fields in Trust, UK, for the formation of the National Playing Fields Association of India (NPFAI) in February 2009. The main objective of the NPFAI is to develop, promote, protect and expand playing fields and open spaces in the country.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Indian Spinal Injuries Centre has established sports facilities for people with spinal cord injuries (wheelchairs users) to hav…
points.text: 553. Indian Spinal Injuries Centre has established sports facilities for people with spinal cord injuries (wheelchairs users) to have them provided the opportunities to participate in various sports activities like wheelchair rugby, wheelchairs basketball, wheelchair tennis and lawn tennis to increase their functional level and physical capacity to be active.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: National Institute of Mental Health has associated with Special Olympics for persons with mental retardation both at local and…
points.text: 554. National Institute of Mental Health has associated with Special Olympics for persons with mental retardation both at local and national level and has been organizing programmes various leisure activities for children and persons with mental retardation such as dance, music festival, picnics, etc.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: National Institute for Empowerment of Persons with Multiple Disabilities for the first time organised Southern regional sports…
points.text: 555. National Institute for Empowerment of Persons with Multiple Disabilities for the first time organised Southern regional sports for persons with multiple disabilities with the objective of exploring hidden talents and potentials of persons with multiple disabilities.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Schemes and Programs to promote Disability Sports: The Ministry of Youth Affairs & Sports, Department of Sports is implementing…
points.text: 556. Schemes and Programs to promote Disability Sports: The Ministry of Youth Affairs & Sports, Department of Sports is implementing the schemes aimed at promotion and development of sports among PwDs. These include the Khelo India Scheme (revamped in 2017-18 with twelve verticals including a dedicated vertical for “Promotion of sports among persons with disabilities”) and the Target Olympic Podium Scheme (TOPS), which provides comprehensive support to elite para-athletes for Olympic and Paralympic preparation.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Khelo India Scheme: Launched in 2016-17 and revamped in 2017-18, the Khelo India Scheme includes playfield development, communi…
points.text: 557. Khelo India Scheme: Launched in 2016-17 and revamped in 2017-18, the Khelo India Scheme includes playfield development, community coaching development, establishment of sports competition structures, and dedicated support for sports for persons with disabilities. The scheme comprises components including: (1) Creation and Upgradation of Sports Infrastructure; (2) Annual Sports Competitions including Khelo India Youth Games, Khelo India University Games, Khelo India Para Games, and Khelo India Winter Games; (3) Talent Search and Development; (4) Sports for Women; (5) Support to National/Regional/State Sports Academies; and (6) Promotion of sports among persons with disabilities. Under this vertical, funds totalling ₹13.73 crore have been released as of 2020 for promotion of sports among disabled sportspersons. The indicative estimated expenditure for this vertical for 2018-19 and 2019-20 was ₹15 crore each year.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Khelo India Para Games: The Khelo India Para Games are organized annually to provide a platform for talent spotting and develop…
points.text: 558. Khelo India Para Games: The Khelo India Para Games are organized annually to provide a platform for talent spotting and development of para-athletes across India. The first edition of Khelo India Para Games was successfully conducted, with subsequent editions providing competitive opportunities for para-athletes. As of 2024, six editions of Khelo India Youth Games, four editions of Khelo India University Games, and five editions of Khelo India Winter Games have been conducted with participation from 36 States/UTs. Financial assistance of ₹6.00 crore was extended to Special Olympics Bharat, All India Sports Council of Deaf, and Paralympic Committee of India for the year 2019-20 to support District and State Games for Divyang athletes. An additional fund of ₹19.75 lakhs was sanctioned to conduct the Indian Open Para Athletics Championship in 2018.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Target Olympic Podium Scheme (TOPS): TOPS was formulated in 2014 and completely revamped to provide sustained support to sporti…
points.text: 559. Target Olympic Podium Scheme (TOPS): TOPS was formulated in 2014 and completely revamped to provide sustained support to sporting excellence. The scheme provides comprehensive support to elite athletes preparing for Olympic and Paralympic Games. Support to para-athletes through TOPS has increased significantly from 9 athletes in 2015 to 32 athletes in 2021. Core group athletes under TOPS receive an Out-of-Pocket Allowance (OPA) of ₹50,000 per month, alongside full funding for their personalized training plans approved by the Mission Olympic Cell (MOC). The scheme provides support for specialized equipment, foreign exposure, coaching, sports science support, and other requirements of elite para-athletes.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: SAI National Centre of Excellence for Para Sports: The Sports Authority of India established a dedicated National Centre of Exc…
points.text: 560. SAI National Centre of Excellence for Para Sports: The Sports Authority of India established a dedicated National Centre of Excellence for para-athletes in Gandhinagar, which houses over 90 para campers across six sports: para athletics, para powerlifting, para swimming, para badminton, para table tennis, and para fencing. The centre provides dedicated para-specific sports ground facilities with special attention to accommodation facilities, training equipment, and specialized coaching support tailored to the needs of para-athletes.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: The Ministry of Youth Affairs and Sports has notified the “Guidelines on Accessible Sports Complex and Residential Facilities f…
points.text: 561. The Ministry of Youth Affairs and Sports has notified the “Guidelines on Accessible Sports Complex and Residential Facilities for Sports Persons with Disabilities.” These mandatory guidelines require: ● Barrier-free routes and universal design principles in all sports halls and facilities ● At least one accessible shower, washroom, and changing room per sports facility ● Specific seating ratios for wheelchair users in stadiums (for example, 6 wheelchair-accessible spaces for stadiums with up to 600 seats, with proportional increases for larger venues) ● Accessible parking spaces, entrance ramps, and signage These guidelines ensure that all new and renovated sports infrastructure is designed with accessibility as a core requirement rather than an afterthought.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: At the state and Union Territory level, inclusive initiatives highlight diverse approaches to disability sports and holistic de…
points.text: 562. At the state and Union Territory level, inclusive initiatives highlight diverse approaches to disability sports and holistic development: Andaman & Nicobar sent a four-member para-athletics team to the Khelo India Para Games, winning two gold medals; Goa runs a dedicated “Sports for Differently Abled School Children” scheme for students in special schools, offering coaching, equipment, and competition exposure; Uttarakhand provides financial assistance for specialised sports equipment, including sports wheelchairs for para-badminton players; and Chandigarh promotes inclusive development through initiatives such as “TAFI” (The Art Festival for Inclusion) alongside the Samagra Shiksha programme, which integrates sports and skill training for children with special needs as part of inclusive education.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Scheme of assistance to National Sports Federation: Assistance is provided to National Sports Federations for conducting Nation…
points.text: 563. Scheme of assistance to National Sports Federation: Assistance is provided to National Sports Federations for conducting National Championships and International Tournaments in India, participation in international tournaments abroad, organizing coaching camps, procuring sports equipment and so on. The most recent Annual Report (2023-24) of the Ministry of Youth Affairs and Sports provides the following details of recurring grant-in-aid sanctioned to National Sports Federations for the 2022-23 period: Name of Federation Grant-in-Aid (Rs in crore) Para Olympic Committee of India Rs 9.16 crore All India Sports Council of the Deaf Rs 4.85 crore Special Olympic Bharat Rs 3.62 crore
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: These funds were part of a total Rs 116.69 crore sanctioned to private institutions and sports organisations during that year t…
points.text: 564. These funds were part of a total Rs 116.69 crore sanctioned to private institutions and sports organisations during that year to promote excellence in sports.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Scheme of Pension to Meritorious Sportspersons was launched in the year 1994.
points.text: 565. Scheme of Pension to Meritorious Sportspersons was launched in the year 1994. Sportspersons who are Indian citizens and have won gold, silver and bronze medals and have attained age of 30 years as well as have retired from active sports career are eligible for life pension.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: The Association for Cricket for Blind in India (ACBI) was set up in 1996 with an objective to use competitive cricket to inculc…
points.text: 566. The Association for Cricket for Blind in India (ACBI) was set up in 1996 with an objective to use competitive cricket to inculcate positive outlook and confidence in blind. The ACBI organised the first Blind Cricket World Cup in 1998. National Institute for the Visually Handicapped (NIVH), Dehradun, developed the audio ball made of hard plastic with ball bearings inside. It is now accepted as per the international standard.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: India hosted the 2025 World Para Athletics Championships in New Delhi, where its para-athletes achieved a record-breaking perfo…
points.text: 567. India hosted the 2025 World Para Athletics Championships in New Delhi, where its para-athletes achieved a record-breaking performance, winning a total of 22 medals (including 6 gold, 9 silver, and 7 bronze) and finishing 10th overall against competitors from over 100 nations, marking the nation’s best result at this global para-athletics meet.
points.article: Article 30: Participation in Cultural Life, Recreation, Leisure and Sports

points.title: Article 31 recognizes the importance of comparable data on disability that is critical for planning, implementation, monitoring…
points.text: 568. Article 31 recognizes the importance of comparable data on disability that is critical for planning, implementation, monitoring, and evaluation of inclusive policies. In this context, the statement: “Persons with disabilities include those who have long term physical, mental, intellectual or sensory impairments which in interaction with various barriers may hinder their full and effective participation in society on an equal basis with others”, as expressed clearly in the convention, marks a shift from medical model to a social model of disability that the statistics of a country has to respond to.

points.title: The RPwD Act 2016 provides a comprehensive legal framework for systematic data collection across various sectors to fulfill Ind…
points.text: 569. The RPwD Act 2016 provides a comprehensive legal framework for systematic data collection across various sectors to fulfill India’s obligations under Article 31 of the UNCRPD: ●Employment Records (Section 22): Every establishment is legally required to maintain records of persons with disabilities regarding employment, facilities provided, and other necessary information. Section 22(2) mandates that every employment exchange must maintain records of PwDs seeking employment, enabling tracking of employment patterns and identifying barriers to workforce participation. ●Disaster Preparedness Records (Section 8(3)): District Disaster Management Authorities (DDMAs) are required to maintain records of persons with disabilities within their districts to ensure their protection and safety during risks, armed conflicts, humanitarian emergencies, and natural disasters. This data enables targeted preparedness and evacuation planning. ●Educational Monitoring (Section 16(vii)): Educational institutions are mandated to monitor the participation, progress, and completion of education for every student with a disability, ensuring that disaggregated data is available to track educational outcomes and identify areas requiring intervention. ●School Surveys (Section 17(a)): The Act mandates a survey of school-going children every five years to identify children with disabilities and ascertain their specific educational needs, enabling evidence-based planning for inclusive education. ●Research Committees (Section 6(2)): States are required to constitute a Committee for Research on Disability to facilitate ongoing research, policy evolution, and evidence-based interventions.

points.title: The major sources of statistics on disabilities are the decadal Population Censuses and nationwide sample surveys on disability…
points.text: 570. The major sources of statistics on disabilities are the decadal Population Censuses and nationwide sample surveys on disability conducted by National Sample Survey Organization (NSSO). Additionally, the National Family Health Survey (NFHS) has emerged as an important source of disability data, with NFHS-5 (2019-21) being the first round to include comprehensive disability status information.

points.title: The 2011 Census is the latest complete census, with the scheduled 2021 Census being postponed and likely to take place in 2027.
points.text: 571. The 2011 Census is the latest complete census, with the scheduled 2021 Census being postponed and likely to take place in 2027. In Census 2011, information in respect of eight types of disabilities was collected. The eight types are referred as (i) In seeing, (ii) In hearing (iii) In speech (iv) In movement (v) Mental Retardation (vi) Mental Illness, (vii) Multiple disabilities (viii) Any other. Census 2011 identified 26.8 million persons with disabilities in India, constituting 2.21% of the total population. Of these, 56% (14.9 million) were males and 44% (11.9 million) were females. Approximately 69% of persons with disabilities (18.6 million) resided in rural areas, while 31% (8.1 million) lived in urban areas. The Census 2011 remains a primary reference point for literacy and demographic data on persons with disabilities across states including Goa, Meghalaya, and Uttarakhand.

points.title: The Census of 2011 is the 15th edition of census since 1872, and the 7th since independence.
points.text: 572. The Census of 2011 is the 15th edition of census since 1872, and the 7th since independence. The definitions were broadly in conformity with those recommended by the aforesaid Technical Advisory Committee (TAC). In Census 2011, the question was moved to number 9 in the priority that right after name, age, sex, religion etc. The definitions, concepts and the instructions were designed in such a manner that the question was canvassed appropriately and improving the chances of netting the disability characteristics of the population. The enumerators were instructed to obtain the response to this question for every number of the household from the main respondent who answered other questions. Annexure 10 covers the disability related questions in Census 2011.

points.title: Publicity: A systematic publicity campaign was launched through print and electronic media that helped in creating an atmospher…
points.text: 573. Publicity: A systematic publicity campaign was launched through print and electronic media that helped in creating an atmosphere where Census 2011 enumerators could canvass the question on disability effectively. The Census used the television, radio, print media and the internet to disseminate information for creating better awareness. A film on ‘Get Yourself Counted’ was made for regional language television channels. The issue got covered on television talk shows, among other things. Information, Education and Communication (IEC) materials like handbills, posters, and leaflets in local languages were distributed in villages and to enumerators.

points.title: Public Private Partnership: The Census Organization was not alone in its endeavour to sensitize the general public about the im…
points.text: 574. Public Private Partnership: The Census Organization was not alone in its endeavour to sensitize the general public about the importance of the Census of India 2011 and seeking their cooperation. Other Union Ministries, civil society organizations, particularly those, working for the welfare of PwDs also put in their intensive efforts to ensure that people come forward and provided correct information in respect of the questions concerning disability.

points.title: National Sample Survey 76th Round (2018): The National Statistical Office (NSO), Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implement…
points.text: 575. National Sample Survey 76th Round (2018): The National Statistical Office (NSO), Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation, conducted a comprehensive Survey of Persons with Disabilities during July-December 2018 as part of the 76th round of the National Sample Survey (NSS). This was the first major disability survey after the enactment of the RPwD Act 2016 and the first such survey since the 58th round conducted in July-December 2002, representing a gap of 16 years between disability surveys.

points.title: The NSS 76th round adopted the classification of disabilities as specified in the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016…
points.text: 576. The NSS 76th round adopted the classification of disabilities as specified in the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, marking a significant methodological advancement. The survey employed a stratified two-stage sampling design using Census 2011 as the sampling frame. The survey covered 8,992 village/urban blocks (5,378 rural villages and 3,614 urban blocks) across all states and union territories of India except villages in Andaman and Nicobar Islands which are difficult to access. Data were collected from 1,18,152 households (81,004 in rural areas and 37,148 in urban areas) and enumerated 5,76,569 persons (4,02,589 in rural areas and 1,73,980 in urban areas). A total of 1,06,894 persons with disabilities (74,946 in rural areas and 31,948 in urban areas) were surveyed using Computer Assisted Personal Interviewing (CAPI) technique, with an inbuilt schedule to maintain confidentiality of respondents’ answers.

points.title: The NSS 76th Round survey found that the prevalence of disability (percentage of persons with disability in the population) was…
points.text: 577. The NSS 76th Round survey found that the prevalence of disability (percentage of persons with disability in the population) was 2.2% in India, with 2.3% in rural areas and 2.0% in urban areas. Prevalence was higher among males (2.4%) than females (1.9%). The incidence of disability in the population – the number of persons with onset of disability (by birth or otherwise) during the 365 days preceding the date of survey – was 86 per 1,00,000 persons.

points.title: The survey collected comprehensive data on multiple dimensions of disability including: (a) incidence and prevalence of disabil…
points.text: 578. The survey collected comprehensive data on multiple dimensions of disability including: (a) incidence and prevalence of disability; (b) cause of disability and age at onset; (c) facilities available to persons with disability; (d) difficulties faced by persons with disability in accessing/using public buildings and public transport; (e) arrangement of regular caregivers; (f) out-of-pocket expenses relating to disability; (g) employment and unemployment particulars; (h) vocational and technical training received or being received; and (i) whether persons possess disability certificates and the percentage of disability as per certificate.

points.title: Accessibility Challenges Identified: The NSS 76th round revealed significant accessibility barriers.
points.text: 579. Accessibility Challenges Identified: The NSS 76th round revealed significant accessibility barriers. During the 365 days preceding the survey, 67.1% of persons with disabilities reported facing difficulties in accessing public transport (including buses, trams, trains, metros, and ferries) due to inadequate public announcement systems, absence of ramps, grooved tiles, lifts, specialized toilet seats, and directional signs. Additionally, 63.9% of persons with disabilities faced difficulties in accessing public buildings (for purposes such as assembly, education, entertainment, workplace, office, or worship). Approximately 5% of persons with disabilities could not afford to undergo required treatment, with this percentage being higher in rural areas (5.7%) than in urban areas (3.5%).

points.title: Support and Services: The survey found that among persons with disabilities aged 3-35 years, 62.9% were ever enrolled in ordina…
points.text: 580. Support and Services: The survey found that among persons with disabilities aged 3-35 years, 62.9% were ever enrolled in ordinary schools and 4.1% were in specialized schools or received specialized therapy (such as speech or behavioural therapy) relating to their disability. Over one-fifth of persons with disabilities reported receiving government aid for education or training, corrective surgery or related treatment, employment, disability pension, or other means of help. However, 76.4% did not receive any support, and only 1.8% received aid or help from organizations other than the government. The labour force participation rate among persons with disabilities aged 15 years and above was 23.8%.

points.title: Economic Impact: Analysis of NSS 76th round data revealed that approximately one-fifth (20.32%) of households’ monthly consumpt…
points.text: 581. Economic Impact: Analysis of NSS 76th round data revealed that approximately one-fifth (20.32%) of households’ monthly consumption expenditure was spent on out-of-pocket expenditure for disability. More than half (57.1%) of households were pushed to catastrophic health expenditure due to one of the members being disabled. Almost one-fifth (19.1%) of households who were above the poverty line before one of their members was treated for disability were pushed below the poverty line after the treatment expenditure, with an average percentage shortfall in income from the poverty line of 11.0% due to disability treatment care expenditure.

points.title: For the first time in its history, the fifth National Family Health Survey (NFHS-5) included disability as a new topic of inves…
points.text: 582. For the first time in its history, the fifth National Family Health Survey (NFHS-5) included disability as a new topic of investigation, representing a major advancement in India’s disability data collection efforts. The NFHS-5 was conducted by the International Institute for Population Sciences (IIPS), Mumbai, under the stewardship of the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, Government of India, with technical assistance from ICF through the Demographic and Health Surveys (DHS) Program funded by USAID.

points.title: The NFHS-5 fieldwork was conducted in two phases: Phase I from 17 June 2019 to 30 January 2020 covering 17 states and 5 union t…
points.text: 583. The NFHS-5 fieldwork was conducted in two phases: Phase I from 17 June 2019 to 30 January 2020 covering 17 states and 5 union territories, and Phase II from 2 January 2020 to 30 April 2021 covering 11 states and 3 union territories. The survey employed a multistage stratified sampling design covering all states and union territories of India, drawing 30,459 Primary Sampling Units (PSUs) from 707 districts. The survey successfully gathered information from 636,699 households and interviewed 2,843,917 household members, including 724,115 women aged 15-49 years and 101,839 men aged 15-54 years, with response rates of 97.5%, 96.9%, and 91.6% respectively. Data collection was conducted using 1,061 field teams, each consisting of one field supervisor, three female interviewers, one male interviewer, two health investigators, and a driver, using Computer Assisted Personal Interviewing (CAPI) technique in 18 local languages.

points.title: NFHS-5 collected information on disability status of individuals in the household questionnaire from the de-jure population by…
points.text: 584. NFHS-5 collected information on disability status of individuals in the household questionnaire from the de-jure population by asking: “Does any usual resident of your household, including you, have any disability?” Individuals identified with disabilities were further classified according to their disability types. The survey included five disability statuses to depict the burden of disability: seeing, hearing, walking, remembering/concentrating, and self-care difficulties. This represented the first comprehensive national- level household survey to systematically collect disability data alongside health, nutrition, and demographic indicators, providing district-level estimates for many important indicators.

points.title: The NFHS-5 data serves multiple purposes: (a) providing essential data on health and family welfare needed for policy and progr…
points.text: 585. The NFHS-5 data serves multiple purposes: (a) providing essential data on health and family welfare needed for policy and programme formulation; (b) setting benchmarks and examining progress the health sector has made over time; (c) providing evidence for the effectiveness of ongoing programmes; (d) identifying the need for new programmes with area-specific focus; (e) identifying groups most in need of essential services; and (f) tracking 30 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) that India aims to achieve by 2030. The survey’s disability data is particularly valuable as it provides socioeconomic and geographic variations of disabilities across states, union territories, and districts, enabling targeted interventions.

points.title: Statistical Publications and Data Dissemination: The Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation publishes regular stat…
points.text: 586. Statistical Publications and Data Dissemination: The Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation publishes regular statistical profiles on persons with disabilities. The report “Persons with Disabilities (Divyangjan) in India - A Statistical Profile: 2021” provides comprehensive analysis based on Census 2011 and NSS 76th round data, covering key aspects such as education, employment, economic conditions, literacy rates, and demographic distribution of persons with disabilities. According to this publication, approximately 54.4% of persons with disabilities are literate, with Kerala having the highest literacy rate among disabled persons at 70.8% and Arunachal Pradesh the lowest at 38.8%. Nearly one-third (approximately 31%) of all persons with disabilities in India are working, with 47% of male and 23% of female disabled population being employed.

points.title: Among children with disabilities aged 5-19 years, Census 2011 data shows that 61% are enrolled in educational institutions, 12%…
points.text: 587. Among children with disabilities aged 5-19 years, Census 2011 data shows that 61% are enrolled in educational institutions, 12% have been in such institutions in the past, and 27% have never attended any educational institution. Notably, 50% of children with mental disabilities have never attended any educational institution, highlighting significant gaps in inclusive education. Among the total disabled population, 45% are illiterates, while 13% have matric/secondary education but are not graduates, and 5% are graduates and above. Approximately 8.5% of disabled literates are graduates or above.

points.title: Following the mandate under Section 6(2) of the RPwD Act 2016 to constitute State-Level Committees for Research on Disability,…
points.text: 588. Following the mandate under Section 6(2) of the RPwD Act 2016 to constitute State-Level Committees for Research on Disability, several states have established research committees and undertaken state- specific data collection efforts: ●Rajasthan: Constituted a State Level Committee for research on disability, nominating five members representing different disability groups to facilitate evidence-based policy formulation. ●Meghalaya: Established a research committee and conducts an annual survey to identify children with disabilities, specifically targeting the identification of children with learning disabilities for mainstreaming into regular education systems. ●Mizoram: The Social Welfare Department published a specific book titled “Persons with Disabilities (PwDs) in Mizoram 2021” to capture updated state-level statistics, providing localized data for targeted interventions. ●Chandigarh and Uttar Pradesh: Confirmed the establishment of research committees on disability to facilitate ongoing research and policy evolution at the state level. These state-level initiatives complement national data collection efforts by providing granular, context- specific information that enables more targeted and effective policy interventions.

points.title: Disaggregated Data and Programme Monitoring: Government authorities utilize disaggregated data to assess implementation of spec…
points.text: 589. Disaggregated Data and Programme Monitoring: Government authorities utilize disaggregated data to assess implementation of specific schemes and identify barriers to inclusion: ●National Rural Employment (MGNREGS): The Ministry of Rural Development maintains annual records tracking the number of persons with disabilities provided with wage employment under the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme. Data shows that 606,149 persons with disabilities worked under MGNREGS in 2020-21, and 576,250 in 2021-22, enabling monitoring of rural employment opportunities for persons with disabilities. ●Technical Education: The All India Council for Technical Education (AICTE) tracks enrolment of persons with disabilities in technical institutions. AICTE recorded 4,721 PwD enrolments in government-aided technical institutions over a three-year period (2021-2024). AICTE also mandates that institutions establish a Disability Database Management System to systematically track enrolment, progression, and completion rates of students with disabilities. ●Social Protection: The National Social Assistance Programme (NSAP) maintains disaggregated data on beneficiaries and expenditure for the National Disability Pension Scheme. In 2022-23, NSAP recorded 823,675 beneficiaries receiving disability pensions with a total expenditure of ₹278.57 crore, enabling monitoring of social protection coverage for persons with disabilities.

points.title: Following the enactment of the RPwD Act 2016, India’s disability data collection has incorporated several methodological improv…
points.text: 590. Following the enactment of the RPwD Act 2016, India’s disability data collection has incorporated several methodological improvements: ●Expanded Classification: Data collection now encompasses all 21 types of disabilities specified in the RPwD Act 2016, compared to 7 types in the previous PWD Act 1995. This includes new categories such as acid attack victims, specific learning disability, speech and language disability, dwarfism, muscular dystrophy, thalassemia, hemophilia, and sickle cell disease. ●Social Model Approach: Surveys now adopt questions and definitions aligned with the social model of disability, recognizing that disability results from the interaction between impairments and environmental/attitudinal barriers, consistent with UNCRPD principles. ●Computer-Assisted Data Collection: Use of Computer Assisted Personal Interviewing (CAPI) techniques ensures better data quality through built-in validation checks, reduced transcription errors, and immediate identification of inconsistencies. ●Comprehensive Scope: Modern surveys collect data not only on prevalence but also on accessibility challenges, support services, financial burden, employment status, educational attainment, and quality of life indicators. ●Multi-Source Data: Integration of data from multiple sources including Census, NSS, NFHS, and administrative records provides a more complete picture of the disability situation in India.

points.title: India has integrated privacy protections and accessibility requirements into its data handling and dissemination processes, ens…
points.text: 591. India has integrated privacy protections and accessibility requirements into its data handling and dissemination processes, ensuring compliance with UNCRPD Article 31 provisions on confidentiality and accessibility: ●ICT Privacy Standards: The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) formulated the Indian Accessibility Standard IS 17802, which includes Functional Performance Requirements for Privacy. This standard ensures that Information and Communication Technology (ICT) features that are contributory to accessibility (such as screen readers or personal headsets) maintain the same level of privacy for persons with disabilities as for other users, preventing unauthorized access to personal information. ●Accessible Dissemination: MeitY’s standards mandate that information intended for the general public be provided in accessible formats without additional cost, ensuring that persons with disabilities can access statistical information on equal basis with others. The Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (MIB) directs all media units to follow GIGW 3.0 (Guidelines for Indian Government Websites 3.0) standards and appoint accessibility auditors to ensure compliance, making disability statistics available in formats usable by persons with visual, hearing, and cognitive disabilities. ●Judicial Records Accessibility: The Supreme Court of India has emphasized that statistical information in the form of judicial records must be accessible to persons with disabilities. The Court directed all courts and tribunals to upload digitally signed, accessible versions of judgments (rather than mere scanned images) to ensure visually impaired individuals using screen readers can access judicial data and statistics. This directive ensures that persons with disabilities have equal access to legal information and court records. ●Confidentiality Protections: Data collection protocols under NSS, NFHS, and other surveys include strict confidentiality safeguards, with the Collection of Statistics Act, 2008 mandating that collected data are used solely for statistical purposes and individual identities are protected. CAPI systems include built-in privacy protections to ensure respondents’ disability status and other personal information remain confidential. In addition, data collection of all digital personal data, including that of PwDs, must be in accordance with the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, which imposes obligations such as informed consent and notice on all data fiduciaries.

points.title: Open Data Platform: The Government of India has made disability statistics available through the Open Government Data (OGD) Pla…
points.text: 592. Open Data Platform: The Government of India has made disability statistics available through the Open Government Data (OGD) Platform India, providing single-point access to disability-related datasets published by various Ministries, Departments, and Organizations. This facilitates research, policy analysis, and evidence-based decision-making by making unit-level data from Census, NSS, and NFHS publicly available to researchers, policymakers, and civil society organizations. The platform ensures transparency and enables independent verification of disability statistics, contributing to improved accountability in implementation of disability rights.

points.title: Article 51 (b) of the Constitution of India lays down that the State shall endeavor to: “maintain just and honourable relations…
points.text: 593. Article 51 (b) of the Constitution of India lays down that the State shall endeavor to: “maintain just and honourable relations among nations”. Article 253 of the Constitution gives power to Parliament to make any law to give effect to International agreements. These two provisions in the Constitution assist India to fulfil its obligations under this article.

points.title: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, which came into effect on 15 June 2017, was enacted to align India’s nationa…
points.text: 594. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, which came into effect on 15 June 2017, was enacted to align India’s national legislation with UNCRPD obligations, replacing the PWD Act 1995 and expanding disability categories from 7 to 21 types. As of 2024, the UNCRPD has been ratified by 191 parties (190 States and the European Union), with India being an active participant in the global disability rights framework.

points.title: Central authorities, particularly the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA), have integrated disability inclusion into…
points.text: 595. Central authorities, particularly the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA), have integrated disability inclusion into disaster management policies in direct alignment with the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015-2030). This international framework guides India’s approach to ensuring that persons with disabilities have equal protection and safety in situations of risk, armed conflict, humanitarian emergencies, and natural disasters, demonstrating India’s commitment to implementing international best practices in disaster preparedness and response for persons with disabilities.

points.title: The World Bank and International Monetary Funds’ effort at mainstreaming disability development is effectuated through their co…
points.text: 596. The World Bank and International Monetary Funds’ effort at mainstreaming disability development is effectuated through their country specific Poverty Reduction Strategy Process.

points.title: The Disability Rights Fund (DRF) encourages and promotes the respect for the general principles stated in the Convention on the…
points.text: 597. The Disability Rights Fund (DRF) encourages and promotes the respect for the general principles stated in the Convention on the Rights of PwDs, the need to ensure the full participation of PwDs in all spheres of life, including the development of national and international laws, policies and programs. DRF supports Disabled Peoples Organizations to take the lead in advocating for the human rights of PwDs at local and national levels, utilizing the mechanism of the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities.

points.title: There are various other organizations other which are supporting disability programs in India.
points.text: 598. There are various other organizations other which are supporting disability programs in India. Some of them are: CBM, Germany; Sight Savers, UK; Sense International, UK; Action Aid, UK; LCH Disability, UK; Voice and Vision, USA; Asian Blind Union; International Council for the Education of Persons with Visual Impairment; Rehabilitation International; Disabled People’s International; Abilympic International; Misereor, Germany; International Association for the Prevention of Blindness; Inclusion International; Low Vision International; Deaf blind International; Vision 2020; and Japan International Development Council.

points.title: India actively promotes the creation of partnerships between all stakeholders, including international development organization…
points.text: 599. India actively promotes the creation of partnerships between all stakeholders, including international development organizations, Organizations of Persons with Disabilities (OPDs/DPOs), and non- governmental organizations (NGOs) to remove participation barriers and advance disability rights. Higher education guidelines emphasize multi-stakeholder collaboration involving international partners to enhance accessibility and inclusion in educational institutions. These partnerships facilitate knowledge exchange, capacity building, and implementation of international best practices in disability inclusion across various sectors.

points.title: UNICEF has been helping Ramakrishna Mission Blind Boys Academy in Kolkata to produce Braille version of the Convention on the R…
points.text: 600. UNICEF has been helping Ramakrishna Mission Blind Boys Academy in Kolkata to produce Braille version of the Convention on the Rights of Child (CRC) and also other Braille productions. UNESCO, on the other hand, has concentrated its monetary assistance on inclusive education. Besides International Organisations, co-operation has been achieved amongst different Nation States. For instance, the United States Agency for International Development funds programs that focus on healthcare issues polio eradication, HIV/AIDS, child survival and infectious diseases, and thus directly and indirectly targeting prevention of disability. In particular, USAID has focussed on employment opportunities and has provided financial support for women and children to participate in various international conferences on disability.

points.title: Marrakesh Treaty: To support the right to education and cultural life for persons with visual impairments and print disabilitie…
points.text: 601. Marrakesh Treaty: To support the right to education and cultural life for persons with visual impairments and print disabilities, India actively promotes the cross-border exchange of resources in accessible formats (such as Braille, audio books, and large print) in line with the Marrakesh Treaty to Facilitate Access to Published Works for Persons Who Are Blind, Visually Impaired, or Otherwise Print Disabled, which India signed on 30 April 2014 and was the first country to ratify on 24 June 2014.

points.title: Educational institutions in India participate in international partnerships to enhance access to accessible educational materials.
points.text: 602. Educational institutions in India participate in international partnerships to enhance access to accessible educational materials. The Indira Gandhi National Open University (IGNOU) is a member of international partnerships such as the Accessible Books Consortium (ABC) Global Book Service, enabling students with print disabilities to access hundreds of thousands of accessible titles from global repositories. This international cooperation ensures that Indian students with disabilities have access to the same breadth of educational materials as their peers worldwide, facilitating educational equity and inclusion.

points.title: International Sports Cooperation: The Department of Sports provides financial support to organizations and athletes with disabi…
points.text: 603. International Sports Cooperation: The Department of Sports provides financial support to organizations and athletes with disabilities participating in international sports events, facilitating the exchange of best practices in para-sports and showcasing Indian talent on global platforms. India supports participation in events such as the Deaflympics (for athletes with hearing impairments), World Youth Badminton Championships, and other international para-sports competitions. This cooperation enables Indian para-athletes to compete at the highest levels, learn from international peers, and contribute to global advancement of disability sports.

points.title: India’s contribution to global disability research and assistive technology is exemplified by the Jaipur Foot initiative of the…
points.text: 604. India’s contribution to global disability research and assistive technology is exemplified by the Jaipur Foot initiative of the Bhagwan Mahaveer Viklang Sahayata Samiti (BMVSS), established in 1975, which is the world’s largest organisation serving persons with physical disabilities and provides artificial limbs, callipers, crutches, wheelchairs, hand-paddled tricycles, and other assistive devices free of cost; as of 2024, BMVSS has rehabilitated around 2.4 million persons with disabilities in India and abroad and has conducted 116 on-the-spot limb-fitment camps across 46 countries, benefiting over 47,500 foreign nationals.

points.title: The Jaipur Foot has expanded its global reach significantly since 2016.
points.text: 605. The Jaipur Foot has expanded its global reach significantly since 2016. In 2018, India’s Permanent Mission to the United Nations, along with BMVSS, organized a panel discussion at the UN headquarters in New York on the Jaipur Foot’s 50-year journey of helping people with disabilities. Following the panel discussion, an exhibition on Jaipur Foot was inaugurated in the UN Secretariat Building. India’s Permanent Representative to the UN highlighted that Jaipur Foot has become an excellent example of South-South cooperation and multi-stakeholder collaboration, having worked with over 1.7 million persons with disabilities in 34 countries.

points.title: BMVSS holds special consultative status with the Economic and Social Council of the United Nations, demonstrating India’s leade…
points.text: 606. BMVSS holds special consultative status with the Economic and Social Council of the United Nations, demonstrating India’s leadership in assistive technology cooperation. In 2024, the former President of the United Nations General Assembly Dennis Francis specially visited the BMVSS centre in Jaipur to understand the manufacturing process of Jaipur Foot, underscoring international recognition of this innovation.

points.title: Recent international camps organized in partnership with the Ministry of External Affairs include: camps in Kabul, Afghanistan,…
points.text: 607. Recent international camps organized in partnership with the Ministry of External Affairs include: camps in Kabul, Afghanistan, aiding 75 individuals; Maputo, Mozambique, benefiting 1,230 people; and planned camps in Tanzania, Nicaragua, Trinidad and Tobago, Guatemala, Sudan, and Myanmar. In the past year alone (2023-24), 1.78 lakh people received assistance in the form of artificial limbs, callipers, wheelchairs, and hearing aids.

points.title: International Research Partnerships: BMVSS has forged significant research agreements with leading international institutions i…
points.text: 608. International Research Partnerships: BMVSS has forged significant research agreements with leading international institutions including Stanford University (USA) and Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT, USA). The Stanford-BMVSS collaboration resulted in the development of the Stanford Jaipur Knee, a four-bar linkage polycentric knee joint that was recognized by TIME Magazine in November 2009 as one of the world’s 50 best inventions. More than 7,500 Stanford Jaipur Knees have been fitted to patients, costing only USD 20 to manufacture compared to thousands of dollars for similar western prosthetics.

points.title: MIT is currently collaborating with BMVSS on three projects: development of a wheelchair and hand-pedalled tricycle for rural c…
points.text: 609. MIT is currently collaborating with BMVSS on three projects: development of a wheelchair and hand-pedalled tricycle for rural conditions, a braking knee joint, and a new foot piece. The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) and four Indian Institutes of Technology (IIT) branches are working on new designs and materials for improving the performance of prostheses using polyurethane, expected to reduce the weight of the Jaipur Foot from its current 850 grams.

points.title: India’s national accessibility standards reflect systematic alignment with international guidelines and best practices, particu…
points.text: 610. India’s national accessibility standards reflect systematic alignment with international guidelines and best practices, particularly in sports and digital accessibility: the Ministry of Youth Affairs and Sports issued the “Guidelines for Accessible Sports Complex and Residential Facilities for Sports Persons with Disabilities” in October 2022, drawing on international frameworks such as the UN Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific (ESCAP) 1995 guidelines on non-handicapping physical environments, while in the digital domain the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) formulated the Indian Accessibility Standard IS 17802 for ICT products and services, harmonising India’s ICT accessibility regime with leading global standards and international best practices to support cross-border technology cooperation and inclusive access for persons with disabilities.
points.subsection: C. Adaptation of International Standards and Best Practices

points.title: India actively engages in international cooperation to procure and deploy advanced assistive technologies while strengthening d…
points.text: 611. India actively engages in international cooperation to procure and deploy advanced assistive technologies while strengthening domestic capacity: at the state level, the Goa Directorate of Health Services partnered with a CSR entity to procure 290 digital programmable hearing aids from Oticon, enabling eligible beneficiaries to access modern global assistive technology; in higher education, the All India Council for Technical Education (AICTE) mandates provision of assistive hardware and software and promotes R&D in assistive technologies to encourage technology transfer and indigenous innovation; complementing this, Artificial Limbs Manufacturing Corporation of India (ALIMCO), under the “Make in India” mission, has entered into agreements with Ottobock for artificial limbs, included high-end devices under CSR projects, and expanded cochlear implant initiatives, including procurement of 56 cochlear implants for the National Institute for Hearing Handicapped, thereby advancing access to cutting-edge assistive technologies for persons with disabilities.
points.subsection: C. Adaptation of International Standards and Best Practices

points.title: International Cooperation in Employment and Skill Development: The Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Grameen Kaushalya Yojana (DDU-GKY), a p…
points.text: 612. International Cooperation in Employment and Skill Development: The Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Grameen Kaushalya Yojana (DDU-GKY), a placement-linked skill development program under the Ministry of Rural Development, has facilitated international labour market cooperation for persons with disabilities. As of May 2025, the program has successfully achieved 575 foreign placements for its trained candidates, including persons with disabilities, demonstrating India’s commitment to facilitating cross-border employment opportunities and economic empowerment for persons with disabilities through international cooperation in skill development and job placement.
points.subsection: C. Adaptation of International Standards and Best Practices

points.title: The Ministers and representatives of members and associate members of the United Nations Economic and Social Commission for Asi…
points.text: 613. The Ministers and representatives of members and associate members of the United Nations Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific (UNESCAP) assembled at the High Level Inter-Governmental meeting for the final review of the implementation of the Asian and Pacific Decade of Disabled Persons, 2003-2012 held at Incheon, Korea in 2012 and adopted the Incheon Strategy “Make the Right Real” for PwDs in Asia and the Pacific. The ESCAP in its 69th Session held from 25 April-1 May, 2013 passed the resolution endorsing the Ministerial declaration and Incheon Strategy. The Incheon Strategy spells out Action Plans at the regional and sub-regional levels to achieve the goals and contains the core indicators for tracking the progress of implementation. India is a Party to the Incheon Strategy since 2012 and has been actively participating in the Asian and Pacific Decade for Persons with Disabilities, 2013-2022.
points.subsection: C. Adaptation of International Standards and Best Practices

points.title: Due to the cross-cutting nature of disability, coordinating mechanisms have been viewed as integral to the realization of disab…
points.text: 614. Due to the cross-cutting nature of disability, coordinating mechanisms have been viewed as integral to the realization of disability rights. In order to provide focussed attention to disability rights, a separate department on disability namely, Department for Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities was set up in the MoSJE in 2012 to act as the nodal Department for the overall policy, planning and coordination of programmes for PwDs. The DEPwD specifically acts as the central focal point for matters relating to the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities and serves as the nodal agency for preparation of Combined Country Reports to the UNCRPD.
points.subsection: A. Focal Points and Coordination Mechanisms

points.title: A dedicated Inter-Ministerial Committee has been constituted to monitor the progress of preparation of Country Reports and ensu…
points.text: 615. A dedicated Inter-Ministerial Committee has been constituted to monitor the progress of preparation of Country Reports and ensure coordinated implementation across government departments. This mechanism ensures horizontal coordination across different sectors of government activity.
points.subsection: A. Focal Points and Coordination Mechanisms

points.title: To ensure comprehensive implementation across all sectors, various Ministries and Departments have been designated as focal poi…
points.text: 616. To ensure comprehensive implementation across all sectors, various Ministries and Departments have been designated as focal points for specific areas under the Convention:
points.subsection: A. Focal Points and Coordination Mechanisms

points.title: At the institutional level, coordination mechanisms have been established in educational and technical institutions:
points.text: 617. At the institutional level, coordination mechanisms have been established in educational and technical institutions:
points.subsection: A. Focal Points and Coordination Mechanisms

points.title: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act), which replaced the Persons with Disabilities Act 1995 (PWDA), est…
points.text: 618. The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act), which replaced the Persons with Disabilities Act 1995 (PWDA), established a new governance structure. Under Sections 60-69 of the RPwD Act, Central and State Advisory Boards on Disability replaced the previous Coordination Committees to serve as apex policy-making and consultative bodies at the Central and State levels.
points.subsection: B. Central and State Advisory Boards on Disability

points.title: The Central Advisory Board on Disability is constituted under Section 60 and is chaired by the Minister in charge of the Depart…
points.text: 619. The Central Advisory Board on Disability is constituted under Section 60 and is chaired by the Minister in charge of the Department of Disability Affairs in the Central Government. The Board consists of:
points.subsection: B. Central and State Advisory Boards on Disability

points.title: Under Section 65, the Central Advisory Board on Disability serves as the national-level consultative and advisory body on disab…
points.text: 620. Under Section 65, the Central Advisory Board on Disability serves as the national-level consultative and advisory body on disability matters and facilitates the continuous evolution of comprehensive policy for empowerment of persons with disabilities. The Board is required to:
points.subsection: B. Central and State Advisory Boards on Disability

points.title: Similarly, State Advisory Boards on Disability are constituted under Section 66 in each State to perform analogous functions at…
points.text: 621. Similarly, State Advisory Boards on Disability are constituted under Section 66 in each State to perform analogous functions at the state level. The State Advisory Board serves as a high-level consultative and advisory body for coordinating all state-level activities relating to PwDs and must include ten persons with disabilities (at least five of whom must be women) representing disability organizations, ensuring meaningful participation of persons with disabilities in state-level policy formulation and monitoring.
points.subsection: B. Central and State Advisory Boards on Disability

points.title: Section 72 of the RPwD Act requires State Governments to constitute District Level Committees to address local concerns of pers…
points.text: 622. Section 72 of the RPwD Act requires State Governments to constitute District Level Committees to address local concerns of persons with disabilities and perform localized monitoring of the Act’s implementation. These committees are mandated to advise district authorities on disability matters and ensure ground-level responsiveness to the needs of persons with disabilities. The constitution and functions of these committees are prescribed by State Governments in their respective rules, though implementation has been inconsistent across states.

points.title: Chief Commissioner of Persons with Disabilities (CCPD): Under Sections 74-75 of the RPwD Act 2016, the Chief Commissioner for P…
points.text: 623. Chief Commissioner of Persons with Disabilities (CCPD): Under Sections 74-75 of the RPwD Act 2016, the Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities is appointed by the Central Government and is now assisted by two Commissioners and an Advisory Committee comprising not more than eleven members drawn from experts in various disabilities. As an independent authority established to monitor implementation and inquire into the deprivation of rights of persons with disabilities, the CCPD functions include:

points.title: The Office of the Chief Commissioner has been strengthened under the 2016 Act and continues to function actively, issuing order…
points.text: 624. The Office of the Chief Commissioner has been strengthened under the 2016 Act and continues to function actively, issuing orders and interim orders on cases involving disability rights violations, employment discrimination, accessibility, and other matters as evidenced by regular case disposals through 2025. The CCPD plays a critical role in consultative processes, including the development of central guidelines for Accessible Sports Complexes and the Accessibility Code for Educational Institutions, which are formulated through extensive consultation with stakeholders and the Office of the CCPD.

points.title: Commissioners of Persons with Disabilities: Sections 79 and 80 of the RPwD Act 2016 requires every State Government to appoint…
points.text: 625. Commissioners of Persons with Disabilities: Sections 79 and 80 of the RPwD Act 2016 requires every State Government to appoint a Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities. The State Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities (SCPD) serves as an independent authority at the state level to monitor implementation, inquire into deprivation of rights, and has powers similar to that of CCPD in relation to the State, including quasi-judicial powers similar to those of a civil court and serves as a regulatory body and grievance redressal agency at the state level. Courts have strengthened the quasi-judicial character of the Chief Commissioner’s office, particularly in employment and service-related disputes involving persons with disabilities, reinforcing the enforcement mechanisms under the Act

points.title: To ensure protection of rights and provide speedy justice, Section 84 of the RPwD Act 2016 requires State Governments to design…
points.text: 626. To ensure protection of rights and provide speedy justice, Section 84 of the RPwD Act 2016 requires State Governments to designate Special Courts for each district to try offences under the Act. Section 85 mandates the appointment of Special Public Prosecutors to ensure effective prosecution of cases involving violations of disability rights. This institutional framework ensures judicial oversight and enforcement of disability rights, with courts empowered to provide both criminal penalties and civil remedies for rights violations.
points.subsection: E. Special Courts and Legal Framework

points.title: The implementation framework explicitly incorporates human rights principles, as evidenced by judicial rulings from the Supreme…
points.text: 627. The implementation framework explicitly incorporates human rights principles, as evidenced by judicial rulings from the Supreme Court and High Courts that actively monitor state compliance and enforce accessibility and non-discrimination as fundamental rights under the Constitution. Courts have recognized disability rights as integral to Articles 14 (equality), 19 (freedoms), and 21 (right to life and dignity) of the Indian Constitution, providing constitutional protection beyond statutory provisions.
points.subsection: E. Special Courts and Legal Framework

points.title: Despite the enactment of the RPwD Act, 2016, implementation has faced persistent gaps, as noted by the Supreme Court of India i…
points.text: 628. Despite the enactment of the RPwD Act, 2016, implementation has faced persistent gaps, as noted by the Supreme Court of India in Seema Girija Lal v. Union of India and subsequent orders through 2023-2025, including the failure of several states to appoint State Commissioners, constitute State Funds for Persons with Disabilities, and establish Special Courts or designate Special Public Prosecutors, alongside incomplete framing of state rules and institutional mechanisms, with the absence of independent oversight (primarily due to non-appointment of State Commissioners) emerging as a critical barrier to effective enforcement.
points.subsection: E. Special Courts and Legal Framework

points.title: Numbering gap in source document
points.text: 629. [Numbering gap in source: no numbered Point 629 paragraph was found in the uploaded document between the surrounding numbered points.]
points.subsection: E. Special Courts and Legal Framework

points.title: Under Sections 86-89 of the RPwD Act 2016, National and State Funds for Persons with Disabilities were mandated to be created t…
points.text: 630. Under Sections 86-89 of the RPwD Act 2016, National and State Funds for Persons with Disabilities were mandated to be created to provide financial support to persons with disabilities. The existing National Fund for Persons with Disabilities and the Trust Fund for Empowerment of Persons with Disabilities were to be subsumed into the National Fund. However, as noted above, several states have not yet constituted the required State Funds, limiting financial resources available for disability programs at the state level.

points.title: The Scheme for Implementation of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016 (SIPDA) was established as an umbrella scheme fully funded…
points.text: 631. The Scheme for Implementation of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016 (SIPDA) was established as an umbrella scheme fully funded by the Central Government to provide financial assistance for various components including barrier-free environment creation, the Accessible India Campaign, and district disability rehabilitation centres. As of 2021, SIPDA encompassed thirteen sub-schemes, though budgetary allocation increased only marginally (from Rs 193 crore in 2016-17 to Rs 210 crore in 2021-22, representing only a 9% increase) despite the doubling of sub-schemes. A Standing Committee on Social Justice and Empowerment noted in 2021 that this inadequate budgetary growth, combined with frequent amendments to SIPDA since 2016, has led to complications in implementation and made it difficult for stakeholders and implementing agencies to understand new processes and access resources effectively.

points.title: Launched in December 2015 to strengthen the Prime Minister’s Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan), this initiativ…
points.text: 632. Launched in December 2015 to strengthen the Prime Minister’s Accessible India Campaign (Sugamya Bharat Abhiyan), this initiative aims to ensure universal accessibility for persons with disabilities to built environment, transportation systems, and information and communication technology. As of 2021, approximately 30% of identified buildings and 65% of identified websites had been made accessible to persons with disabilities, with multiple deadline extensions from the original July 2016 target to June 2022. The Standing Committee on Social Justice and Empowerment recommended in 2021 that the DEPwD should adhere to deadlines without further extensions and that penalties under the Act should be invoked if timelines are not met, while also calling on various government Ministries and Departments to urgently finalize sector-wise accessibility standards.

points.title: The RPwD Act, 2016 and its implementation framework embed participatory governance by mandating representation of persons with…
points.text: 633. The RPwD Act, 2016 and its implementation framework embed participatory governance by mandating representation of persons with disabilities and civil society across monitoring and policy processes, including statutory requirements for substantial PwD representation on Central and State Advisory Boards, institutionalised NGO participation through Equal Opportunity Facilitation Cells in AICTE-approved institutions, consultative formulation of central guidelines such as accessible sports and education standards in coordination with the Office of the Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities and disability organisations, inclusion of Disabled People’s Organisations in disaster risk reduction planning under national guidelines, partnership-based inclusion in higher education recommended by the University Grants Commission, and a multi-stakeholder National Action Plan for Skill Development of PwDs.
points.subsection: I. Civil Society and Stakeholder Participation
